title: Context Graphs for Proactive Enterprise Agents
summary: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) and agentic frameworks have advanced enterprise AI considerably, yet agents remain fundamentally reactive: they wait for a human query before acting. This paper argues that genuine enterprise productivity gains require proactive agents: systems that surface relevant, actionable information to workers before they ask. We propose the Context Graph, a live relational data structure that models enterprise entities, their relationships, and state transitions over time. Built on this graph, we define a Delta Detection Engine that continuously monitors state changes, a Proactivity Scorer that ranks candidate insights by urgency, relevance, and persona-fit, and a Surfacing Layer powered by an LLM that delivers ranked notifications with grounded explanations. We formalize each component, derive a unified Proactivity Score function, and provide a complete end-to-end Python implementation using NetworkX and the Anthropic Claude API. Evaluation across three generic enterprise case studies (contract lifecycle management, engineering incident response, and sales pipeline hygiene) demonstrates that context-graph-driven proactivity achieves Precision@5 of 0.83, a false positive rate of 0.11, and reduces mean time to surface from 47 minutes (reactive baseline) to under 30 second.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a proactive enterprise AI architecture that automatically surfaces relevant, time-sensitive insights to workers using a dynamic context graph and LLM-powered notification layer, significantly outperforming reactive RAG/agent baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing enterprise AI systems built on RAG and agent frameworks are largely reactive, only responding after a human issues a query. This limits productivity gains because critical changes, risks, or opportunities may go unnoticed until someone explicitly asks. The authors aim to enable AI that anticipates user needs and pushes the right information at the right time, reducing latency and cognitive load in enterprise workflows.
AI.method: The authors introduce the Context Graph, a live relational data structure representing enterprise entities, their relationships, and state transitions. On top of this, they define three main components: (1) a Delta Detection Engine that continuously tracks and extracts meaningful state changes in the graph; (2) a Proactivity Scorer that computes a unified Proactivity Score for candidate insights based on urgency, relevance, and persona-fit; and (3) a Surfacing Layer using an LLM to turn high-scoring candidates into ranked, grounded notifications. They formalize these components mathematically and provide a full Python implementation using NetworkX for graph handling and the Anthropic Claude API for LLM-driven surfacing.
AI.result: Across three illustrative enterprise scenarios—contract lifecycle management, engineering incident response, and sales pipeline hygiene—the proposed system shows strong performance: Precision@5 of 0.83, a false positive rate of 0.11, and a large reduction in mean time to surface relevant insights from 47 minutes (for a reactive baseline) to under 30 seconds.
AI.conclusion: Context-graph-driven proactive agents can substantially improve enterprise productivity versus traditional reactive RAG/agent setups. By modeling entities and their evolving relationships, continuously detecting deltas, scoring them for proactivity, and using an LLM to communicate insights, the system can surface high-quality, timely information with low false positives. The authors suggest this architecture as a practical blueprint for building proactive enterprise AI and demonstrate its feasibility with an end-to-end implementation.

title: AI-integrated models for assessing agricultural resilience
summary: Agricultural supply chains are vulnerable to disruptions through linked biophysical and economic systems. We develop an AI-powered tool that integrates economic models (GTAP) with biophysical models (APSIM) to analyze supply chain shocks, enabling policymakers and market participants to assess cross-disciplinary impacts through queries and responses written in natural language.

AI.tldr: The paper presents an AI-powered interface that links economic and biophysical models to analyze agricultural supply-chain shocks via natural-language queries.
AI.motivation: Agricultural supply chains face complex disruptions driven by both biophysical factors (e.g., crop growth, weather) and economic dynamics (e.g., trade, prices). Existing tools are often siloed, technical, and difficult for policymakers and market participants to use directly. There is a need for an integrated, user-friendly system that lets non-technical users explore the cross-disciplinary impacts of shocks on agricultural supply chains.
AI.method: The authors couple a global economic model (GTAP) with a biophysical crop systems model (APSIM) and place an AI-powered natural-language interface on top of them. The AI layer interprets user queries, translates them into appropriate model scenarios or data retrieval tasks, runs or accesses the GTAP and APSIM models, and then returns synthesized responses in natural language that capture interactions across the biophysical and economic systems.
AI.result: The integrated tool can process natural-language questions about agricultural supply-chain shocks and produce responses that reflect underlying simulations from both GTAP and APSIM. It demonstrates the ability to represent linked biophysical–economic impacts of disruptions, thus helping users understand how shocks propagate through supply chains, though detailed quantitative performance is not specified in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: By combining GTAP, APSIM, and an AI-driven natural-language interface, the paper provides a cross-disciplinary decision-support tool that makes complex agricultural supply-chain analysis more accessible to policymakers and market participants, facilitating better-informed responses to potential disruptions.

title: Adversarial Social Epistemology for Assemblies of Humans and Large Language Models
summary: We outline an adversarial social epistemology (ASE) for densely interactive communicative landscapes in which public assertions are scaffolded by chains of testimony, inference, institutional certification, and tacit trust. In such landscapes, agents have incentives and affordances to distort, color, omit, fabricate, or strategically under-specify information for private, reputational, rhetorical, or material gains. We argue that these phenomena are not adequately captured by familiar descriptions of epistemic bubbles, echo chambers, or misinformation diffusion. What requires explanation is how communicative agents exploit the commitments and entitlements that normally make scaffolded assertions trustworthy. We provide language that delivers the requisite analysis, outline mechanisms that subvert trust in scaffolded public communications, and outline machinery for auditing and redressing trust breaches arising from subverting the auditability of inferential chains, drawing on epistemic networks, enriched with an inferentialist semantics for interpreting assertions.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a framework called adversarial social epistemology (ASE) to analyze how, in complex communication environments, people can strategically manipulate information while still appearing trustworthy.
AI.motivation: Existing concepts like epistemic bubbles, echo chambers, and misinformation diffusion do not fully explain how actors exploit normally trustworthy structures—such as testimony chains, institutional credentials, and tacit trust—for strategic manipulation. The authors want a theory that explicitly addresses these adversarial uses of social-epistemic norms and infrastructures.
AI.method: They propose a conceptual and theoretical framework, drawing on social epistemology, inferentialism, and epistemic network models. They introduce new terminology for characterizing adversarial communicative behavior, analyze mechanisms by which agents subvert trust in scaffolded assertions, and integrate inferentialist semantics into network representations of assertions and their justificatory chains.
AI.result: The paper identifies and systematizes mechanisms by which agents distort or strategically under-specify information while leveraging existing commitments, entitlements, and institutional certifications. It shows how these mechanisms undermine the auditability and reliability of inferential chains in public communication, and it specifies structures and tools for analyzing such phenomena using epistemic network models enriched with inferentialist semantics.
AI.conclusion: Adversarial social epistemology offers a more precise way to understand manipulation in complex information environments than prevailing notions like bubbles or misinformation flows. By focusing on how agents subvert the auditability and trustworthiness of scaffolded assertions, and by using enriched epistemic network models, the framework supports more targeted strategies for auditing, diagnosing, and remedying breaches of epistemic trust in public communications.

title: Unveiling Public Opinion: A Study of Sentiment Analysis Using LSTM and Traditional Models
comment: 6 pages, 5 figures. Published in the Proceedings of the 2025 IEEE Conference on Computing, Communication, and Data Engineering (C-CODE 2025)
summary: In this age of social media, sites like Twitter have become meeting places for people to share their views and feelings on a wide range of issues and current events as they unfold in real time. Sentiment analysis, a critical application of NLP, has become indispensable due to the massive influx of user-generated content, enabling the extraction of meaningful insights from the opinions and emotions expressed in textual data. Sentiment analysis on Twitter employs sophisticated computational techniques to categorize tweets into positive, negative, or neutral sentiments. This method not only examines individual expressions but also analyzes vast databases related to specific subjects or events. By spotting these emotions, machine learning models help improve public opinion interpretation and trend forecasting. This paper examines the effectiveness of various machine learning and deep learning approaches. Designed for this use, the system evaluates logistic regression, random forest, naïve bayes, gradient boosting, and LSTM networks, among other algorithms applied in sentiment classification. This work identifies the optimal sentiment analysis model using a Kaggle Twitter dataset that has been preprocessed through tokenization, lemmatization, and stopword elimination. Emphasizing the better performance of the LSTM approach, the model attained a training accuracy of 90.98%, a testing accuracy of 80.00%, and a micro-average ROC- AUC score of 0.92. These results show that the model outperforms conventional machine learning techniques in capturing contextual and sequential textual aspects.

AI.tldr: The paper compares several machine learning and deep learning algorithms for Twitter sentiment analysis using a Kaggle dataset and finds that an LSTM model performs best, achieving 80% test accuracy and a 0.92 ROC-AUC.
AI.motivation: With the rapid growth of user-generated content on social media platforms like Twitter, there is a need for effective sentiment analysis tools to interpret public opinion and forecast trends in real time. Existing traditional machine learning approaches may struggle to fully capture contextual and sequential information in short, noisy tweets, motivating an investigation into whether deep learning methods, especially LSTMs, can provide superior performance.
AI.method: The authors perform sentiment classification on a Kaggle Twitter dataset that has been preprocessed using tokenization, lemmatization, and stopword removal. They implement and evaluate multiple algorithms—including logistic regression, random forest, naive Bayes, gradient boosting, and LSTM networks—on the same dataset. Performance is compared using metrics such as training accuracy, test accuracy, and micro-average ROC-AUC to determine the best-performing model.
AI.result: Among the evaluated models, the LSTM network achieves the strongest performance, with 90.98% training accuracy, 80.00% testing accuracy, and a micro-average ROC-AUC score of 0.92, outperforming traditional machine learning baselines on the sentiment classification task.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that LSTM-based deep learning models are more effective than conventional machine learning methods for Twitter sentiment analysis because they better capture contextual and sequential aspects of text. The reported metrics demonstrate that LSTMs can significantly improve the accuracy and robustness of sentiment classification on social media data.

title: Aligning Clinical Needs and AI Capabilities: A Survey on LLMs for Medical Reasoning
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have emerged as important tools in healthcare, showing growing potential for clinical reasoning and patient care. This survey examines recent progress in medical LLMs, focusing on reasoning applications and requirements. We present a dual-view approach that connects clinical practice with computational methods. On the clinical side, we establish a five-level competency scheme following Miller's Pyramid, progressing from knowledge recall to dynamic case management. On the computational side, we link deductive, inductive, and abductive reasoning patterns to common medical goals and tasks. We also introduce a benchmark dataset spanning five levels of medical reasoning capability and report results on 18 state-of-the-art models, revealing that medical specialist models excel in diagnosis-centric tasks while general models lead in decision support and dialogue. We conclude by discussing current progress and open challenges, including data limitations, hallucination, and grounding issues, and outline directions toward safer, more reliable, and workflow-ready systems.

AI.tldr: Survey of medical large language models with emphasis on clinical reasoning, competency levels, and benchmarking across reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly used in healthcare, but their clinical reasoning abilities, competency requirements, and alignment with real clinical workflows are not systematically characterized or evaluated. There is a need to map clinical competencies to computational reasoning patterns and to benchmark models accordingly.
AI.method: Propose a dual-view framework: (1) a five-level clinical competency scheme based on Miller's Pyramid, from simple knowledge recall to complex, dynamic case management; (2) a taxonomy of computational reasoning modes (deductive, inductive, abductive) mapped to medical goals and tasks. Construct a benchmark dataset that spans the five competency levels, then evaluate 18 state-of-the-art general and medical-specialist LLMs on this benchmark.
AI.result: Medical-specialist LLMs perform best on diagnosis-centered tasks, whereas general-purpose LLMs achieve higher scores on broader decision support and dialogue-oriented tasks. The benchmark surfaces substantial gaps in higher-level reasoning, and exposes common issues such as hallucinations and lack of grounding across models.
AI.conclusion: Medical LLMs show promising but uneven capabilities across reasoning levels and task types. Specialist and general models have complementary strengths, but none yet achieve robust, workflow-ready performance across all competency levels. Progress will require better clinical data, strategies to reduce hallucinations, stronger grounding in validated knowledge and patient context, and designs that integrate more safely and reliably into clinical workflows.

title: From Solvers to Research: Large Language Model-Driven Formal Mathematics at the Research Frontier
summary: Recent developments in AI for Mathematics (AI4Math), especially Large Language Model (LLM)-driven theorem provers, has achieved remarkable success in formal proof generation for well-defined mathematical problems through Interactive Theorem Proving (ITP) languages. However, current systems remain fundamentally limited in tackling frontier research mathematics, such as discovering new theorems or resolving open conjectures, which are often open-ended, under-specified, and involve multiple layers of abstraction. We argue that the next leap in AI4Math systems requires a decisive shift from predefined problem-solvers to research agents that can address frontier mathematical challenges with rigorous formal mathematical reasoning. In this position paper, we provide a systematic review of the field, covering datasets, auto-formalization, and proof synthesis. More importantly, we identify core limitations of existing systems in serving as mathematical research agents, examining issues across datasets, relational structure, mathematical exploration, tool ecosystem, and human-AI collaboration, outlining a strategic road-map for the future of AI4Math.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that current AI for Mathematics systems, especially LLM-based theorem provers, are good at solving predefined formal problems but are inadequate for frontier research; it proposes a roadmap toward AI research agents capable of genuine mathematical discovery.
AI.motivation: While LLM-driven theorem provers have recently achieved strong performance in generating formal proofs within interactive theorem proving frameworks, they still cannot handle open-ended, under-specified, and highly abstract problems that characterize genuine frontier mathematical research. This gap between formal proof automation and real mathematical discovery motivates a systematic analysis of the field and a forward-looking agenda for building AI systems that function as research collaborators, not just problem-solvers.
AI.method: The paper is a position and survey work rather than an empirical study. It systematically reviews the AI4Math landscape, including datasets, auto-formalization techniques, and proof synthesis methods, and then analyzes the limitations of existing approaches. It structures this analysis around several key axes—datasets and their coverage, representation of relational mathematical structure, mechanisms for exploration and conjecture generation, the supporting tool ecosystem, and patterns of human–AI collaboration—to derive a strategic roadmap.
AI.result: The main result is a structured critique and synthesis of current AI4Math research: it identifies concrete limitations of present-day LLM-based theorem provers and related tools when applied to frontier mathematical research tasks. It organizes these limitations into thematic areas and articulates where current systems fail, for example in handling open, ill-specified problems, performing deep abstraction, and coordinating exploratory reasoning over extended projects.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that achieving the next major leap in AI for Mathematics requires moving beyond narrowly defined, benchmark-driven theorem proving toward building full-fledged mathematical research agents. These agents must integrate rigorous formal reasoning with capabilities for exploration, abstraction, tool use, and collaboration with humans. The authors propose a strategic roadmap along dimensions of data, representations, exploration strategies, tooling, and interaction paradigms to guide future work toward this goal.

title: Alignment Plausibility: A New Standard for Assuring AI in Healthcare
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have become significant providers of mental health support, yet they remain products of an attention economy whose operational and commercial targets favour sustained engagement over the friction that effective psychological support often requires. Developers' safety responses have been largely reactive, addressing the most visible and acute harms while subtler, longer-term patterns of risk (e.g., dependency, boundary erosion, the amplification of distorted beliefs) receive less attention. We contend that making LLMs structurally safe requires alignment organised at three levels that mirror how society assures the safety of human clinical practice: 1) explicit value specification grounded in the codified normative commitments of clinical practice; 2) training that embeds those values in the model; and 3) oversight that detects drift and longer-term harm during deployment, much as clinical supervision does for human practice. Organising alignment in this way yields a construct we call alignment plausibility - a structured demonstration that a system's values, training regime, and oversight mechanisms are together consistent with safe and positive outcomes. We propose alignment plausibility as a regulatory construct (by drawing analogy to the established construct of biological plausibility) for AI in health: a principled way to argue for, or against, trust that systems are aligned to positive health outcomes, will cause no harm even where capable of doing so, and will ultimately lead to patient benefit.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that to make large language models (LLMs) safe for mental health support, their alignment must be structured and evidenced across values, training, and oversight, forming what the authors call “alignment plausibility,” a proposed regulatory standard similar to biological plausibility in medicine.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly used for mental health support but are built within an attention economy that prioritizes engagement over the kinds of constructive friction and boundaries needed for effective psychological care. Existing safety measures mainly address obvious, acute harms (e.g., self-harm prompts) and neglect subtle, long-term risks such as user dependency, blurred boundaries, and reinforcement of distorted beliefs. There is currently no principled, systematic way to assess whether systems are genuinely aligned with safe, beneficial mental health outcomes, especially over time and in deployment.
AI.method: Conceptual and normative analysis rather than empirical experimentation. The authors map how safety is ensured in human clinical practice (codes of ethics, formal training, and clinical supervision) and propose an analogous three-level alignment framework for LLMs in mental health contexts: (1) explicit value specification derived from clinical norms, (2) training approaches that embed those values in the model, and (3) ongoing oversight mechanisms that monitor for drift and emergent harms. They then introduce and define the construct of “alignment plausibility,” drawing an analogy with “biological plausibility” in biomedical regulation, to argue how regulators could systematically evaluate whether an AI system is likely to be safe and beneficial in health applications.
AI.result: The paper delivers a structured framework for thinking about safety in mental‑health LLMs and articulates the construct of “alignment plausibility.” It clarifies what kinds of documentation and evidence would be needed at each of the three levels (values, training, oversight) to credibly claim that a system is aligned with safe clinical norms. It also reframes alignment from a vague aspiration into something that can be assessed and argued about in regulatory and professional settings.
AI.conclusion: To make LLMs structurally safe for mental health use, alignment must be organised similarly to safeguards in human clinical practice, spanning explicit value commitments, value-consistent training, and robust oversight. When these elements are coherently specified and evidenced, they form “alignment plausibility,” which the authors propose as a regulatory construct for AI in health, analogous to biological plausibility in biomedicine. This construct can underpin trustworthy claims that systems will tend to support positive health outcomes, avoid harm even when capable of it, and ultimately benefit patients.

title: DeepSearch-World: Self-Distillation for Deep Search Agents in a Verifiable Environment
summary: Training tool-use agents to improve from their own experience remains challenging, as supervised fine-tuning relies on fixed teacher-distilled trajectories, while sparse-reward reinforcement learning provides weak supervision for long-horizon interactions. We present DeepSearch-Evolve, a self-distillation framework for web agents built on DeepSearch-World, a deterministic and verifiable environment with reproducible search and page-reading tools. DeepSearch-World contains 420K multi-hop QA tasks constructed from entity-level random walks and supports key agentic cognitive behaviors useful for self-evolving, including progress verification, grounded reflection, and failure recovery. DeepSearch-Evolve iteratively performs trajectory generation, filtering, data mixing, and fine-tuning to train stronger agents. Without distillation from more capable models, DeepSearch-World-9B achieves competitive performance compared with open-source agents, reaching 31.2% on BrowseComp, 61.5% on GAIA, and 93.4% on HotpotQA, showing that verifiable environments enable scalable self-evolution for long-horizon web agents. We will release the environment, 420K training pool, validation set, model, and code to facilitate future research on self-improving deep search agents.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces DeepSearch-Evolve, a self-distillation framework for web agents trained in a deterministic environment (DeepSearch-World) that enables agents to improve from their own experience and achieve competitive performance on multiple web-based QA benchmarks without relying on stronger teacher models.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches for training tool-use web agents struggle because supervised fine-tuning depends on fixed, teacher-generated trajectories that cannot adapt, and sparse-reward reinforcement learning gives weak learning signals for complex, long-horizon tasks. The authors want a scalable way for agents to self-improve through interaction, using an environment where progress and correctness can be reliably checked.
AI.method: They build DeepSearch-World, a deterministic, verifiable web-like environment with reproducible search and page-reading tools and 420K multi-hop QA tasks generated via entity-level random walks. On top of this, they design DeepSearch-Evolve, a self-distillation loop where agents repeatedly: (1) generate trajectories on tasks, (2) filter them based on verifiable signals such as progress checks and grounded reflection, (3) mix new and old data, and (4) fine-tune the model on the curated trajectories to iteratively strengthen the agent.
AI.result: A 9B-parameter model trained only with this self-evolution framework, without distillation from larger or proprietary models, attains strong results on several benchmarks: 31.2% on BrowseComp, 61.5% on GAIA, and 93.4% on HotpotQA. These numbers are competitive with existing open-source web agents that often rely on more powerful teachers or less controlled environments.
AI.conclusion: Deterministic, verifiable environments like DeepSearch-World can support scalable self-distillation for long-horizon web agents. With appropriate mechanisms for verification, reflection, and failure recovery, agents can substantially improve by learning from their own trajectories, reducing reliance on external teacher models and enabling reproducible research via the released environment, data, and code.

title: Idiobionics: The Unification of Privacy and Intelligent Robotic Prostheses
summary: The human body is at the center of a growing family of technologies designed to tightly and persistently couple biological and digital systems. Robotic prostheses are a representative example of this tight coupling. Also referred to as bionic limbs, robotic prostheses are devices that support people who have lost limbs in pursuing daily life activities such as walking and grasping objects. Bionic limbs are now perceptive and responsive owing to their integration with advanced sensors and artificial intelligence-based control approaches. Consequently, such robotic prostheses can now be viewed as semiautonomous wearable robotic systems that can co-adapt with their users. However, the same sensing and control advancements that increase the capability of robotic prostheses also introduce threat vectors that could be exploited by malicious entities to violate the privacy of users. To fully realize the benefits of next-generation bionic limbs, we maintain it is important to directly understand and address these privacy risks and the barriers they might present to user adoption. This paper therefore introduces a new line of inquiry we term idiobionics to holistically investigate issues at the intersection of privacy and intelligent bionic limbs. As the main contribution of this paper, we define idiobionics, ground it in related literature, and provide preliminary evidence showing and discussing potential adversarial attacks that could exploit intelligent bionic limb designs. We then contribute a curated list of open research questions within idiobionics that are relevant to researchers in wearable robotics and other human-facing autonomous systems. We expect that idiobionics research will help unlock the full potential of robotic prostheses and related bionic devices.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new research area, idiobionics, focusing on privacy and security risks in intelligent bionic limbs (robotic prostheses) and outlines potential attacks and open research questions.
AI.motivation: Advances in sensors and AI-based control have turned robotic prostheses into semiautonomous wearable robots tightly coupled with the human body. While this boosts user capabilities and co-adaptation, it also opens new privacy and security threat vectors that may hinder user trust and adoption. There is a gap in systematically studying these issues for bionic limbs, motivating a dedicated line of inquiry.
AI.method: The authors conceptually define a new research area called idiobionics, review and connect it to related work in wearable robotics and human-facing autonomous systems, and provide preliminary, qualitative evidence and examples of possible adversarial attacks that could target intelligent bionic limb designs. They then synthesize and curate a set of open research questions to guide future work.
AI.result: The paper delivers: (1) a formal definition and conceptual framing of idiobionics as the study of privacy and security issues in intelligent bionic limbs; (2) illustrative scenarios and preliminary evidence of how adversaries might exploit sensing and control in bionic limbs; and (3) a structured list of open research problems for the community.
AI.conclusion: Idiobionics is proposed as a framework to systematically address privacy and security in intelligent bionic limbs. By understanding potential adversarial attacks and articulating key research questions, the field can help ensure that future bionic devices are both powerful and trustworthy, thereby enabling broader and safer adoption of robotic prostheses and related technologies.

title: How Do I Know What to Say Next? Barenholtz's Autogenerative Theory as an Enrichment of Harrisean Integrationism
summary: Roy Harris's Integrationist linguistics offers a compelling critique of the referentialist tradition embedded deep at the heart of computational approaches to language, arguing that language is not a code that maps onto a pre-given world but a situated, bipartite activity oriented toward prospective joint action. Yet Integrationism leaves certain explanatory gaps: it does not fully account for the structural mechanism by which signs sustain prospective openness, it undertheorises the continuity between linguistic and non-linguistic semiotic activity, and it offers no detailed account of the structural properties of the accumulated archive of past integrations. This paper argues that Elan Barenholtz's autogenerative theory of language, developed in response to the behaviour of Large Language Models (LLMs), can fill precisely these gaps, enriching Integrationism without undermining any of its core commitments. Specifically, the autogenerative account provides: a structural mechanism for the prospective openness that Harris identifies as central to bipartite communication; a computational correlate for Harris's thesis of semiotic continuity between language and other sign-making activity; and a theory of the archive: what the accumulated residue of past integrations looks like and how new participants draw upon it. The synthesis preserves Harris's ontological primacy of the situated integrative act while adding explanatory content that Integrationism itself does not supply. For practitioners and researchers in natural language processing and large language model design, the argument offers a principled account of what the statistical structure that LLMs so effectively exploit actually is, and of what it cannot, by its nature, provide.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes combining Roy Harris’s Integrationist linguistics with Elan Barenholtz’s autogenerative theory of language to better explain how language functions, especially in relation to LLMs.
AI.motivation: Integrationism critiques referentialist and code-based views of language and sees language as situated, prospective joint action, but it leaves gaps: it lacks a structural account of how signs remain open-ended, it underdevelops the relationship between linguistic and non-linguistic signs, and it does not explain the structural nature of the historical archive of prior linguistic integrations. The growing importance and puzzling success of Large Language Models also motivates a clearer account of what exactly their statistical structures capture.
AI.method: Conceptual and theoretical synthesis: the author examines Harris’s Integrationism and Barenholtz’s autogenerative theory of language, identifies where the latter can supply structural and computational detail missing in the former, and aligns the two frameworks so that Integrationism’s core commitments are preserved while its explanatory scope is expanded. The argument is developed at the level of theory rather than empirical experimentation.
AI.result: The autogenerative theory is shown to (1) supply a structural mechanism for the prospective openness central to bipartite communication in Integrationism, (2) offer a computational instantiation of semiotic continuity between language and other sign activities, and (3) characterize the archive of past integrations that LLM-like systems exploit. This yields a more detailed, structurally explicit version of Integrationism.
AI.conclusion: The combined framework maintains Integrationism’s focus on situated integrative acts as ontologically primary but enriches it with structural and computational content drawn from autogenerative theory. For NLP and LLM researchers, this offers a principled view of what the statistical regularities exploited by LLMs correspond to—namely an archive of past integrations—and clarifies the inherent limits of what such models, by their very nature, can provide in terms of meaning and prospective action.

title: Infinity-Parser2 Technical Report
summary: We present Infinity-Parser2, a large multimodal model that couples a controllable data-synthesis pipeline with multi-task reinforcement learning for end-to-end document parsing, addressing the persistent scarcity of faithfully annotated parsing corpora. Our contributions are threefold. First, we build a scalable synthesis engine, pairing a controllable rendering framework with an iterative refinement loop, and use it to construct and open-source Infinity-Doc2-5M: a 5-million-sample bilingual (Chinese/English) corpus spanning diverse document types, annotated with element bounding boxes, canonical content forms (Markdown, HTML, LaTeX, SMILES, structured charts), and full-page reading order. Second, we introduce a verifiable, multi-task reward system that enables Joint Reinforcement Learning across eight co-trained objectives (document parsing, layout analysis, table parsing, math formula parsing, chart parsing, chemical formula parsing, document VQA, and general multimodal understanding), unifying perception, structure, and reasoning in a single optimization signal. Third, we release two variants under a shared architecture: Infinity-Parser2-Flash, optimized for low-latency inference with a $3.68\times$ throughput gain over Infinity-Parser-7B, and Infinity-Parser2-Pro, engineered for precision-critical settings. Infinity-Parser2-Pro reaches state-of-the-art 87.6% on olmOCR-Bench and 74.3% on ParseBench, surpassing DeepSeek-OCR-2, PaddleOCR-VL-1.5, and MinerU2.5, with strong generalization to charts, chemical formulas, and document VQA.

AI.tldr: Infinity-Parser2 is a large multimodal model for end-to-end document parsing trained via controllable synthetic data and multi-task reinforcement learning, achieving SOTA results on multiple OCR/parse benchmarks.
AI.motivation: High-quality, faithfully annotated document parsing datasets are scarce and expensive to produce, which limits progress in comprehensive document understanding (structure, layout, text, and reasoning) across diverse formats and languages.
AI.method: They design (1) a scalable, controllable document synthesis and rendering pipeline that iteratively refines synthetic documents and produces Infinity-Doc2-5M with detailed structural annotations; and (2) a unified, verifiable multi-task reward system to perform joint reinforcement learning over eight tasks (document parsing, layout, tables, math, charts, chemical formulas, document VQA, general multimodal understanding) within a shared architecture. They then train and release two model variants optimized separately for speed and precision.
AI.result: Infinity-Parser2-Flash yields 3.68× higher inference throughput than Infinity-Parser-7B, while Infinity-Parser2-Pro achieves state-of-the-art performance on major document parsing benchmarks (87.6% on olmOCR-Bench, 74.3% on ParseBench), outperforming prior systems like DeepSeek-OCR-2, PaddleOCR-VL-1.5, and MinerU2.5, with strong generalization to charts, chemical formulas, and document VQA.
AI.conclusion: Controllable large-scale synthetic data combined with multi-task reinforcement learning can effectively unify perception, structure extraction, and reasoning in a single multimodal model, delivering both efficient and highly accurate document parsing systems that set new SOTA on standard benchmarks and generalize well to specialized document understanding tasks.

title: Scalable and Culturally Specific Stereotype Dataset Construction via Human-LLM Collaboration
comment: Weicheng Ma, John Guerrerio: equal contribution; published in EMNLP 2025 Main
summary: Research on stereotypes in large language models (LLMs) has largely focused on English-speaking contexts, due to the lack of datasets in other languages and the high cost of manual annotation in underrepresented cultures. To address this gap, we introduce a cost-efficient human-LLM collaborative annotation framework and apply it to construct EspanStereo, a Spanish-language stereotype dataset spanning multiple Spanish-speaking countries across Europe and Latin America. EspanStereo captures both well-documented stereotypes from prior literature and culturally specific biases absent from English-centric resources. Using LLMs to generate candidate stereotypes and in-culture annotators to validate them, we demonstrate the framework's effectiveness in identifying nuanced, region-specific biases. Our evaluation of Spanish-supporting LLMs using EspanStereo reveals significant variation in stereotypical behavior across countries, highlighting the need for more culturally grounded assessments. Beyond Spanish, our framework is adaptable to other languages and regions, offering a scalable path toward multilingual stereotype benchmarks. This work broadens the scope of stereotype analysis in LLMs and lays the groundwork for comprehensive cross-cultural bias evaluation.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces EspanStereo, a Spanish-language stereotype dataset built using a cost-efficient human-LLM collaborative annotation framework, enabling cross-country analysis of stereotypes in LLMs.
AI.motivation: Most stereotype and bias analyses in LLMs are English-centric because non-English datasets are scarce and manual annotation in underrepresented cultures is expensive. There is a need for culturally grounded, multilingual resources to evaluate LLM biases, especially across diverse Spanish-speaking regions.
AI.method: The authors propose a human-LLM collaborative framework: LLMs are used to generate candidate stereotypes, and native, in-culture human annotators validate and refine them. They apply this framework to create EspanStereo, a dataset of stereotypes in Spanish covering multiple Spanish-speaking countries in Europe and Latin America, including both known stereotypes from literature and newly identified, region-specific ones. They then evaluate Spanish-capable LLMs on this dataset.
AI.result: EspanStereo successfully captures both well-documented and culturally specific stereotypes that are not present in English-focused resources. When evaluating different Spanish-supporting LLMs on EspanStereo, the authors find substantial variation in how stereotypical the models are across different national and regional contexts.
AI.conclusion: The collaborative framework is an effective and cost-efficient way to build culturally grounded stereotype datasets. EspanStereo broadens stereotype analysis beyond English and shows that LLM biases vary significantly across Spanish-speaking countries. The framework can be generalized to other languages and regions, paving the way for scalable, multilingual, cross-cultural bias benchmarks for LLMs.

title: VectorizationLLM: Smart Vectorization Based AI Assistant
summary: VectorizationLLM is a specialized Large Language Model based on Google open-weight LLMs. The model is designed to assist students to learn smart vectorization, time/wave vector analysis, piecewise functions, Fourier analysis, and differential equations in MATLAB. The course application is CTEC 247: Applied Computational Analysis II by the Department of Electrical & Computer Engineering Technology at New York Institute of Technology Old Westbury. The LLM model is designed to be an instructive assistant, providing detailed explanations of concepts with examples from in-class notes without providing direct answers to questions. The model is designed with a RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation) knowledge base and system prompt architecture. Examples in both code, text, and images are provided in the LLM responses.

AI.tldr: VectorizationLLM is a domain‑specific teaching assistant LLM to help students learn MATLAB-based vectorization and related math topics using RAG and course materials, emphasizing explanations over direct answers.
AI.motivation: Students in the CTEC 247 course often struggle with advanced MATLAB topics like smart vectorization, time/wave vector analysis, piecewise functions, Fourier analysis, and differential equations. Traditional tools either give full solutions (encouraging copying) or lack the course-specific context. The authors aim to build an LLM that supports learning by explaining concepts with course-aligned examples while avoiding simply providing final answers.
AI.method: They adapt Google open‑weight LLMs into a specialized model called VectorizationLLM. The system uses a Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline with a curated knowledge base built from in‑class notes, code examples, and images. A carefully crafted system prompt architecture constrains the LLM to behave as an instructive assistant, focusing on step‑by‑step explanations, illustrations, and MATLAB examples, and explicitly discouraging direct answer dumping.
AI.result: The resulting VectorizationLLM can respond with multimodal explanations (text, MATLAB code snippets, and images) grounded in course materials. It guides students through concepts such as vectorization and Fourier analysis using familiar in‑class examples, while avoiding direct solution-giving behavior. Although quantitative metrics are not mentioned in the abstract, the system appears to function as a course-aligned, explanation-focused tutor.
AI.conclusion: VectorizationLLM demonstrates how a general open-weight LLM can be turned into a specialized, course-specific teaching assistant using RAG and prompt engineering. By grounding responses in class materials and emphasizing explanation over final answers, the model is positioned as a tool to enhance student understanding of MATLAB-based computational analysis concepts rather than as an answer-generation engine.

title: When Debiasing Backfires: Counterintuitive Side Effects of Preprocessing-Based Stereotype Mitigation
summary: Preprocessing-based methods for stereotype mitigation, such as pre-/post-training on debiased corpora, are widely used in NLP. While these approaches reduce measurable stereotypes for targeted groups, we find they often induce unintended shifts-side effects, where stereotyping or counter-stereotyping can increase relative to neutral baselines for other demographics, including across unrelated demographic categories. We demonstrate these side effects across two model families (encoder-only and decoder-only), multiple preprocessing strategies (removing stereotypical sentences, removing group mentions, and swapping group references), and both pre- and post-training at different data scales on Wikipedia. Standard benchmarks frequently miss these shifts. Using attention-rollout analysis, we observe that such side effects are not accompanied by large changes in attention flow, complicating mechanistic explanations. We discuss implications for evaluation, provide actionable diagnostics, and argue for side-effect-aware, transparent mitigation practices.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that common data-preprocessing methods meant to reduce stereotypes in language models often introduce new, hidden stereotype shifts affecting other demographic groups that standard benchmarks fail to detect.
AI.motivation: Many NLP systems use preprocessing-based debiasing (e.g., training on debiased corpora) to mitigate stereotypes. These methods are popular because they are relatively simple and model-agnostic. However, there is limited understanding of their broader impact across demographics and whether they unintentionally worsen or shift stereotypes for groups that are not the explicit debiasing target.
AI.method: The authors systematically evaluate several preprocessing strategies—removing stereotypical sentences, removing group mentions, and swapping group references—applied as pre- and post-training on Wikipedia for both encoder-only and decoder-only model families and at different data scales. They compare stereotype levels before and after intervention for targeted and non-targeted groups, and across unrelated demographic categories. They also use attention-rollout analysis to inspect whether changes in stereotype behavior correspond to changes in the internal attention flow of models. Standard bias benchmarks are used and contrasted with more fine-grained diagnostics introduced by the authors.
AI.result: They find that while these preprocessing-based debiasing methods reduce measurable stereotypes for the groups they explicitly target, they frequently cause side effects—namely, increases in stereotyping or counter-stereotyping relative to neutral baselines for other demographics, including groups not directly related to the targeted ones. These shifts occur across both encoder-only and decoder-only models, across all tested preprocessing strategies, and at multiple data scales. Standard benchmarks often fail to capture these side effects. Attention-rollout analysis reveals no large, interpretable changes in attention patterns that would straightforwardly explain the behavioral shifts.
AI.conclusion: Preprocessing-based debiasing is not benign: it can redistribute or shift stereotypes rather than simply reduce them. Because standard benchmarks often miss these side effects and model internals (e.g., attention patterns) do not show simple explanatory changes, practitioners should adopt side-effect-aware evaluation and transparent reporting when deploying debiasing methods. The paper calls for more comprehensive diagnostic tools and more cautious use of preprocessing-based stereotype mitigation in NLP.

title: A Graph Neural Network Model for Real-Time Gesture Recognition Based on sEMG Signals
summary: For seemless control of advanced hand prostheses and augmented reality, accurate and immediate hand gestures recognition is essential. Surface electromyography (sEMG) signals obtained from the forearm are commonly employed for this purpose. In this paper, we present a novel approach for sEMG representation that utilizes graph networks which contain information about muscle activation patterns in the forearm. Based on these graph networks, we have developed a machine learning algorithm capable of real-time hand gesture recognition using a graph neural network. The algorithm's performance was evaluated using sEMG signals acquired from myoband, which has 8 electrodes placed around the forearm, involving 8 healthy subjects. The proposed method demonstrated an average classification accuracy of 99\%, surpassing the performance of state-of-the-art techniques. The average time for both graph construction and prediction stood at 48ms utilizing a M1 pro CPU, rendering the approach well-suited for real-time applications.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a graph-based representation of forearm sEMG signals and a graph neural network algorithm that achieves 99% accuracy for real-time hand gesture recognition with 48 ms latency.
AI.motivation: Accurate and low-latency hand gesture recognition is crucial for controlling advanced hand prostheses and augmented reality interfaces. Existing methods using sEMG often struggle to fully exploit spatial relationships between electrodes and muscle activation patterns, limiting robustness and performance in real-time settings.
AI.method: sEMG signals from 8 electrodes around the forearm (myoband) are transformed into graph networks that encode muscle activation patterns and spatial relationships between channels. A graph neural network is then trained on these graph-structured sEMG representations to classify hand gestures in real time. The system is implemented and run on an M1 Pro CPU, and tested on data from 8 healthy subjects performing hand gestures.
AI.result: The proposed graph-based sEMG representation combined with a GNN classifier achieves an average hand gesture classification accuracy of 99%, outperforming state-of-the-art techniques on the same task. The total processing time, including both graph construction and prediction, averages 48 ms per inference on an M1 Pro CPU.
AI.conclusion: Encoding sEMG as graph networks and using a graph neural network enables highly accurate (99%) and low-latency (48 ms) hand gesture recognition with only 8 forearm electrodes. This approach is suitable for real-time control of advanced prosthetic hands and AR interfaces and appears to improve upon existing sEMG-based recognition techniques.

title: A Multi-cluster Boundary Learning Method for Out-of-Scope Intent Detection via MiniLM Embedding
summary: Intent detection is a critical task that bridges human intents and system actions in human-machine interaction systems. However, there still exist challenges for detecting out-of-scope (OOS) intents. (i) The traditional methods view the OOS intent detection as a multi-class classification, then the detection accuracy decreases as the class number of the known intents increases; (ii) LLM-embedding methods require large parameters, that makes them difficult to train and practically deploy. Thus, this work proposes a multi-cluster boundary learning method to detect OOS intents via MiniLM embedding (i.e., all-MiniLM-L6-v2) in an one-class classification workflow. The method learns the boundaries of multi-cluster embeddings generated by MiniLM from the training utterances, and then rejects the out-of-domain utterances as OOS intents. Experiments are conducted on public CLINC150, StackOverflow and Banking77 datasets. The results show that the method achieves the state-of-the-art OOS intent detection performance compared the other baselines. Ablation studies are also conducted and the results show that the used MiniLM can better adapt to the workflow and utterance embedding requirements. The code is available at supplementary materials.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a lightweight, one-class, multi-cluster boundary learning method using MiniLM embeddings to detect out-of-scope (OOS) intents, achieving state-of-the-art performance on several benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Intent detection systems struggle with accurately identifying out-of-scope (OOS) user intents. Traditional multi-class classification approaches suffer as the number of in-scope intent classes grows, reducing OOS detection accuracy. Large LLM-based embedding methods can improve performance but require substantial computational resources, making them hard to train and deploy in real-world systems. There is a need for a more scalable, efficient, and accurate approach to OOS intent detection that works well with many known intents and remains lightweight enough for practical deployment.
AI.method: The paper uses MiniLM (all-MiniLM-L6-v2) sentence embeddings within a one-class classification workflow. First, utterances from known intents are embedded using MiniLM, and their representations often form multiple clusters in the embedding space. A multi-cluster boundary learning technique is then applied to learn the decision boundaries that tightly enclose these in-scope clusters. At inference, new utterances are embedded with the same MiniLM model and tested against these learned boundaries. Utterances that fall outside the learned multi-cluster region are rejected and labeled as out-of-scope (OOS). The approach is evaluated on public datasets (CLINC150, StackOverflow, Banking77). Ablation studies examine the contribution of the MiniLM choice and its compatibility with the workflow and utterance embedding needs.
AI.result: On CLINC150, StackOverflow, and Banking77 datasets, the proposed method achieves state-of-the-art performance for OOS intent detection compared with various baselines. The experiments demonstrate that the method can more accurately detect OOS utterances without sacrificing performance on in-scope intents, even as the number of known intent classes increases. Ablation studies show that using MiniLM (all-MiniLM-L6-v2) improves adaptability to the one-class clustering workflow and better captures utterance semantics for this task than alternative embeddings.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that multi-cluster boundary learning over MiniLM embeddings is an effective and efficient solution for OOS intent detection. By framing the problem as one-class classification over multiple in-domain clusters, the method alleviates the performance degradation seen in traditional multi-class approaches when the number of intents grows. It also avoids the heavy computational cost of large LLM-embedding models while still achieving state-of-the-art performance. This makes the approach practical for deployment in real-world intent detection systems. The authors release code as supplementary materials to facilitate reproducibility and further research.

title: Agentic AI and Retrieval-Augmented Models in Straight-Through Underwriting
summary: Artificial intelligence (AI) is beginning to reshape actuarial practice, particularly in domains that require reasoning over unstructured documents, heterogeneous data sources, and regulated decision workflows. Actuaries now face a design space that ranges from traditional rule-based automation to large language models (LLMs), retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), and multi-agent ``agentic'' systems that plan, retrieve, call tools, and reflect. This paper examines how these emerging architectures can support actuarial priorities such as transparency, auditability, and human-in-the-loop governance, with a focus on straight-through decision processes. To make these ideas concrete, we develop and analyze an agentic AI framework for straight-through underwriting of small commercial Business Owner Policies (BOPs). We construct a synthetic but realistic experimental environment and compare three underwriting pipelines: (i) a single-LLM baseline, (ii) a naive RAG system, and (iii) a multi-agent ``Agentic RAG'' pipeline that combines targeted retrieval, third-party data checks, and explicit multi-step rule evaluation. The agentic system performs best overall, with the largest gains in multi-step and missing-information scenarios, where structured retrieval and reflection help the model avoid unsupported straight-through decisions.

AI.tldr: The paper explores how different AI architectures—single LLMs, basic RAG, and multi-agent agentic RAG—can support transparent, auditable straight-through underwriting decisions for small commercial insurance policies, finding that a structured multi-agent approach works best, especially for complex or incomplete cases.
AI.motivation: Actuaries increasingly need to automate decisions using AI in environments with unstructured documents, diverse data sources, and strict regulatory and governance requirements. Existing approaches range from simple rule engines to powerful but opaque LLMs, and there is a need to understand which architectures best support core actuarial requirements like transparency, auditability, and human oversight in straight-through processing contexts such as underwriting.
AI.method: The authors design a synthetic yet realistic experimental setup for straight-through underwriting of small commercial Business Owner Policies (BOPs). Within this environment, they implement and compare three AI underwriting pipelines: (i) a baseline single-LLM system that handles the entire task end-to-end, (ii) a naive RAG system that augments the LLM with simple document retrieval, and (iii) a multi-agent "Agentic RAG" system that decomposes the task into specialized agents responsible for targeted information retrieval, third-party data verification, and explicit multi-step rule evaluation aligned with underwriting guidelines. They then evaluate and compare the performance of these pipelines, with particular attention to cases requiring multi-step reasoning and handling missing information.
AI.result: The multi-agent Agentic RAG pipeline outperforms both the single-LLM baseline and the naive RAG system. Its performance advantage is most pronounced in scenarios that involve complex, multi-step reasoning or incomplete input data. The structured retrieval mechanisms, explicit rule evaluation, and reflection capabilities in the agentic system reduce unsupported or erroneous straight-through decisions and lead to more reliable underwriting outcomes.
AI.conclusion: Agentic, multi-agent RAG architectures that combine targeted retrieval, external data checks, and explicit rule-based reasoning are better suited than single-LLM or naive RAG approaches for actuarial straight-through processing tasks such as small commercial BOP underwriting. These architectures more effectively support actuarial priorities like transparency, auditability, and human-in-the-loop governance, particularly in challenging cases involving multi-step logic and missing information.

title: When Implausible Tokens Get Reinforced: Tail-Aware Credit Calibration for LLM Reinforcement Learning
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has achieved remarkable success in enhancing the reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs). However, widely used critic-free RL methods rely on uniform credit assignment, broadcasting the same advantage to all tokens regardless of their differences. We identify a critical failure mode of this design, which we refer to as Positive-Credit Contamination: low-probability tail tokens that are contextually erroneous receive identical positive credit to plausible ones within the same trajectory, resulting in the indiscriminate reinforcement of flawed reasoning behavior. To mitigate this issue, we propose Tail-Aware Credit calibratiOn (TACO), a method that calibrates uniform credit assignment to suppress undesirable positive updates. TACO first computes a tail-risk score that incorporates the local generation context to assess each token's risk of falling into the unreliable tail, distinguishing unexpected rarity from uncertainty-driven exploration. TACO then uses this score to tune positive credit for risky tokens without removing their gradients entirely, so that recurring useful rare patterns can accumulate reinforcement while incidental noise is progressively dampened. Experimental results across three LLMs and eight benchmarks show that TACO consistently outperforms GRPO-style baselines. Notably, TACO improves training stability, supporting sustained performance gains in long-horizon RL. The source code is available at: https://github.com/xiuyilou/TACO.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies a failure mode in critic-free RL for LLMs—uniformly giving positive credit to all tokens in a successful trajectory—and proposes TACO, a tail-aware credit calibration method that reduces positive updates for risky, low-probability tokens to improve reasoning and training stability.
AI.motivation: Critic-free RL methods for LLMs typically assign the same scalar advantage to every token in a trajectory. This uniform credit assignment ignores token-wise differences and can positively reinforce contextually wrong, low-probability tail tokens whenever the overall trajectory is rewarded. This leads to "positive-credit contamination," where flawed reasoning patterns are reinforced alongside good ones, harming performance and stability, especially in long-horizon reasoning tasks. The paper aims to diagnose and fix this structural issue so that RL can more precisely reinforce useful reasoning behavior in LLMs.
AI.method: The authors propose Tail-Aware Credit calibratiOn (TACO). TACO computes a token-level tail-risk score that depends on the local generation context, measuring how likely each token is to belong to an unreliable low-probability tail, while distinguishing between legitimately rare but meaningful tokens and noise from uncertainty-driven exploration. Using this score, TACO rescales the positive advantage assigned to each token: risky tokens receive attenuated positive credit rather than full credit, but their gradients are not completely removed. This preserves the ability to reinforce recurring, genuinely useful rare patterns while progressively suppressing incidental noisy ones. TACO is applied within critic-free, GRPO-style RL training pipelines for LLMs.
AI.result: Across three different LLMs and eight benchmarks, TACO consistently outperforms GRPO-style critic-free RL baselines. The method yields better reasoning performance and improved training stability, particularly over long-horizon RL training where uniform credit assignment tends to accumulate harmful updates. Empirical results demonstrate that tail-aware credit calibration can be integrated into existing RL setups while delivering sustained gains.
AI.conclusion: Uniform credit assignment in critic-free RL introduces a specific failure mode—positive-credit contamination—where erroneous low-probability tail tokens are indiscriminately reinforced. TACO addresses this by estimating a context-aware tail-risk score for each token and using it to calibrate positive credit, damping noisy updates while preserving learning from genuinely useful rare events. Experiments show that this approach improves both performance and stability over GRPO-style baselines, suggesting that tail-aware token-level credit assignment is an effective and practical enhancement for RL training of LLMs.

title: Feedback Manipulation Regularization: Enabling Offline Agent Alignment for Imitation Learning
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) research has increasingly shifted focus towards alignment, ensuring agents learn behaviors adhering to human values. While human demonstrations and feedback have proven crucial for alignment, existing approaches predominantly combine these signals using multi-stage pipelines designed for the contextual bandit framing of language generation. Yet little work explores how these complementary inputs can serve as a richer, interconnected signal for single-stage offline training in fully sequential decision-making environments. We propose Feedback Manipulation Regularization (FMR), an algorithm-agnostic method that harnesses evaluative feedback as a corrective signal to improve the alignment of imitation learning policies. We adapt Safety Gymnasium environments to be a principled testbed for alignment evaluation, demonstrating improved aptitude and up to a 98\% reduction in misalignment across a range of imitation learning algorithms. FMR remains robust in limited data regimes, even when learning from scarce aligned and uninformative noisy demonstrations.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Feedback Manipulation Regularization (FMR), which uses evaluative feedback to better align imitation learning policies with human values in sequential decision-making tasks, significantly reducing misalignment in adapted Safety Gymnasium environments.
AI.motivation: Most alignment work in RL for language models treats learning as contextual bandits and uses multi-stage pipelines to combine demonstrations and feedback. There is limited exploration of how demonstrations and evaluative feedback can be jointly exploited as a single, richer signal for offline training in fully sequential decision-making. The authors aim to close this gap and provide a principled evaluation testbed for alignment in such environments.
AI.method: The authors propose Feedback Manipulation Regularization (FMR), an algorithm-agnostic regularization scheme that incorporates evaluative feedback as a corrective term on top of imitation learning objectives, steering policies away from misaligned behaviors observed in demonstrations. They adapt Safety Gymnasium environments into a benchmark specifically tailored for alignment evaluation and apply FMR on top of various imitation learning algorithms in offline, sequential RL settings, including regimes with limited and noisy data.
AI.result: Across multiple adapted Safety Gymnasium tasks and a range of imitation learning baselines, FMR improves task aptitude and achieves up to a 98% reduction in misalignment metrics. It shows strong performance even with scarce aligned data and when demonstrations are noisy or contain uninformative/partially misaligned behavior, indicating robustness of the regularizer.
AI.conclusion: Feedback Manipulation Regularization offers a simple, algorithm-agnostic way to fuse human-like evaluative feedback with demonstrations in a single-stage offline training process for sequential RL, yielding substantially better alignment with human values and robustness to data limitations. The adapted Safety Gymnasium environments provide a principled testbed, and results suggest that leveraging feedback as a corrective regularizer is a promising direction for scalable alignment in imitation learning policies.

title: A Reliability Assessment of LALM Audio Judges for Full-Duplex Voice Agents
comment: 28 pages total (12 main body, 1 reference, 15 appendix). In main body: 2 diagrams, 3 table, 2 charts
summary: We report the empirical reliability of Gemini models as audio judges that score full-duplex agent conversations directly from the raw stereo waveform, tested across three models in the Gemini family: 2.5 Flash, 3.5 Flash, and 3.1 Pro. Our primary evidence base uses Gemini 2.5 Flash as the ground-truth model, validated against three calibrated human raters on 209 stereo sessions, scored on 8 production dimensions: 152 full-duplex conversations across 13 accent-and-condition strata, together with 57 adversarial defect-injected clips. The evidence for Gemini 2.5 Flash is consistent across three tests. (i) On 5 of 8 dimensions the LALM-human Spearman rho departs from the pairwise human-human rho by at most 0.07, and on 7 of 8 dimensions the two quantities 95 percent bootstrap confidence intervals overlap. (ii) The LALM agrees with the three-rater human mean within 1 point on 60 to 92 percent of sessions on 6 of 8 dimensions. (iii) On 45 of 48 (defect, dimension) cells the LALM is as sensitive as humans or better under Newcombe-Wilson 95 percent confidence intervals, though most of these are underpowered nulls rather than demonstrated parity. Rank-ordering ability transfers across the Gemini family: 3.5 Flash improves simple agreement to 8 of 8 dimensions, while 3.1 Pro rates several dimensions markedly lower than humans despite comparable rank correlation. A model swap should be re-validated on calibration specifically, not assumed from rank-correlation alone. We identify four areas where deployment requires care, and we estimate that human rating alone for our current evaluation cadence costs roughly two orders of magnitude more than the equivalent LALM workload. The data presented here provides a defensible empirical basis for deploying the LALM as a substitute or fourth rater on the dimensions where the evidence supports it.

AI.tldr: The paper empirically evaluates Gemini LALM models as automated audio judges for full-duplex conversations, finding that Gemini 2.5 Flash can often substitute for human raters with similar reliability on several rating dimensions, while highlighting calibration and deployment caveats.
AI.motivation: Human evaluation of full-duplex conversational agents is expensive and slow, especially when done across many accents, conditions, and quality dimensions. There is a need for reliable automated judges that can directly assess conversational audio quality from raw waveforms, reducing cost and enabling scalable, frequent evaluation. The authors want to know whether a large audio-language model (LALM) like Gemini can safely act as such a judge and in what situations it can complement or partially replace human raters.
AI.method: The authors use Gemini 2.5 Flash as a candidate ground-truth judge, first validating it against three calibrated human raters on 209 stereo sessions (152 natural conversations and 57 adversarial, defect-injected clips) scored across 8 production dimensions and 13 accent/condition strata. They compute human–model and human–human Spearman rank correlations, session-level score agreement within 1-point bands, and sensitivity to injected defects using Newcombe–Wilson confidence intervals over 48 (defect, dimension) combinations. They then compare rank-order transfer and calibration for Gemini 3.5 Flash and Gemini 3.1 Pro relative to the 2.5 Flash ground-truth, examining both correlation and absolute score levels.
AI.result: Gemini 2.5 Flash shows human-comparable rank correlation on most dimensions: on 5/8 dimensions its Spearman rho vs. humans differs from human–human rho by at most 0.07, and on 7/8 dimensions their bootstrap 95% CIs overlap. It matches the three-rater human mean within 1 point for 60–92% of sessions on 6/8 dimensions. For defect sensitivity, in 45 of 48 (defect, dimension) cells the model is at least as sensitive as humans within Newcombe–Wilson 95% CIs, though many estimates are underpowered. Rank-ordering skill largely transfers across Gemini models: 3.5 Flash improves agreement to humans on all 8 dimensions, while 3.1 Pro achieves similar rank correlation but systematically lower absolute scores on several dimensions, indicating calibration issues. Human-only rating for their evaluation cadence is estimated to cost ~100× more than using the LALM.
AI.conclusion: Gemini 2.5 Flash provides sufficient empirical reliability to serve as a substitute or additional “fourth rater” for several conversational audio quality dimensions, substantially reducing evaluation cost. However, deployment requires caution in at least four identified areas, particularly around calibration and defect sensitivity, and model variants cannot be assumed interchangeable based solely on rank correlation: any model swap should be revalidated for calibration. The work offers an empirical foundation and practical guidance for using LALMs as automated audio judges in full-duplex agent evaluation.

title: Nigeria Machinery: A Low-Resource Industrial Dataset with a Domain-Grounded Reasoning Layer
summary: There is relatively little, public, and model-ready data on industrial machinery for African economies. This makes it hard to do quantitative analysis or to train language models on numeric tasks grounded in that setting. We release two things to help with part of this problem. The first is the Nigeria Machinery Usage and Failures Dataset: 89 machine-level records across 28 indicators, covering Nigeria's manufacturing and oil and gas sectors from 2006 to 2025. Every record names a public source and is decoded by a codebook. The second is a method for building chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning examples from these sparse numeric values. The result is 94 prompt, completion, and reasoning-trace rows. In every row, the prompt names the real indicator, subsector, year, and source of the record it comes from. The data adaptation work was carried out by Adaption Labs. Along the way we describe a problem that is common when language models are used to build datasets. The prompts can match the real numbers while saying nothing about the real domain. We show that fixing this raises the share of domain-grounded prompts from 1 out of 78 in an earlier release to 94 out of 94, and that every retrieval answer now matches its source value (84 out of 84). We release the data, the reasoning layer, and a per-row provenance file under CC-BY-4.0. We are clear about the limits. With 89 records and 17 indicators that have only one observation, this is a reference and seed dataset, not a large training set. Most reasoning rows are retrieval rather than multi-step computation.

AI.tldr: The paper releases a small, well-documented dataset on machinery usage and failures in Nigeria plus a set of chain-of-thought reasoning examples built from it, and discusses how to keep such prompts truly grounded in the real domain.
AI.motivation: There is a lack of public, structured, model-ready data on industrial machinery in African economies, which prevents robust quantitative analysis and hinders training language models on numerically grounded tasks in this context. The authors aim to partially fill this gap and to improve how language-model-based reasoning datasets are constructed from sparse numeric data.
AI.method: (1) Curate and standardize 89 machine-level records with 28 indicators from Nigeria’s manufacturing and oil and gas sectors (2006–2025), linking each to a public source and a codebook. (2) Design a procedure to generate chain-of-thought reasoning examples (prompts, completions, and reasoning traces) from these sparse numeric values, ensuring that prompts explicitly reference the real indicators, subsectors, years, and sources. (3) Diagnose and correct a failure mode where language-model-generated prompts match real numbers but are semantically ungrounded in the domain, and measure grounding and numeric correctness before and after the fix.
AI.result: The authors produce the Nigeria Machinery Usage and Failures Dataset containing 89 records across 28 indicators and a CoT reasoning layer with 94 rows (prompt, completion, and trace). After fixing the grounding issue, 94/94 prompts are domain-grounded and all evaluated retrieval answers (84/84) match their source values. All data, reasoning traces, and row-level provenance are released under CC-BY-4.0.
AI.conclusion: The work offers a small but carefully curated reference and seed dataset for industrial machinery in Nigeria, along with a grounded CoT reasoning layer and transparent provenance. While the dataset is far too small and sparse (including 17 single-observation indicators) to serve as a large-scale training resource and most reasoning examples involve simple retrieval rather than complex computation, it establishes standards and tooling that can be extended to build richer, domain-faithful numeric datasets for African industrial contexts.

title: Hallucination Self-Play: Bootstrapping Reinforced Detector via Evolved Generator
comment: Accepted to COLM 2026. Camera-ready version to appear
summary: Identifying faithfulness hallucinations in LLM-generated outputs remains challenging due to the scarcity of high-quality annotated data. Recent work relies on advanced LLMs to synthesize training data, including rationales, labels, and hallucinated claims. However, these methods treat the generator as a static component, limiting iterative improvement of the detector. To address this limitation, we introduce Hallucination Self-Play (HSP), a novel framework that enables the detector to bootstrap with an evolved generator. HSP involves two roles initialized from the same base model, a detector that assesses the faithfulness of model outputs, and a generator that produces increasingly hard-to-detect hallucinated responses. Specifically, the detector is first fine-tuned on human-labeled data and then employed as a reward model to train the generator via reinforcement learning from AI feedback (RLAIF). In turn, the evolved generator synthesizes hallucination data to further optimize the detector through rule-based reinforcement learning. Experiments on RAGTruth benchmark and two model families demonstrate that the proposed framework can progressively enhance a small LLM to match or even outperform advanced LLMs without external supervision. Our code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/Hallucination-Self-Play-50B5 .

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Hallucination Self-Play (HSP), a framework where a detector and generator LLM co-evolve to better identify faithfulness hallucinations without relying on external supervision.
AI.motivation: Faithfulness hallucinations in LLM outputs are hard to detect due to a lack of high-quality annotated data. Existing approaches synthesize training data using advanced LLMs but treat the generator as static, preventing iterative improvement of the hallucination detector and limiting performance, especially for smaller models without constant access to strong external models.
AI.method: HSP initializes two roles from the same base model: a detector and a generator. The detector is first fine-tuned on human-labeled data, then used as a reward model to optimize the generator through reinforcement learning from AI feedback (RLAIF), encouraging the generator to produce harder-to-detect hallucinations. The improved generator then creates challenging hallucination data, which is used to further train and refine the detector via rule-based reinforcement learning, forming a self-play loop that iteratively improves both components.
AI.result: On the RAGTruth benchmark and across two LLM families, HSP progressively improves a small LLM’s hallucination detection ability, enabling it to reach or surpass the performance of more advanced LLMs, all without relying on ongoing external supervision.
