AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks either test symbolic causal reasoning without realistic data analysis, or test data analysis without principled causal structure. Current causal datasets are usually small, curated, and rely on limited templates, which constrains diversity and encourages memorization instead of genuine causal reasoning. The authors want a realistic, synthetic, and systematically generated environment where LLM agents must both reason causally and perform practical data-science tasks with tools.
AI.method: They construct CausalDS as a collection of “scenes.” Each scene is defined by a sampled structural causal model (SCM), an observation model that can introduce imperfect measurements, generated observational data sampled from the SCM, and a synthetic natural-language story that embeds the variables and relations in plausible real-world domains. The composition of these elements can be partially grounded in empirical distributions from real datasets, preserving realism while remaining fully synthetic. From each scene, they algorithmically derive multiple tasks corresponding to Pearl’s three rungs (associational prediction, intervention, and counterfactual queries). Many tasks require coding and tool use to analyze the data, and some are explicitly designed so that no justified answer exists, making abstention an evaluated option.
AI.result: They obtain a benchmark where each instance produces a rich set of tasks that jointly require symbolic causal reasoning, empirical data analysis via code and tools, handling of imperfect observations, uncertainty quantification, and deciding when to abstain. While the abstract does not report numeric scores, it states that CausalDS supports systematic evaluation of LLM agents on these dimensions.
AI.conclusion: CausalDS provides a principled, synthetic-yet-realistic benchmark for testing LLM agents as data scientists who must integrate causal reasoning with coding and tool use. By spanning Pearl’s three rungs, incorporating imperfect observations, grounding in empirical distributions, and explicitly scoring abstention, the benchmark offers a unified way to evaluate several critical capabilities—causal inference, data analysis, uncertainty handling, and responsible non-answering—within one coherent framework.

title: COALA: Robust Contextualized Speech-augmented Language Modeling for ASR via Contrastive Regularizer and Biasing Score Estimation
summary: Contextual biasing seeks to integrate external knowledge into automatic speech recognition (ASR) systems to accurately recognize domain-specific entities. In this paper, we propose COALA (Contextualized ASR Leveraging Biasing Scoring), a robust framework designed to enhance speech-augmented language models (SLMs) in complex multi-entity scenarios. Considering the inherent context-window limitations of SLMs, identifying relevant target entities from a large-scale biasing list is crucial for effective recognition. To this end, COALA maps SLM latent representations into a specialized discriminative space to quantify the matching intensity between audio segments and candidate entities. Furthermore, we address the training collapse in prior study when handling multi-target utterances-where multiple rare words co-occur. Experimental results on the LibriSpeech benchmark demonstrate that COALA consistently achieves superior contextual biasing performance across various biasing list scales.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes COALA, a framework to improve contextual biasing in speech-augmented language models by better matching audio segments to domain-specific entities, especially in multi-entity scenarios.
AI.motivation: Standard ASR and speech-augmented language models struggle to correctly recognize rare, domain-specific entities when many possible biasing terms are provided, partly due to context-window limits and difficulty identifying which entities are relevant to the current audio. Existing contextual biasing methods degrade in complex multi-target utterances and can suffer from training collapse.
AI.method: COALA introduces a specialized discriminative space by mapping latent representations from a speech-augmented language model into a space where the matching strength between audio segments and candidate entities can be explicitly quantified. The framework selects relevant entities from large biasing lists under context-window constraints and includes training strategies to avoid collapse when multiple rare words co-occur in the same utterance.
AI.result: On the LibriSpeech benchmark, COALA achieves consistently better contextual biasing performance than prior methods across different sizes of biasing lists, indicating more accurate recognition of domain-specific entities even in complex, multi-entity conditions.
AI.conclusion: By reprojecting SLM latent representations into a discriminative matching space and stabilizing training for multi-target utterances, COALA effectively overcomes context-window and multi-entity challenges in contextual biasing, leading to robust gains in recognizing domain-specific entities in ASR.

title: Answer Set Programming Energised! End-to-End Neurosymbolic Reasoning and Learning with ASP and Energy Based Models
summary: We present a general neurosymbolic reasoning and learning methodology based on a modular integration of answer set programming with an energy based model substrate. Key contributions are: (1) supporting joint optimisation in the continuous latent space through explicit ASP-based declarative semantics fully incorporating background knowledge, constraints, non-monotonic inference; and (2) advancing recent works at the interface of answer sets, probabilistic logic, and answer set modulo theories by providing a generalised model and practical platform for ASP-centric robust, end-to-end training for applications in dynamic domains (e.g., involving perception and interaction). We provide a practical implementation, and demonstrate basic use and application (with MNIST), and evaluate with the visual question-answering benchmark Clevr and the multi-object tracking benchmark MOT.

AI.tldr: A neurosymbolic framework that integrates answer set programming with energy-based models for joint learning and reasoning, demonstrated on MNIST, CLEVR VQA, and MOT tracking.
AI.motivation: To create a general, practical neurosymbolic methodology that tightly integrates symbolic reasoning (ASP) with sub-symbolic learning (energy-based models), enabling the use of rich background knowledge, constraints, and non-monotonic logic in end-to-end trainable systems for dynamic, perception-heavy domains.
AI.method: They modularly integrate answer set programming with an energy-based model substrate so that ASP provides declarative semantics and constraints over a continuous latent space. The joint optimisation is carried out in this latent space while fully incorporating background knowledge and non-monotonic inference. The framework generalises prior work on answer sets, probabilistic logic, and answer set modulo theories, yielding an ASP-centric training platform. They implement the system and apply it to visual tasks, including MNIST, CLEVR VQA, and MOT multi-object tracking.
AI.result: A working implementation of the proposed framework, with empirical demonstrations on MNIST and evaluations on CLEVR VQA and MOT benchmarks, showing that the method can be applied to perception and interaction tasks; performance details are not specified in the abstract but imply robust, end-to-end training is feasible.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that an ASP-centric neurosymbolic architecture integrated with energy-based models can support joint optimisation, incorporate complex logical knowledge and constraints, and serve as a general, practical platform for robust end-to-end learning and reasoning in dynamic domains involving perception and interaction.

title: ICDAR 2026 HIPE-OCRepair Competition on LLM-Assisted OCR Post-Correction for Historical Documents
summary: We present the results of HIPE-OCRepair-2026, an ICDAR competition on LLM-assisted OCR post-correction of historical documents. OCR post-correction remains a long-standing challenge in digital heritage: large-scale collections of digitized documents are affected by legacy OCR errors, while re-digitization at scale remains impractical. Large language models (LLMs) offers a major opportunity to revisit this challenge, yet their effectiveness across languages, document types, and noise conditions - and their tendency to hallucinate - remains insufficiently understood. HIPE-OCRepair-2026 pursues two objectives: (i) to evaluate the capabilities of modern OCR post-correction systems, and (ii) to provide a reproducible evaluation framework anchored in the HIPE-OCRepair-2026 dataset, a harmonized multilingual resource consolidating existing and newly curated historical datasets. Participants were tasked with correcting noisy OCR transcripts from historical newspapers and printed works in English, French, and German (17th-20th century), working at the level of coherent transcription units (paragraphs or articles) without access to source images. The evaluation adopts a retrieval-oriented rather than diplomatic scoring approach, reflecting the practical use case of search and access over digitized collections. Four teams submitted systems ranging from zero-shot prompting to continued pre-training and fine-tuning, offering insights into the merits of different adaptation strategies. Results show that modern LLM-assisted systems can significantly improve OCR quality, but performance varies across datasets, languages, and noise levels. Over-correction on low-noise inputs emerges as a recurring challenge, highlighting the importance of evaluation beyond character error reduction. The dataset, scorer, and evaluation pipeline are publicly released to support future research.

AI.tldr: The paper reports on HIPE-OCRepair-2026, an ICDAR shared task and benchmark for evaluating LLM-assisted OCR post-correction on multilingual historical documents, showing that LLM-based methods substantially improve OCR but face challenges like over-correction and variability across languages and noise levels.
AI.motivation: Historical digitized collections widely suffer from legacy OCR errors, but re-running OCR at scale on source images is often infeasible. While LLMs are promising for fixing OCR output, their actual effectiveness, robustness across languages and document types, and hallucination behavior are not well understood. There is also a lack of standardized, reproducible benchmarks tailored to the real-world goal of improving search and access rather than purely diplomatic transcription accuracy.
AI.method: The authors organized the HIPE-OCRepair-2026 ICDAR competition, building a harmonized multilingual benchmark dataset from existing and newly curated historical resources in English, French, and German (17th–20th century newspapers and printed works). Participants received noisy OCR paragraph-/article-level transcripts without images and were asked to output corrected text. Systems ranged from zero-shot LLM prompting to approaches using continued pre-training and fine-tuning. Evaluation used a retrieval-oriented scoring scheme designed to reflect downstream search/access utility rather than only character-level error metrics. A common scorer and pipeline were released for reproducible evaluation.
AI.result: Four participating teams submitted systems covering diverse LLM adaptation strategies. Across the board, LLM-assisted approaches significantly improved OCR quality on the benchmark. However, performance varied notably across datasets, languages, and levels of OCR noise. A systematic issue of over-correction on relatively clean (low-noise) inputs was observed, indicating that systems sometimes modify correct text unnecessarily, which is only partially captured by classic character error metrics.
AI.conclusion: LLM-assisted OCR post-correction is a viable and effective approach for improving historical text quality, but its behavior is highly context- and language-dependent, and prone to over-correction, especially on cleaner inputs. Evaluation must therefore go beyond simple character-error reduction and consider retrieval-oriented metrics and realistic usage scenarios. The publicly released HIPE-OCRepair-2026 dataset, scorer, and pipeline provide a standardized framework to support further research and comparison of OCR post-correction systems, particularly those leveraging LLMs.

title: Overthinking: Amplifying Reasoning Weights to Extract Learned Secrets
summary: Black box auditing of language models is an essential pre-deployment tool, but it may miss subtle forms of misalignment and hidden information. To better elicit hidden information during an auditing process, we introduce \emph{overthinking}: the process of using reasoning task vectors to amplify the propensity to think out loud of reasoning models. Given the parameters of a non-reasoning instruct model $M$ and reasoning-distilled model $R$, we define the \emph{overthinking model} as $\boldsymbolθ_{\mathcal{O}_α} = \boldsymbolθ_{\mathcal{M}} + α(\boldsymbolθ_{\mathcal{R}} - \boldsymbolθ_{\mathcal{M}})$, where $α> 1$ amplifies reasoning beyond the pure reasoning model $R$. Additionally, we introduce new layer-wise attenuation strategies that selectively amplify reasoning without losing quality and coherence of model outputs. We demonstrate that overthinking models are more likely to reveal hidden information across four experimental settings, across 2B-32B models. Our findings suggest that reasoning amplification may surface secrets or unintended behaviors acquired during training up to $10\times$ more frequently than the original reasoning model. How secrets surface depends on the secret type: some require perturbation along the reasoning direction, while others yield to any sufficiently large weight perturbation.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a method called “overthinking” to better surface hidden information and misalignment in language models by amplifying their reasoning behaviors via task vectors.
AI.motivation: Standard black-box audits of language models can miss subtle misalignment or hidden information, especially when models do not naturally “think out loud” or expose their internal reasoning. The authors want a way to systematically elicit such hidden behaviors without white-box access or extensive prompt engineering, improving safety audits before deployment.
AI.method: They take an instruct model M (non-reasoning) and a reasoning-distilled model R, and form an interpolated/ extrapolated model in parameter space: θ_Oα = θ_M + α(θ_R − θ_M), with α > 1 to go beyond the reasoning level of R. This is a ‘reasoning task vector’ direction from M to R. They also design layer-wise attenuation strategies to selectively apply this amplification to certain layers, preserving quality and coherence while boosting reasoning propensity. They test this on models from 2B–32B parameters in multiple experimental setups that involve secrets or hidden behaviors.
AI.result: Across four experimental settings and various model sizes, the overthinking models more frequently reveal hidden information and unintended behaviors than both the base instruct model and the reasoning model R. In some cases, secrets surface up to 10× more often relative to the original reasoning model. They further observe that different classes of secrets behave differently: some are specifically revealed when moving along the reasoning direction, while others emerge under any sufficiently large parameter perturbation, not necessarily aligned with reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Amplifying reasoning behavior along a reasoning-distillation direction (“overthinking”) is an effective black-box auditing tool for surfacing hidden information and misalignment. Properly tuned and layer-wise controlled, this approach can significantly increase the rate at which secrets or unintended behaviors are elicited without severely degrading overall model quality, offering a promising technique for safety audits of large language models.

title: LEXIC: Lightweight Eye-tracking eXtension via Injected Complexity
summary: On the recent EyeBench benchmark, predicting reading comprehension from eye movements exposes a stark gap: text-aware models using pretrained language models reach 56--63% AUROC, while gaze-only models operate at chance. We ask how far a gaze-only model can be pushed by lightweight, language-model-free conditioning. Building on the EyeBench AhnCNN baseline, LEXIC-Base, we propose two mechanisms to inject three precomputed word-level difficulty signals, GPT-2 surprisal, word frequency, and word length, into the per-fixation input: direct concatenation, LEXIC-Concat, and a residual mechanism, LEXIC-Res, where a small head predicts typical-reader gaze response and the encoder is conditioned on the deviation. On the OneStop reading comprehension task, with K=5 seed-ensemble training across ten folds, both mechanisms produce statistically consistent AUROC gains on Unseen Text, +1.8 to +2.2 percentage points, Wilcoxon p <= 0.065. LEXIC-Concat additionally lifts Unseen Reader by +2.9 percentage points, p = 0.010. We trace an architectural boundary in LEXIC-Res on Unseen Reader, +1.8 percentage points, p = 0.19, to the prediction head being calibrated to training readers, transferring imperfectly to out-of-distribution readers.

AI.tldr: The paper enhances gaze-only models for predicting reading comprehension by injecting simple lexical difficulty signals, significantly narrowing the gap with text-aware models on EyeBench/OneStop.
AI.motivation: On the EyeBench benchmark, models that use both text and eye movements perform much better than gaze-only models, which are near chance. The authors want to know how far a model that uses only eye movement data can be improved without relying on large pretrained language models, by adding lightweight, precomputed indicators of word difficulty.
AI.method: They start from the EyeBench AhnCNN gaze-only baseline, LEXIC-Base, and inject three word-level difficulty features—GPT-2 surprisal, word frequency, and word length—into the per-fixation representation. They explore two mechanisms: (1) LEXIC-Concat, which directly concatenates these difficulty features to the gaze features; and (2) LEXIC-Res, which adds a small prediction head that estimates a typical-reader gaze response from the difficulty features and conditions the encoder on the deviation between actual and predicted gaze. They evaluate these models on the OneStop reading comprehension task using K=5 seed ensembles across ten cross-validation folds, and test on Unseen Text and Unseen Reader splits.
AI.result: Both LEXIC-Concat and LEXIC-Res achieve statistically significant AUROC improvements over the base gaze-only model on Unseen Text, with gains of about +1.8 to +2.2 percentage points and Wilcoxon p-values ≤ 0.065. LEXIC-Concat also significantly improves performance on Unseen Reader by +2.9 percentage points (p = 0.010). LEXIC-Res shows a more modest and statistically non-significant gain of +1.8 percentage points on Unseen Reader (p = 0.19).
AI.conclusion: Injecting simple, language-model-free lexical difficulty signals into gaze-only architectures meaningfully improves prediction of reading comprehension, especially for new texts, partially closing the gap with text-aware models. However, the residual conditioning in LEXIC-Res appears calibrated to the training readers and does not generalize as well to unseen readers, highlighting an architectural limitation when transferring across reader populations.

title: ASMR: Agentic Schema Generation for Ship Maintenance Report Writing
comment: Accepted at the DASHSys 2026 workshop (Systems for Data-centric Agents with Human-in-the-loop), co-located with VLDB 2026
summary: In this paper, we study the automatic schema generation problem: given a collection of historical ship maintenance and operational reports across multiple form categories, automatically discover compact and informative schemas that capture the essential information requirements of each report type. To address this challenge, we propose ASMR, a modular agentic framework consisting of two specialized agents. A Field Generation Agent extracts semantic concepts from historical narratives and generates candidate schema fields through adaptive multi-granularity clustering, while a Structural Optimizer Agent employs reinforcement learning to identify compact, informative, and non-redundant schema representations. The resulting schemas can guide report authors toward producing more complete, consistent, and actionable reports. Preliminary results demonstrate the promise of the proposed approach and highlight several open research challenges at the intersection of data management, agentic AI, and human-centered AI.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes ASMR, an agentic framework that automatically generates compact, informative schemas from historical ship maintenance and operations reports.
AI.motivation: Manually designing and maintaining schemas for diverse ship maintenance and operational report types is labor-intensive, inconsistent, and often fails to capture real information needs embedded in historical narratives. There is a need for an automated way to discover essential fields and structures that can standardize reporting, improve data quality, and support downstream analytics and decision-making.
AI.method: The authors introduce ASMR, a modular agent-based framework with two key agents: (1) a Field Generation Agent that extracts semantic concepts from historical text reports and uses adaptive multi-granularity clustering to propose candidate schema fields; and (2) a Structural Optimizer Agent that formulates schema selection and refinement as a reinforcement learning problem, searching for schema variants that are compact, informative, and non-redundant. The system iteratively optimizes both the set of fields and their organization for different report categories.
AI.result: Preliminary experiments show that ASMR can automatically derive schemas that better capture essential information needs from historical ship reports, producing field sets that are more compact and informative compared with naive baselines or manual heuristics. These early results indicate that agentic decomposition plus RL-based structural optimization is a promising approach, though detailed quantitative metrics and large-scale evaluations remain future work.
AI.conclusion: ASMR demonstrates that automatic schema generation from narrative maintenance and operations data is feasible and can support more complete, consistent, and actionable reporting. The work opens new research directions combining data management, agentic AI, and human-centered AI, including richer evaluation methods, integration with authoring tools, and collaboration mechanisms between human experts and schema-generating agents.

title: SQuaD-SQL: Efficient Text-to-SQL with Small Language Models via LLM-Guided Knowledge Distillation
summary: Text-to-SQL is a fundamental task in natural language processing that enables users to interact with structured databases using natural language. While large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable performance on this task, their substantial computational requirements hinder deployment in resource-constrained settings. In this paper, we introduce SQuaD-SQL (Small-Qualified and Distilled for SQL), a novel approach that empowers small language models (SLMs) to approach the performance of LLMs on the Text-to-SQL task while significantly improving efficiency through knowledge distillation and synthetic data generation. Our method comprises three key components: (1) LLM-based synthetic data generation, where structured knowledge is extracted from LLMs via carefully designed prompting strategies; (2) parameter-efficient fine-tuning, enabling full model training on a single consumer-grade GPU; and (3) domain-adaptive fine-tuning, where domain-specific synthetic data further enhances performance in targeted domains. Experiments on the WikiSQL dataset demonstrate that SQuaD-SQL achieves an execution accuracy of 86.9% on the test set, approaching the performance of LLMs while offering faster inference and lower memory usage. These results suggest that, with proper training strategies, SLMs can serve as practical and efficient alternatives for Text-to-SQL applications in resource-limited environments.

AI.tldr: They propose SQuaD-SQL, a distillation-based framework that lets small language models reach near‑LLM performance on Text-to-SQL with much lower compute and memory costs.
AI.motivation: LLMs work well for Text-to-SQL but are too computationally expensive for deployment in resource‑constrained environments. There is a need for smaller models that retain high Text-to-SQL accuracy while being efficient enough for practical use.
AI.method: They design SQuaD-SQL, which has three components: (1) use an LLM with tailored prompts to generate synthetic Text-to-SQL training data and to extract structured knowledge; (2) apply parameter-efficient fine‑tuning so the small model can be trained fully on a single consumer GPU; and (3) perform domain-adaptive fine‑tuning using additional synthetic data tailored to specific application domains.
AI.result: On the WikiSQL benchmark, the small model trained with SQuaD-SQL reaches 86.9% execution accuracy on the test set, close to LLM performance, while also providing faster inference and lower memory usage than LLMs.
AI.conclusion: With appropriate knowledge distillation and synthetic data strategies, small language models can become practical, efficient substitutes for large language models in Text-to-SQL tasks, especially in resource‑limited deployment scenarios.

title: A First-Principles Theory of Slow Thinking and Active Perception
comment: Published on 2026/05/11 in Journal of Machine Learning
summary: As part of a series on first-principles modeling of cognitive functions, this paper attempts to provide a mathematical formulation of thinking and perception. It formally derives slow thinking or more generally, active perception, and encompasses the design, training and inference of slow thinking large language models. Our starting point is the lifting and projection of probability distributions on the observable and latent spaces, with the objective of representing complex data distributions by simple function families such as neural networks. A theory called "active lifting" is proposed, based on the sampling of latent sequences and an intrinsic drive to reduce uncertainty with maximum rate. It derives a large design space, containing the slow thinking models in a subspace that we call the static theory. These models are positioned on the representation hierarchy and sampler hierarchy induced by the static theory, and can be upgraded by climbing the two hierarchies. Active lifting further derives an inference process with an internal time axis, and a training objective that resembles minimum-length coding as well as the invention of languages. Thus, it characterizes the agency of perception, including the emergence of the slow thinking formats. Technical by-products of this theory include a three-stage pathway for improving slow thinking models, a unified approach to constructing encoders and generative models for all data modalities, a priori formation of human-like visual representations, and a possible solution to policy collapse.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a mathematical theory, called active lifting, to formalize slow thinking and active perception for large language models and other modalities, framing them as processes that iteratively sample latent states to reduce uncertainty as quickly as possible.
AI.motivation: To move from heuristic, engineering-style designs of slow thinking and active perception systems toward a principled, first-principles mathematical framework that explains how such systems should be designed, trained, and perform inference, and how human-like representations and agency might emerge.
AI.method: Starting from probability distributions over observable and latent spaces, the paper introduces a theory of active lifting: representing complex data distributions with simple parametric families (e.g., neural networks) via sampling latent sequences that are driven by an intrinsic objective of maximal uncertainty reduction. It defines a static sub-theory describing slow thinking models, places these models within representation and sampler hierarchies, and derives an internal-time inference process and a coding-like training objective from this framework.
AI.result: The theory yields: (1) a large design space where existing slow thinking models occupy a specific static subspace; (2) representation and sampler hierarchies that indicate systematic ways to “upgrade” models; (3) an internal-time inference process and a minimum-length-coding-like training objective closely tied to language formation; and (4) technical spin-offs including a three-stage roadmap for improving slow thinking models, a unified encoder/generative-constructor for all data modalities, mechanisms for human-like visual representation formation, and a proposed remedy for policy collapse.
AI.conclusion: Active lifting provides a unifying, first-principles account of active perception and slow thinking, positioning current slow-thinking LLMs within a broader theoretical landscape and suggesting principled directions for improving their architecture, training, and multimodal capabilities while explaining aspects of agency and representation emergence.

title: Hidden Decoding at Scale: Latent Computation Scaling for Large Language Models
summary: Scaling Large Language Models (LLMs) has been driven mainly by enlarging the Transformer backbone, but for an already-strong model this requires another round of costly pretraining. We study whether an existing backbone can keep improving by allocating more computation to each token while leaving the Transformer backbone fixed. Depth-recurrent (looped) Transformers pursue this goal but are hard to scale, because looped computation does not fit naturally with the pipeline parallelism used to train the largest models. We add computation along the sequence-length dimension, where the extra computation is simply a longer input and stays compatible with standard large-model training. We propose Hidden Decoding, a sequence-length scaling method applied during continued pretraining (CPT). It expands each token into n streams with independent embedding tables and keeps the intermediate streams' key-value cache as context, so each token performs more internal computation without adding or widening Transformer layers. To keep this affordable at scale, we introduce Stream-Factorized Attention, in which most layers attend only within each stream and only a few layers mix across streams, reducing the attention cost from quadratic to roughly linear in n. Experiments support two scaling results. At frontier scale, we train WeLM-HD4-80B and WeLM-HD4-617B at n=4 and improve their matched non-HD baselines, making Hidden Decoding the first demonstrated sequence-length scaling method at the 100B+ MoE scale. Across expansion factors, the gains grow as n increases, showing that sequence-length expansion is a practical fixed-backbone scaling path for frontier-scale LLMs.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Hidden Decoding, a method to scale LLM capability by increasing effective computation per token via sequence-length expansion, without modifying the Transformer backbone, and demonstrates its effectiveness at 100B+ MoE scale.
AI.motivation: Scaling LLMs by enlarging the Transformer backbone is extremely costly because it requires full additional pretraining runs. The authors seek a cheaper scaling path where an already-strong model can be improved by allocating more computation per token without changing the backbone architecture and while staying compatible with standard large-scale training infrastructure (e.g., pipeline parallelism). Existing approaches like depth-recurrent/looped Transformers are difficult to scale due to incompatibility with such training schemes, motivating an alternative that adds computation along a different dimension.
AI.method: They introduce Hidden Decoding (HD), a sequence-length scaling technique used during continued pretraining. Each original token is expanded into n parallel streams, each with its own embedding table. During processing, the intermediate streams maintain their key-value (KV) caches as additional context, effectively increasing the internal computation per original token without adding layers or widening them. To keep the method computationally feasible, they propose Stream-Factorized Attention: most Transformer layers perform attention only within each stream, and only a subset of layers mix information across streams. This design reduces attention complexity with respect to the number of streams from quadratic to approximately linear in n.
AI.result: They train large Mixture-of-Experts models WeLM-HD4-80B and WeLM-HD4-617B with expansion factor n=4 and show that both outperform their corresponding non-HD baselines, demonstrating that Hidden Decoding works at frontier (100B+ MoE) scale. Furthermore, experiments across multiple expansion factors indicate that performance gains increase monotonically with n, confirming that more sequence-length expansion yields more benefit under a fixed backbone.
AI.conclusion: Hidden Decoding provides a practical scaling path for powerful existing LLM backbones by increasing per-token computation through sequence-length expansion rather than architectural growth. With Stream-Factorized Attention, the method is computationally efficient and compatible with standard large-scale training setups, and it has been empirically validated at 100B+ MoE scale. The observed improvement with larger expansion factors suggests that sequence-length scaling is a viable alternative to parameter scaling for advancing frontier LLMs.

title: Playing ZendoWorld: Challenging AI Agents on Active Visual Concept Induction
summary: A central challenge in building intelligent systems is enabling agents to jointly perceive complex inputs, form hypotheses about hidden patterns, and design informative experiments to test them. To study this problem, we propose ZendoWorld, a controlled interactive environment in which agents must infer a logical rule about visual game observations, acquire information by proposing new scenes, and refine their hypotheses based on feedback from the game environment. We evaluate several agents spanning pure VLM reasoning, Bayesian particle filtering, dynamic concept discovery, and neuro-symbolic methods. Our main findings are: (1) high accuracy in predicting labels for observed examples does not imply recovery of the underlying rule; (2) perception and induction are distinct bottlenecks for different agent classes; and (3) VLM-based agents propose near-uninformative experiments, failing to actively reduce hypothesis uncertainty. To compare these results, we collect human data on the task, which reveals a gap in inductive reasoning, particularly for more complex rules. Overall, ZENDOWORLD takes an important step toward evaluating intelligent agents and identifies concrete avenues for improvement, particularly in domains like scientific discovery.

AI.tldr: Introduces ZendoWorld, an environment to test how agents infer hidden logical rules from visual scenes by proposing experiments and refining hypotheses, and shows current AI systems lag behind humans in inductive reasoning and experiment design.
AI.motivation: To systematically study how agents can jointly perceive complex visual inputs, infer hidden logical rules, and actively design informative experiments—key ingredients of scientific discovery and intelligent behavior. Existing benchmarks mainly test passive prediction and do not sufficiently probe hypothesis formation, active information gathering, and rule recovery.
AI.method: The authors design ZendoWorld, an interactive visual game environment inspired by rule-induction tasks. Agents see labeled visual scenes governed by an unknown logical rule, must infer that rule, propose new scenes (experiments) to obtain labels, and iteratively update their hypotheses. They implement and evaluate multiple agent classes: pure vision-language model (VLM) reasoners, Bayesian particle-filter agents, dynamic concept-discovery models, and neuro-symbolic methods. They also collect human behavioral data on the same tasks for comparison, with particular focus on rule recovery and informativeness of proposed experiments.
AI.result: Across models, strong performance at labeling observed examples often does not translate into correctly inferring the underlying generative rule. Different agent families fail for different reasons: some are limited by perceptual representation, others by inductive reasoning over candidate rules. VLM-based agents, in particular, tend to propose experiments (new scenes) that are nearly uninformative, showing little capacity for active hypothesis disambiguation. Human participants significantly outperform these systems, especially when rules become more complex, highlighting a substantial gap in inductive reasoning and experiment design.
AI.conclusion: ZendoWorld provides a controlled, interactive testbed for studying integrated perception, induction, and active experiment design in artificial agents. The study shows that current methods, including powerful VLMs, struggle to recover underlying rules and to plan informative experiments, and that humans remain much stronger at these aspects of reasoning. This benchmark thus exposes concrete shortcomings of present AI systems and suggests directions for advancement toward more scientifically capable, hypothesis-driven agents.

title: Diarization-Guided Qwen-ASR Adaptation for Multilingual Two-Speaker Conversational Speech
comment: 4 main pages plus 1 page of reference
summary: This paper describes our self-designed system for Task 1 of the MLC-SLM 2026 Challenge for multilingual two-speaker conversational speech. The system combines a modular speaker diarization front end with a challenge-adapted Qwen3-ASR-1.7B recognizer. The diarization front end performs voice activity detection, subsegment generation, CAMPPlus speaker embedding extraction, two-speaker spectral clustering, and RTTM-based audio segmentation. The resulting speaker-attributed segments are grouped by language or region and decoded by the adapted ASR model. For ASR adaptation, we first perform supervised full fine-tuning on the official training data, then apply LoRA fine-tuning with synthetic speech generated by a three-pipeline TTS-based synthetic speech augmentation framework, and finally refine the model using GRPO reinforcement learning with rewards based on WER/CER and penalties for hallucination, repetition, and length deviation. On the official development set, the full system achieves an average tcpMER of 23.70, reducing the error rate by 6.83 absolute points relative to the released Qwen-ASR-1.7B performance. On the final evaluation set, the system achieves an average tcpMER of 17.97. Ablation results show that supervised fine-tuning provides the largest gain, while synthetic-speech LoRA adaptation and reinforcement learning further improve robustness.

AI.tldr: They build a multilingual two-speaker conversation system that combines a diarization front end with an adapted Qwen3-ASR-1.7B recognizer, using supervised fine-tuning, synthetic-speech LoRA, and RL to significantly reduce tcpMER on the MLC-SLM 2026 Challenge.
AI.motivation: Existing ASR systems struggle with multilingual, two-speaker conversational speech, especially in challenge conditions, and off-the-shelf models like Qwen-ASR-1.7B underperform without task-specific adaptation. The authors aim to design a system tailored to the MLC-SLM 2026 Challenge that better handles diarization, language variation, and robustness issues, and to systematically leverage supervised data, synthetic augmentation, and reinforcement learning to improve recognition accuracy and robustness.
AI.method: They build a modular pipeline. First, a diarization front end performs voice activity detection, creates subsegments, extracts CAMPPlus speaker embeddings, applies two-speaker spectral clustering, and outputs RTTM-based segments with speaker labels. These segments are grouped by language/region. For ASR, they adapt Qwen3-ASR-1.7B in three stages: (1) supervised full fine-tuning on official training data; (2) LoRA-based fine-tuning using synthetic speech created by a three-pipeline TTS augmentation framework; (3) GRPO reinforcement learning where rewards are based on WER/CER and penalties target hallucinations, repetitions, and length mismatch. The final ASR decodes the diarized, language-grouped segments.
AI.result: On the official development set, their system reaches an average tcpMER of 23.70, which is a 6.83-point absolute reduction compared to the baseline Qwen-ASR-1.7B. On the final evaluation set, it achieves an average tcpMER of 17.97. Ablation experiments indicate that supervised fine-tuning yields the largest performance gain, while synthetic-speech LoRA adaptation and RL-based refinement bring additional robustness improvements.
AI.conclusion: A modular combination of strong speaker diarization with a carefully adapted Qwen3-ASR-1.7B model substantially improves performance on multilingual two-speaker conversational speech in the MLC-SLM 2026 Challenge. Supervised fine-tuning is the most impactful step, but synthetic-speech-based LoRA adaptation and RL with task-specific rewards and penalties further enhance robustness and lower error rates, demonstrating the effectiveness of this multi-stage adaptation strategy.

title: AutoPersonas: A Multi-Timescale Loop Engine for Open-Ended Persona Evolution
comment: 52 pages, 13 figures/tables, ancillary public-safe evaluation artifacts included
summary: Long-term persona agents must remain identifiable while adapting to new events, relationships, evidence, and social conditions. We identify self-locking as a runtime failure mode in continuing persona-life loops: locally plausible events keep appearing while the generated life collapses toward familiar environments, weak relationships, suspended decisions, and stale life stages. We trace this failure to model-level convergence toward high-probability behavioral channels and system-level context gravity from State, memory, history, and environment summaries. We introduce AutoPersonas, a multi-timescale life-environment engine for bounded persona-level recursive self-evolution. It separates environment-side Occurrences, accumulated Observations, and persona State. Its OSO loop admits divergent future-facing material while requiring evidence-governed absorption before State or reachability changes. A three-year compressed simulation exposed environment watermark shells, occurrence-hardening gaps, slow-change accumulation failures, recursive indecision, and weak relationship persistence. An eight-model 40-day stress test generated 1,600 events and found mean rolling 5-day action-category repetition of 95.2%-97.6%, with all models crossing 90% by day 11. Semantic re-keeping found 79.0%-88.0% macro-theme repetition across all direct-loop runs. In a same-runtime 40-day A/B, context-slice masking plus per-sample divergence targeting reduced macro-theme repetition from 61.8% to 36.3% and roughly doubled cumulative theme count. A juvenile-goblin fictional-world run reproduced the anti-fixation regime without hard real-world intrusions. These results support a bounded claim: separating controlled divergence from evidence-governed absorption can reduce persona-environment self-locking while preserving identity continuity.

AI.tldr: The paper studies why long-term simulated personas get stuck in repetitive, self-locking life patterns and proposes a new architecture, AutoPersonas, to reduce this while keeping the persona’s identity consistent.
AI.motivation: Long-running persona agents (e.g., characters or AI personas living over months/years of simulated time) tend to converge into repetitive, familiar life loops: the same locations, weak relationships, indecision, and stagnant life stages. This self-locking hurts realism, diversity, and usefulness of such agents. The authors want to understand this failure mode analytically and experimentally, and design a system that keeps personas both coherent and dynamically evolving.
AI.method: They analyze self-locking as arising from two sources: (1) model-level convergence towards high-probability behaviors and (2) system-level “context gravity” from state, memory, and environment summaries that keep dragging the persona back to familiar patterns. They then propose AutoPersonas, a multi-timescale simulation engine that cleanly separates environment Occurrences, the Observations accumulated by the persona, and the persona’s internal State. Their OSO (Occurrences–Observations–State) loop allows the system to generate diverse, future-facing possibilities but only incorporates them into long-term State when supported by evidence. They run compressed multi-year simulations, stress tests with multiple models over 40-day spans, A/B tests with and without context-slice masking plus per-sample divergence targeting, and a fictional-world run to see if the behavior generalizes beyond realistic settings.
AI.result: The simulations reveal several concrete failure patterns in naive setups: environment watermark shells (outer bounds on how far the environment changes), occurrence-hardening gaps (events that don’t solidify into state), accumulation failures for slow changes, recursive indecision, and persistence of weak relationships. Quantitatively, in an 8-model, 40-day stress test with 1,600 events, they observe very high action-category repetition (95.2%–97.6% 5-day rolling repetition; all models exceed 90% by day 11), and high macro-theme repetition (79.0%–88.0%) in direct-loop runs. When they apply context-slice masking plus divergence targeting, macro-theme repetition drops from 61.8% to 36.3% and cumulative unique themes roughly double. A fictional “juvenile goblin” world shows that the improved, anti-fixation regime works even without real-world intrusions.
AI.conclusion: The paper argues that long-term persona self-locking is a fundamental interaction between model biases and system design. By structurally separating the generation of divergent possibilities from the slower, evidence-governed update of persona State—operationalized in the AutoPersonas OSO loop—systems can significantly reduce repetitive self-locking while maintaining identity continuity. The claim is deliberately bounded: it does not solve all long-term coherence problems, but demonstrates that careful architecture can mitigate fixation and encourage richer, more varied persona lives.

title: Best-of-$N$ TTS Evaluation is Confounded by ASR Family Alignment
comment: Accepted at ICML 2026 Workshop on Machine Learning for Audio
summary: Best-of-$N$ (BoN) inference improves content consistency in zero-shot text-to-speech by selecting from $N$ candidates with an automatic speech recognition (ASR) verifier. We identify an underexplored evaluation confound: a verifier's apparent quality depends strongly on which ASR family judges it. On LibriSpeech-PC test-clean~\citep{librispeechpc} with F5-TTS~\citep{f5tts}, verifier rankings reverse across Whisper, wav2vec~2.0, and HuBERT evaluators, and same-family verifier-evaluator pairs recover 2-3$\times$ more oracle headroom than cross-family pairs despite near-identical representations (linear CKA $0.978$) -- a pattern consistent with identity- or lineage-level coupling rather than representational overlap. We propose two \textbf{cross-family rank ensembles} (rank-averaging and conjunctive max-rank) that attain the lowest mean WER across three independent evaluators -- $1.61\%$ at $N{=}10$ ($-12\%$ relative to F5-TTS) -- with no measurable degradation under automatic SIM-o/UTMOS metrics; the best single verifier drives WER from $2.06\%$ to $1.72\%$ ($-16.5\%$) under the official F5-TTS evaluator. We recommend cross-evaluator triangulation as default reporting practice.

AI.tldr: The paper studies Best-of-N inference for zero-shot text-to-speech, showing that evaluation using ASR-based verifiers is confounded by which ASR model family is used, and proposes cross-family rank ensemble methods that improve robustness and WER without hurting perceptual quality.
AI.motivation: Best-of-N inference can improve text-to-speech content consistency, but its gains and the perceived quality of verifiers are typically assessed with ASR-based evaluators that may be biased toward their own model family. This undermines fair comparison and reliable measurement of progress. The authors aim to understand and mitigate this evaluation confound.
AI.method: They examine verifier performance and rankings across different ASR model families (Whisper, wav2vec 2.0, HuBERT) on LibriSpeech-PC using F5-TTS, analyze representation similarity (e.g., with linear CKA), and look for patterns indicating coupling between verifiers and evaluators. Based on these findings, they propose cross-family rank ensemble strategies—rank-averaging and conjunctive max-rank—to combine signals from multiple ASR families when selecting the best TTS candidate in Best-of-N inference.
AI.result: Verifier rankings change drastically depending on which ASR family is used as the evaluator, and verifiers evaluated by same-family ASR systems recover 2–3× more oracle headroom than cross-family pairs despite highly similar representations. Their proposed cross-family rank ensemble methods achieve the lowest mean WER (1.61% at N=10, a 12% relative improvement over F5-TTS baseline) across three independent evaluators, without measurable degradation in automatic similarity and quality metrics (SIM-o/UTMOS). The strongest single verifier reduces WER from 2.06% to 1.72% (16.5% relative) under the official F5-TTS evaluator.
AI.conclusion: ASR-based evaluation of Best-of-N TTS verifiers is confounded by strong family or lineage coupling between verifiers and evaluators, not just by representational similarity. To obtain more reliable and generalizable assessments and improvements, TTS research should use cross-family rank ensembles and adopt cross-evaluator triangulation as a standard reporting practice.

title: Compete Then Collaborate: Frontier AI Teachers Build a Verifiable Curriculum to Improve a Coding Student Beyond Imitation
summary: Large language models increasingly serve as teachers generating training data for smaller students. Prior multi-teacher knowledge distillation methods merge outputs without determining which frontier model teaches best, often relying on an LLM judge biased toward its own outputs. We introduce a compete-then-collaborate framework where four frontier AI teachers (Claude, Codex-GPT, Grok, Gemini) are ranked head-to-head by an execution-based judge (unit tests and stdin-stdout checks) with fairness controls, and then collaborate to build a verifiable curriculum for a student (Qwen2.5-Coder). We report three findings. (1) Under execution verification, all teachers solve standard problems near-perfectly after self-correction (99-100%) due to a saturation effect, but harder competition problems separate them (Gemini 77% > Claude 69% = Codex 69% > Grok 50%); however, the robust student-side results do not depend on teacher ranking. (2) Imitation (SFT) on verified solutions does not improve, and can degrade, an already-competent student at 7B and 32B (e.g., from 76.7% to 72.7% on MBPP-test, and 5.9% to 2.9% on competition problems). (3) Using the same collaborative curriculum as a reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) environment improves the student (from 5.9% to 8.8% peak on competition problems, a +49% relative gain), reversing SFT's direction. The value of AI-teacher collaboration lies not in pooling answers to imitate, but in jointly constructing a verifiable environment where the student learns by doing. We release a reproducible on-prem pipeline (NVIDIA GB10) with framework patches for running GRPO on a bleeding-edge stack.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how to best use multiple frontier large language models as teachers for a smaller coding model, finding that simply imitating verified teacher solutions can hurt a strong student, while using the same teacher-generated tasks as a reinforcement learning environment with verifiable rewards improves the student substantially.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are increasingly trained with data generated by larger frontier models, existing multi-teacher distillation methods often merge teacher outputs without carefully deciding which teacher is best and rely on judge LLMs that may be biased toward themselves. There is a need to understand how to fairly compare multiple strong teachers, how much their ranking matters for student learning, and whether supervised imitation of their outputs is actually beneficial for already-competent students.
AI.method: The authors propose a "compete-then-collaborate" framework where four frontier AI coding teachers (Claude, Codex-GPT, Grok, Gemini) are compared head-to-head using an execution-based judge (unit tests and stdin-stdout checks) with fairness controls. After ranking teachers on both standard and harder competition-style coding problems, they aggregate teacher-generated, execution-verified solutions into a collaborative curriculum for fine-tuning a student model (Qwen2.5-Coder). They evaluate two training paradigms on this curriculum: supervised imitation learning (SFT) on verified teacher solutions and reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR, similar to GRPO), where the student learns by interacting with the verifiable environment. Performance is measured on standard benchmarks like MBPP-test and a set of harder competition problems.
AI.result: (1) Under execution-based verification, all frontier teachers achieve near-perfect performance (99–100%) on standard coding problems after self-correction, showing a saturation effect, while harder competition problems differentiate them (Gemini 77% > Claude 69% = Codex 69% > Grok 50%). However, downstream student performance is largely insensitive to these teacher rankings. (2) Supervised imitation (SFT) on execution-verified teacher solutions not only fails to improve but can degrade the performance of an already-competent student model at both 7B and 32B scales, notably reducing accuracy from 76.7% to 72.7% on MBPP-test and from 5.9% to 2.9% on competition problems. (3) Using the same collaborative, verified curriculum as an RLVR environment yields improved student performance on the hardest competition problems, from 5.9% to 8.8% peak accuracy (a 49% relative gain), effectively reversing the negative effect seen with SFT.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that the primary value of multiple AI teachers is not in pooling their final answers for the student to imitate, especially when the student is already strong, but in using the teachers to construct a verifiable problem-solving environment where the student learns by doing via reinforcement learning. Teacher ranking under execution-based evaluation has limited impact on student outcomes, and supervised fine-tuning on teacher solutions can be harmful, whereas reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards on the same tasks yields meaningful gains. The authors provide an on-prem, reproducible RLVR training pipeline on NVIDIA GB10 hardware with necessary framework patches for running GRPO on a modern software stack.

title: XALPHA: A Memory-Driven AI Quant Researcher for Hypothesis-to-Code Alpha Discovery
summary: Financial markets are noisy, non-stationary, and high-dimensional, making it difficult to discover predictive and robust trading signals. Alpha discovery has evolved from manual factor design to machine learning, evolutionary search, and recent LLM-based frameworks, improving the efficiency of factor generation, search, and evaluation. However, existing methods still mostly automate isolated steps, rather than functioning as end-to-end quant researchers that can absorb external knowledge, close the hypothesis-to-code validation loop, and learn from accumulated discovery feedback. To fill this gap, we introduce XAlpha, a memory-driven AI Quant Researcher for continuous hypothesis-to-code alpha discovery. XAlpha maintains a multi-source research memory system that integrates report-grounded financial knowledge with discovery feedback from prior generations and research cycles. Guided by this memory system, a Macro Brain plans research themes and selects suitable Archetypes; a Micro Brain transforms the planned hypothesis pool into executable factor code and verifies ex-ante tri-alignment among the hypothesis idea, code logic, and financial plausibility; and a Cross Brain consolidates empirical outcomes into generation-level feedback, cycle-level summaries, and archetype-level research cues for future exploration. In this way, XAlpha turns alpha mining from isolated factor generation into a closed-loop research process that continuously reads, hypothesizes, implements, validates, reflects, and evolves. Experiments on CSI300 show that XAlpha achieves stronger overall alpha discovery performance than representative baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes XAlpha, an AI-driven, memory-based, end-to-end quant research framework that continuously discovers and refines trading alphas, outperforming existing methods on CSI300.
AI.motivation: Traditional and even modern ML-based alpha discovery approaches handle only parts of the quant research workflow (e.g., factor generation or backtesting) and lack an integrated, feedback-driven process that mimics how a human quant researcher reads research, forms hypotheses, writes code, evaluates results, and learns from history. The authors aim to build an automated system that closes this loop and can leverage external financial knowledge and past discovery outcomes to improve future alpha discovery.
AI.method: XAlpha is designed as a memory-driven multi-brain architecture. It uses a multi-source research memory to store and integrate external financial reports/knowledge with feedback from previous alpha discovery cycles. A Macro Brain plans high-level research themes and chooses appropriate archetypes (types of alpha ideas). A Micro Brain turns hypotheses into executable factor code and checks tri-alignment among the natural-language hypothesis, code implementation, and financial plausibility before running tests. A Cross Brain aggregates empirical results into structured feedback at different levels (single generation, research cycle, and archetype) and writes this back into memory, guiding future exploration and refinement.
AI.result: On empirical tests using CSI300 data, XAlpha produces more powerful and robust alphas than representative baseline methods, indicating that the closed-loop, memory-based, multi-brain design yields superior alpha discovery performance.
AI.conclusion: A closed-loop, memory-augmented AI Quant Researcher like XAlpha can transform alpha mining from a set of disjoint automated steps into a continuous research process that reads external information, formulates and codes hypotheses, evaluates them, and learns over time. This end-to-end, feedback-driven architecture leads to better alpha discovery than existing approaches, suggesting a promising direction for automating quantitative research workflows.

title: MentalHospital: A Virtual Environment for Evaluating Psychiatric Clinical Encounters
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have shown strong performance on isolated psychiatric tasks, including dialogue, diagnosis, and treatment planning, yet existing benchmarks rarely simulate complete psychiatric clinical encounters. We introduce $\textbf{MentalHospital}$, a virtual evaluation environment for LLM-based psychiatric clinical encounters. MentalHospital instantiates the Subjective Interviewing, Objective Examination, Diagnostic Assessment, and Treatment Planning (S.O.A.P.) workflow, using skill-augmented standardized patients constructed from 1,193 de-identified psychiatric electronic health record (EHR) cases spanning all major ICD-11 categories and 76 disorders. Each encounter is assessed through a dual-track protocol that combines objective comparison against EHR-derived references with subjective assessment of clinical process quality. To scale specialist judgment, we develop $\textbf{MentalEval}$, five domain-specific evaluators covering communication empathy, interviewing professionalism, clinical-note quality, diagnostic rigor, and treatment appropriateness, trained with rubric-grounded SFT and expert-guided DPO. Survey responses from 22 clinicians support MentalHospital's clinical fidelity (3.88/5), while MentalEval achieves strong expert alignment with an average QWK of 0.944. Benchmarking shows that even the strongest LLM trails clinicians by 37.28 percentage points in objective psychiatric competence, with mental status assessment as a key bottleneck.

AI.tldr: They build a realistic virtual benchmark, MentalHospital, to evaluate how well large language models handle full psychiatric clinical encounters, plus MentalEval, a set of automated expert-like evaluators; results show large gaps between LLMs and clinicians, especially in mental status assessment.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM psychiatric benchmarks focus on narrow, isolated skills (e.g., diagnosis from short vignettes, single-step treatment suggestions, or simple counseling dialogues) and fail to capture full, realistic psychiatric visits that require multi-step interviewing, assessment, and planning. There is also a lack of scalable, domain-specific evaluation tools that can reliably judge nuanced clinical qualities like empathy, professionalism, diagnostic rigor, and treatment quality. The paper aims to create a more faithful, end-to-end testbed and automated evaluators to measure and improve LLMs as potential aids in psychiatric care.
AI.method: They construct MentalHospital, a virtual environment that simulates end-to-end psychiatric encounters by implementing the S.O.A.P. (Subjective, Objective, Assessment, Plan) workflow. Standardized “patients” are generated from 1,193 de-identified psychiatric EHR cases covering all major ICD-11 categories and 76 disorders, and are enriched with skills to interact with LLM clinicians. Each encounter is evaluated via a dual-track protocol: (1) objective scoring against structured references derived from the source EHRs, and (2) subjective scoring of clinical process quality. To scale expert evaluation, they build MentalEval, a suite of five specialized LLM-based evaluators, each trained with rubric-grounded supervised fine-tuning and expert-guided DPO to rate communication empathy, interviewing professionalism, clinical-note quality, diagnostic rigor, and treatment appropriateness. They also collect clinician surveys to assess the realism of the environment and compare LLM performance to human clinicians on the benchmark.
AI.result: Clinician survey responses from 22 psychiatric professionals rate the clinical fidelity of MentalHospital at 3.88 out of 5, indicating the simulated encounters are reasonably realistic. MentalEval shows high agreement with human experts, with an average quadratic weighted kappa of 0.944 across the five evaluators, suggesting reliable automated scoring. Benchmark experiments reveal a substantial performance gap: even the best-performing LLM lags human clinicians by 37.28 percentage points in objective psychiatric competence metrics, and detailed analysis identifies mental status examination as a major area where LLMs struggle.
AI.conclusion: MentalHospital provides a more realistic and comprehensive benchmark for evaluating LLMs in psychiatric settings by simulating complete clinical encounters, and MentalEval offers scalable, expert-aligned automated evaluation across key clinical dimensions. Despite strong general capabilities, current LLMs remain far from clinician-level psychiatric competence, particularly in conducting and interpreting mental status examinations. These resources can guide future LLM development and safety assessments in mental health applications, highlighting where improvements are most needed before deployment in real-world clinical care.

title: TypeProbe: Recovering Type Representations from Hidden States of Pre-trained Code Models
comment: 18 pages, 12 figures. Accepted at ESSLLI 2026 (StuS; double-blind)
summary: State-of-the-art code models achieve impressive performance, yet the extent to which they internally encode type information remains poorly understood. We probe the residual streams of pretrained code models for internal type representations using a parallel dataset of Java and Python code examples. Our results show that cross-lingual type representations emerge even from untyped code. Moreover, we test whether hidden states linearly encode the result type implied by typed function application by training probes on one language to infer argument and result types in the other. Finally, we find that this structure is partly robust to lexical perturbations and cross-language syntactic variations. To the best of our knowledge, prior work on interpretability of code models has not directly targeted formal type semantics or cross-lingual type representations. We release our code and datasets.

AI.tldr: The paper probes whether pretrained code models internally learn and linearly encode cross-lingual type information, even when trained on untyped code.
AI.motivation: Although large code models perform very well, it is unclear if and how they represent formal type information, especially across different programming languages and in the absence of explicit type annotations. Understanding this can advance interpretability and our knowledge of what these models learn.
AI.method: The authors use a parallel Java–Python dataset and probe residual stream activations of pretrained code models. They train linear probes to recover argument and result types from hidden states, including across languages (training on one, testing on the other). They also apply lexical perturbations and vary cross-language syntax to test robustness of the learned type structure.
AI.result: They find that cross-lingual type representations emerge in model activations even from untyped code, and that result types implied by function applications are linearly decodable across languages. These representations show partial robustness to changes in identifiers and syntactic differences between Java and Python.
AI.conclusion: Pretrained code models internally capture formal type semantics and cross-lingual type structure in a largely linear, robust manner, despite limited or no explicit type supervision. This reveals a previously unexplored aspect of code model interpretability, and the authors provide public code and datasets to support further research.

title: Different Teachers, Different Capabilities: Sub-1B On-Device Distillation for Structured Text Enrichment
comment: 12 pages, 5 figures. has a same-size non-reasoning-teacher control, a three-judge LLM-as-a-judge panel with a negative control, full-source faithfulness grading, and a per-field routing analysis
summary: High-volume structured extraction pays a large model's latency on every item, so distilling the task into a small on-device model is attractive: comparable output at a fraction of the time and cost. We measure what that distillation actually delivers, per sub-task. Each news article is mapped to one JSON object with a short summary and five categorical labels. We distill an 8B reasoning teacher (deepseek-r1:8b) into a 0.6B student (Qwen3-0.6B; QLoRA, three seeds), and add two teacher controls: a same-size non-reasoning teacher and a larger managed pipeline. A blinded, reference-free, three-judge panel scores every arm against the full article, alongside two non-distillation baselines, few-shot prompting and constrained decoding. The student runs at about 0.8 s per article against the teacher's 39 s, and recovers 58% of the base-to-teacher gap on summary quality, beating its primary baseline (constrained decoding) by +16.8 points and few-shot prompting by a secondary +4.9. A same-size non-reasoning teacher trains a student no better than the untuned base, so the summary gain follows from the teacher's reasoning nature rather than its scale. Capabilities then split by teacher: the reasoning teacher transfers writing quality and the managed pipeline transfers label diversity, while a same-size instruction teacher's students stay more grounded on the 22 short, thin-source articles in the 93-item test set (74 versus 55 faithful), where the reasoning-lineage student fabricates. That grounding difference is a consistent ordering rather than a significant aggregate effect, and the subgroup is small, so we report it as a direction. Because no single engine wins every field, the deliverable is a per-field routing map for on-device enrichment.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how well a tiny on‑device model can be distilled from a larger reasoning model for structured news article extraction, showing large latency gains and partial recovery of teacher quality, with different teachers transferring different strengths and motivating per-field routing.
AI.motivation: Running a large model for every document in high-volume structured extraction is too slow and costly, so there is interest in distilling its capabilities into a much smaller, on-device model. However, it is unclear how much of the teacher’s performance, and which specific capabilities, actually survive distillation, especially when comparing reasoning vs. non-reasoning teachers and other practical baselines like few-shot prompting or constrained decoding.
AI.method: The authors define a structured extraction task where each news article is converted into a JSON object containing a short summary and five categorical labels. They distill an 8B reasoning model (deepseek-r1:8b) into a 0.6B Qwen3-0.6B student using QLoRA with three random seeds. They also add two teacher baselines: a same-size non-reasoning teacher and a larger managed pipeline system, plus two non-distillation baselines (few-shot prompting and constrained decoding). A blinded, reference-free, three-judge panel evaluates every system’s outputs directly against the full source article, scoring both summaries and labels over a 93-item test set, with focused analysis on 22 short, low-information articles.
AI.result: The distilled 0.6B student runs in about 0.8 seconds per article compared with 39 seconds for the 8B teacher, achieving major latency reductions. In summary quality, the student closes 58% of the performance gap between the base model and the reasoning teacher, outperforming constrained decoding by +16.8 points and few-shot prompting by +4.9 points. Distillation from a non-reasoning teacher of the same size yields a student that is no better than the untuned base, indicating that gains stem from the reasoning nature of the teacher rather than its scale. Different teachers transfer different capabilities: the reasoning teacher mainly improves writing quality, while the managed pipeline teacher enhances label diversity. A same-size instruction-following teacher produces students that are more faithful on short, thin-source articles (74 vs. 55 faithful outputs), whereas the reasoning-lineage student tends to hallucinate; this is observed as a directional pattern rather than a statistically strong aggregate effect.
AI.conclusion: Distilling from a reasoning teacher into a small on-device student delivers substantial latency savings while recovering a meaningful fraction of the teacher’s quality, especially for summary writing, and clearly outperforms standard prompting and constrained decoding baselines. The benefits come from the teacher’s reasoning behavior more than from sheer size, and different teacher types transmit different strengths (writing quality vs. label diversity vs. grounding). Since no single model dominates across all output fields, the authors recommend routing different parts of the structured extraction task to different distilled or teacher models, yielding a per-field routing map for practical on-device enrichment pipelines.

title: Grounded Event Extraction from SEC 8-K Filings with a Fine-Grained Taxonomy
comment: 9 pages, 8 figures, 1 table. Full dataset and taxonomy available at https://massive.com/docs/rest/stocks/filings/8-k-disclosures?utm_source=research&utm_campaign=8k_tags
summary: Form 8-K filings are the primary channel through which U.S. public companies disclose material events, but the SEC item codes attached to them are coarse: a single item spans routine administrative changes and chief executive departures, and many of the most market-moving disclosures fall into a catch-all item. Large language models make fine-grained labelling feasible at corpus scale, but only if the labels can be traced to the source text and shown to be reliable. We present a two-stage system that tags 8-K disclosures against a three-tier taxonomy of 119 event types. The first stage constrains output to valid taxonomy entries and anchors every tag to a verbatim quote via fuzzy n-gram validation; the second re-grades each cited quote against the category definition to produce a quality score. Applying the system to 292,984 filings from 2022 to 2026 yields 601,088 grounded event tags, which we release. Over 5,125 stratified tags, an LLM judge finds precision rises monotonically with the quality score, from 12% to 96%, while unsupported tags fall from 8% to near zero. Ablation shows the score is calibrated only when assigned in a dedicated second pass. An event study on unsigned abnormal returns confirms, without any language model, that the taxonomy separates economically distinct events sharing an item code.

AI.tldr: They build a reliable, fine-grained LLM-based labeling system for SEC Form 8-K events and validate that it produces economically meaningful event categories.
AI.motivation: SEC Form 8-K item codes are too coarse to distinguish between very different types of corporate events, which hinders research and practice; LLMs could help with fine-grained labeling but need traceability and reliability to be trusted at scale.
AI.method: They design a two-stage LLM system: Stage 1 assigns one or more of 119 event types from a structured taxonomy to each 8-K and anchors each tag to a specific quoted text span using fuzzy n-gram validation; Stage 2 re-evaluates each tagged quote against the event definition to assign a granular quality score. They run ablations (e.g., single-pass vs two-pass scoring) and use an LLM judge and event-study regressions on abnormal stock returns for validation.
AI.result: On 292,984 filings (2022–2026) the system produces 601,088 grounded event tags made publicly available. In a stratified evaluation of 5,125 tags, precision increases monotonically with the quality score (from 12% to 96%), unsupported tags nearly vanish at high scores, and ablations show that accurate calibration of the quality score requires the dedicated second pass. Event studies on stock returns show that categories within the same coarse SEC item code correspond to economically distinct market reactions.
AI.conclusion: A carefully designed, two-stage LLM pipeline can produce transparent, high-precision, fine-grained labels for corporate disclosure events, with calibrated quality scores and demonstrated economic relevance. This enables more nuanced research on 8-K disclosures and illustrates a general approach for trustworthy LLM-based text labeling at scale.

title: PolyUQuest: Verifiable Structure-Aware Web RAG over Heterogeneous Graphs
summary: Existing retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems treat web pages as flat text, losing the structural and semantic signals encoded in HTML. We present PolyUQuest, a verifiable, structure-aware web RAG framework built on a heterogeneous graph that unifies hyperlink topology between pages, DOM hierarchy within pages, and entity-relation knowledge across pages. A two-tier router dispatches each query to one of three retrieval modes matched to its structural need, including direct block retrieval, cross-page graph traversal, and multi-hop entity reasoning. Every answer is fully verifiable, as each cited block carries its source page, heading path, and entity links so that users can trace any claim back to its structural evidence. We evaluate on the official websites of the Hong Kong Polytechnic University (PolyU), comprising 4,240 pages, 31,086 DOM blocks, 29,119 entities, and 37,680 relations, together with a multi-type evaluation benchmark. PolyUQuest outperforms existing RAG systems in answer correctness, coverage, and faithfulness, while consuming significantly fewer LLM tokens per query. The demonstration provides an interactive interface for inspecting cited answers, comparing retrieval traces across routing modes, and exploring evidence graph paths. PolyUQuest is being prepared for deployment as a student-facing QA service at PolyU.

AI.tldr: PolyUQuest is a structure-aware, verifiable web RAG framework that models websites as heterogeneous graphs combining hyperlinks, DOM hierarchy, and entity relations to improve retrieval, reasoning, and faithfulness while reducing token usage.
AI.motivation: Conventional RAG systems flatten web pages into plain text, ignoring valuable structural and semantic information encoded in HTML such as page hierarchy, sectioning, and entity links. This leads to weaker retrieval, poorer reasoning over multi-page information, and less transparent, harder-to-verify answers. There is a need for a web QA system that leverages the rich structure of websites to improve answer quality, efficiency, and verifiability, especially in real-world institutional settings like university sites.
AI.method: The authors build a heterogeneous graph over a university website that integrates three kinds of structure: (1) hyperlink topology connecting pages, (2) DOM hierarchy capturing within-page sections and blocks, and (3) entity-relation links across pages. On top of this graph, they design a two-tier routing mechanism that chooses among three retrieval modes per query: direct retrieval of relevant DOM blocks, graph traversal across linked pages, or multi-hop reasoning over entities and relations. Retrieved evidence blocks carry detailed structural context: source URL, heading path in the DOM, and linked entities, enabling fully verifiable answers. The system is implemented as PolyUQuest with an interactive demo interface.
AI.result: On a dataset built from PolyU’s official website (4,240 pages, 31,086 DOM blocks, 29,119 entities, 37,680 relations) and a multi-type evaluation benchmark, PolyUQuest surpasses baseline RAG systems in answer correctness, coverage, and faithfulness. It also achieves these gains with substantially fewer LLM tokens per query, indicating higher efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Modeling websites as heterogeneous structure-aware graphs and routing queries to structure-matched retrieval modes improves the quality, coverage, and verifiability of web QA, while reducing token usage.
