PolyUQuest demonstrates that such an approach can be practically deployed for student-facing QA on a large university website, with an interface that makes evidence and reasoning paths transparent to users.

title: Echoes Across Vietnam's Highlands, Delta, and Coast: A Multilingual Corpus for Cham, Khmer, and Tay-Nung
summary: Vietnam's ethnic minority languages are almost absent from the field of Natural Language Processing (NLP), and the challenge goes beyond data scarcity: Cham, Khmer, and Tay-Nung differ sharply in script, Vietnamese contact, and standardization, conditions under which standard multilingual adaptation can learn the wrong signals. We introduce CKTN, the first corpus and benchmark for these languages (44,367 documents, 24M subword tokens), spanning continued pretraining, category classification, and summary-document retrieval. We show that existing multilingual encoders severely fragment these languages, and that common adaptation metrics can mislead: models may lower language-modeling loss or excel at lexical-overlap retrieval while still failing at semantic generalization across documents. We address this with a script-aware adaptation recipe - vocabulary augmentation combined with calibrated replaced-token pretraining - that prevents the discriminator from exploiting trivial script mismatches. The result is an encoder with substantially less fragmentation and the strongest classification performance among evaluated models, exposing the limits of lexical-overlap retrieval as an evaluation signal.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CKTN, the first NLP corpus and benchmark for three low-resource Vietnamese ethnic minority languages (Cham, Khmer, Tay-Nung), and proposes a script-aware adaptation method that improves representation quality and downstream performance over existing multilingual encoders.
AI.motivation: Ethnic minority languages in Vietnam (Cham, Khmer, Tay-Nung) are virtually absent from NLP research. Beyond limited data, they differ greatly in script, degree of contact with Vietnamese, and level of standardization. Under these conditions, off-the-shelf multilingual models can learn misleading or spurious patterns (e.g., driven by script or surface overlap), and existing adaptation and evaluation metrics may suggest improvement while models still fail to generalize semantically. A dedicated corpus and more appropriate adaptation strategies are needed to support these languages fairly and effectively.
AI.method: The authors build CKTN, a corpus of 44,367 documents (24M subword tokens) for Cham, Khmer, and Tay-Nung, and define tasks spanning: (1) continued pretraining, (2) category classification, and (3) summary–document retrieval. They then analyze how current multilingual encoders represent these languages, showing severe fragmentation (poor cross-document semantic coherence). They further examine standard adaptation metrics such as language modeling loss and lexical-overlap-based retrieval performance, demonstrating that these can improve even when semantic generalization does not. To address this, they propose a script-aware adaptation recipe combining vocabulary augmentation for the target scripts with calibrated replaced-token pretraining, designed so the discriminator cannot rely on trivial script mismatches and must instead learn more robust semantic features.
AI.result: Empirical results show that popular multilingual encoders fragment representations of Cham, Khmer, and Tay-Nung and can appear to adapt successfully by conventional metrics while still failing on semantic tasks. The proposed script-aware adaptation with vocabulary augmentation and calibrated replaced-token pretraining yields an encoder with notably reduced representation fragmentation and the best classification performance among all evaluated models on the CKTN benchmark. The experiments also reveal that lexical-overlap-based retrieval is a weak and potentially misleading signal of true semantic capability in these settings.
AI.conclusion: CKTN provides the first substantial corpus and benchmark for three Vietnamese ethnic minority languages and exposes limitations of standard multilingual adaptation and evaluation practices under conditions of script mismatch and low standardization. Script-aware adaptation—via targeted vocabulary expansion and carefully calibrated replaced-token pretraining—produces more coherent representations and stronger downstream performance. The work underscores that improvements in language modeling loss or lexical-overlap retrieval do not guarantee semantic generalization, and that evaluation protocols and adaptation techniques must be reconsidered for low-resource, script-diverse languages.

title: Understanding Axes of Difficulty For Long Context Tasks Via PredicateLongBench
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated rapidly improving long-context capabilities, prompting a wave of benchmarks designed to evaluate them. However, existing long-context evaluations - from Needle-in-a-Haystack (NIAH) tests to more recent multi-hop reasoning and summarization tasks - predominantly measure average-case performance, and many are either saturated or lack robustness. Notably absent is a systematic way to probe how models perform as we scale up the difficulty of tasks along various axes. We address this gap by proposing PredicateLongBench, a benchmark that stress-tests long-context reasoning by asking models to identify the longest contiguous subsequence of words in a long input that satisfies given predicates/constraints (e.g., lexicographic ordering), drawn from a broader predicate class. The central innovation of our benchmark is the identification and systematic exploration of multiple different axes of difficulty which test multiple aspects of long context understanding. We provide two complementary generation pipelines - a fully synthetic setup using random word-like strings, and a real-world setup that samples words from natural documents while preserving their distributional properties. We find that frontier models struggle to perform well as we scale up the difficulty of tasks along our axes, demonstrating the utility of our benchmark in understanding the limitations of current long-context capabilities. Furthermore, the tasks in PredicateLongBench, though challenging, are conceptually simple and do not require LLM-based generations or judges.

AI.tldr: PredicateLongBench is a new benchmark that stress‑tests how well long‑context LLMs can find the longest span in a long input that satisfies formal constraints, revealing weaknesses that average‑case long‑context benchmarks miss.
AI.motivation: Existing long‑context benchmarks mainly test average‑case performance (e.g., NIAH, multi‑hop tasks, summarization), many are close to saturated, and they often lack a principled way to scale difficulty or to isolate different aspects of long‑context reasoning. There is no systematic framework to probe how models behave as tasks become harder along well‑defined axes such as length, structure, or type of constraint. The authors aim to create a more diagnostic, robust, and unsaturated benchmark that exposes where long‑context capabilities break down.
AI.method: The authors design PredicateLongBench, in which a model is given a long sequence of words and a predicate/constraint (e.g., the subsequence must be in lexicographic order), and must output the longest contiguous subsequence that satisfies this predicate. They formalize a broader class of predicates and systematically vary multiple axes of difficulty (such as sequence length, predicate complexity, distribution of valid spans, and others) to probe different facets of long‑context reasoning. They build two data generation pipelines: (1) a fully synthetic one that uses random word‑like strings to tightly control structure and noise; and (2) a real‑world one that samples words from natural documents while preserving empirical distributional properties, thereby keeping the benchmark realistic while retaining control over predicates. The tasks are designed to be deterministic and checkable without LLM‑based grading.
AI.result: When evaluated on PredicateLongBench, frontier LLMs that perform well on existing long‑context benchmarks show significant drops in performance as the benchmark’s difficulty is scaled along the defined axes. Models particularly struggle to reliably identify the correct longest predicate‑satisfying span under harder settings, indicating that their long‑context capabilities are more brittle and limited than suggested by average‑case benchmarks. The authors demonstrate that the benchmark is unsaturated, sensitive to differences between models, and robust due to objective, automatic evaluation.
AI.conclusion: PredicateLongBench offers a principled, fine‑grained way to evaluate and stress‑test long‑context reasoning in LLMs by using predicate‑based longest‑span tasks with systematically varied difficulty. The findings show that current frontier models fail on more challenging configurations, highlighting gaps between advertised context window sizes and true effective long‑context understanding. Because tasks are simple, deterministic, and judge‑free, the benchmark can serve as a reliable tool for diagnosing and driving progress in long‑context LLM research.

title: Large-Language-Models-as-a-Judge in Theory-Agnostic Adaptive Metric-Alignment for Prototypical Networks in Personality Recognition
summary: Personality recognition has traditionally been constrained by theory-dependent formulations, where models are trained to fit predefined psychological taxonomies rather than uncovering shared underlying behavioral structure. This limits generalization, as personality itself is better understood as theory-invariant, while existing annotations reflect only partial and sometimes inconsistent views of the same latent traits. In this work, we introduce JAM ((J)udge for (A)daptive (M)etric-Alignment), a theory-agnostic framework that shifts learning from adapting to predefined personality theories toward discovering unified latent pseudo-facets that capture shared psychological structure. Rather than constraining the model to any personality taxonomy during training or inference, the framework learns generalizable psychological representations and can infer an individual's latent psychological profile directly from the textual samples, without requiring theory-specific labels. JAM achieves this through an Attention-Pooled Graph Prototypical Network that learns structured representations via clustering in embedding space, together with a Cross-Theory Harmonization (CTH) approach that integrates (i) Human-Guided Linkage and (ii) Machine-Induced Consensus to unify heterogeneous datasets without relying on predefined labels. To further improve robustness and data quality, we incorporate an LLM-as-a-Judge mechanism operating in two configurations, (i) LLM-before-the-loop and (ii) LLM-in-the-loop which identifies ambiguous samples to guide adaptive metric learning. Experiments show that JAM improves cross-framework generalization and performance, establishing a strong step toward theory-agnostic personality inference and supporting low-resource personality theories. The related code repository, model weights, and artifacts are available at https://research.jingjietan.com/JAM

AI.tldr: The paper proposes JAM, a theory-agnostic framework for personality recognition that learns shared latent psychological representations from text, improving cross-theory generalization without relying on predefined personality taxonomies.
AI.motivation: Existing personality recognition models are tied to specific psychological taxonomies (e.g., Big Five) and trained on labels derived from those theories. These labels can be incomplete, inconsistent, and theory-dependent, which limits model generalization across datasets and theories. The authors are motivated to model personality as a theory-invariant latent structure and to leverage heterogeneous, sparsely annotated datasets without being constrained by any single taxonomy.
AI.method: The authors introduce JAM (Judge for Adaptive Metric-Alignment), a framework that (1) uses an Attention-Pooled Graph Prototypical Network to learn clustered, structured representations—"latent pseudo-facets"—in embedding space from textual data, without fixing a personality taxonomy; (2) applies Cross-Theory Harmonization (CTH) that combines Human-Guided Linkage with Machine-Induced Consensus to align and unify datasets that come from different personality frameworks; and (3) employs an LLM-as-a-Judge in two modes—LLM-before-the-loop and LLM-in-the-loop—to detect ambiguous or low-quality samples and guide adaptive metric learning, thereby improving robustness and representation quality.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate that JAM yields better cross-framework generalization and improved performance on personality inference tasks compared to baselines that rely on specific taxonomies. It also performs well for low-resource personality theories, suggesting that the learned latent pseudo-facets capture shared psychological structure that transfers across frameworks and datasets.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that personality can be modeled in a theory-agnostic manner through latent pseudo-facets learned directly from text, and that JAM is an effective framework for this. By combining graph-based prototypical representations, cross-theory harmonization, and LLM-based judging for sample selection, the approach advances theory-agnostic personality inference and enables better use of heterogeneous and low-resource personality datasets.

title: Psychological Competence as a Missing Dimension in AI Evaluation
summary: Current AI evaluation frameworks focus primarily on technical performance, including accuracy, robustness, reasoning ability, and policy compliance. These measures remain essential, but they are not sufficient for systems that interact directly with users through natural language. Human-facing AI systems are increasingly used as advisors, coaches, tutors, and companions. In these roles, their responses can shape how users reason, interpret emotions, form beliefs, calibrate trust, and make decisions. The relevant unit of evaluation is therefore not only the model, but the human-AI interaction. This paper introduces psychological competence as a missing dimension in AI evaluation. We define psychological competence as the capacity of a human-facing AI system to support user cognition, emotional interpretation, and behavioral decision-making in ways that are appropriate to the user, context, and purpose of the interaction. This includes interaction properties such as framing, tone, perceived authority, responsiveness, uncertainty handling, and conversational guidance. Existing evaluation approaches capture parts of this problem but rarely assess these psychological effects directly. Drawing on behavioral science and human-AI interaction research, we outline a conceptual framework for psychological competence and its core domains. Rather than proposing a specific benchmark, we define the construct, clarify its boundaries, and describe how it may be assessed through scenario-based probes, structured human evaluation, and model-assisted evaluation methods. We argue that psychological competence should become a core consideration for model providers, deploying organizations, researchers, and regulators concerned with the real-world effects of human-facing AI systems.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that current AI evaluation overlooks how AI systems affect users psychologically and proposes “psychological competence” as a new core evaluation dimension for human-facing AI.
AI.motivation: Existing AI evaluations emphasize technical metrics like accuracy and robustness, but human-facing AI is now used as tutors, advisors, and companions whose outputs shape users’ reasoning, emotions, beliefs, trust, and decisions. There is a gap in systematically evaluating these psychological impacts of AI interactions.
AI.method: Conceptual and framework-building: the authors synthesize insights from behavioral science and human–AI interaction research to define the construct of psychological competence, delineate its core domains, and propose high-level methods for assessing it, such as scenario-based probes, structured human evaluations, and model-assisted evaluations. They do not present a concrete benchmark or empirical study but instead develop a structured lens for future evaluation work.
AI.result: The paper provides a formal definition of psychological competence for AI systems, identifies relevant interaction properties (e.g., framing, tone, perceived authority, responsiveness, uncertainty handling, conversational guidance), and organizes them into a conceptual framework that connects these properties to users’ cognition, emotions, and behavior. It also maps out candidate assessment approaches that can be used to measure these properties more directly.
AI.conclusion: Psychological competence is a missing but essential dimension for evaluating human-facing AI. Evaluations should shift from focusing solely on model-centric technical metrics to also considering the human–AI interaction and its psychological effects. The authors urge model providers, deployers, researchers, and regulators to incorporate psychological competence into the design, testing, and governance of AI systems used in real-world, user-facing settings.

title: Prompt Compression via Activation Aggregation
summary: Large language models process prompts by propagating activations through dozens of layers before generating a response. We ask whether the task-relevant information contained in an instruction prompt can be compressed into a single activation vector and re-injected into the model, replacing the original token sequence? We show this is achievable using a learned weighted sum of activations extracted at an intermediate layer and injected at an early layer of the target LLM. The compressed vector preserves task-relevant information, incurring an accuracy drop of under $2\%$ relative to full prompt processing. Beyond its practical implications, including reducing per-query computation for fixed instruction prompts without reprocessing the original token sequence, our analysis reveals structure in the activation space of LLMs: (i) mid-layer representations transfer meaningfully to early layers, suggesting a degree of cross-layer compatibility in how information is encoded; (ii) a single activation vector encodes a quantifiable and recoverable amount of semantic information; (iii) a weighted sum of activations is a robust representation compressor.

AI.tldr: They show that an instruction prompt for an LLM can be compressed into a single learned activation vector that, when injected into the model, nearly matches the performance of processing the full token sequence.
AI.motivation: Investigate whether the essential task-relevant information contained in a natural-language instruction prompt really requires full token-by-token processing, or whether it can be represented more compactly in activation space to save computation and better understand how LLMs encode information.
AI.method: Extract activations from an intermediate layer of a target LLM while it processes instruction prompts, then learn a weighted sum over these activations to form a single compressed activation vector. At inference, instead of reprocessing the original instruction tokens, inject this vector into an early layer of the LLM and evaluate performance. Analyze transfer between layers and the semantic content of the compressed vectors.
AI.result: The learned single-vector representation can replace the original prompt tokens with less than 2% accuracy loss relative to the full prompt. Mid-layer activations are compatible with early-layer injection, and the weighted sum of activations works as a robust compression method that still encodes semantically meaningful, quantifiable information about the instruction.
AI.conclusion: Instruction prompts can be effectively compressed into a single activation vector while preserving almost all task performance, revealing that LLM activation spaces are cross-layer compatible and that semantic information can be stored and recovered from compact weighted sums of activations. This both reduces computation for repeated prompts and yields insights into the internal representation structure of LLMs.

title: INTENT: An LSTM Framework for Vehicle Intention Prediction in Intersection Scenarios with Comprehensive Ablation Analysis
summary: Vehicle intention prediction is a pivotal aspect in the agility and safety of autonomous vehicles in all driving scenarios; if genuine enhancement of autonomous vehicles are required, we need to make them adopt human interpretation of driver's intention especially in cases that require a lot of human interaction as well as complex driving behaviors like the ones at intersections, roundabouts and emergency cases such as sudden stops where vehicle intention prediction helps in taking the correct evasive action within a real time period where every second of action makes an impact and can prevent a catastrophe from taking place. In the worst case, it helps minimize the damage and make safety a priority. Intention prediction can also be used to enhance trajectory prediction (intention conditioned trajectory prediction). In this study, The INTENT framework is proposed using LSTM model to predict the vehicle's intention at intersections 2 seconds ahead of the event occurrence to predict whether the cars in intersections are going straight, turning left, or turning right. Various model experiments and ablation study are thoroughly tested on InD dataset achieving 99.71% accuracy.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes INTENT, an LSTM-based framework that predicts vehicle maneuvers (straight, left, right) at intersections 2 seconds in advance with very high accuracy.
AI.motivation: Accurate early prediction of vehicle intentions is crucial for autonomous vehicles to act safely and agilely, especially in complex scenarios like intersections, roundabouts, and emergencies where every second matters. Human-like understanding of driver intent can reduce accidents, improve evasive actions, and enhance trajectory prediction.
AI.method: The authors design the INTENT framework using a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) neural network to model temporal driving behavior. The model takes sequential motion data from vehicles and predicts their maneuver class (go straight, turn left, turn right) at intersections 2 seconds before the maneuver happens. They conduct various model experiments and an ablation study to evaluate architecture/design choices. The system is trained and evaluated on the inD naturalistic driving dataset.
AI.result: On the inD dataset, INTENT achieves 99.71% accuracy in classifying vehicle intention at intersections 2 seconds ahead of the event. Ablation studies show the contribution of different model components to performance.
AI.conclusion: The INTENT LSTM-based framework can reliably predict vehicle intentions at intersections with near-perfect accuracy 2 seconds before maneuvers, demonstrating its potential to improve autonomous driving safety and to serve as a strong prior for intention-conditioned trajectory prediction.

title: When Synthetic Speech Is All You Have: Better Call GRPO
summary: LLM-based ASR adapted to regulated domains such as banking is bottlenecked by privacy: real speech is costly and legally constrained to collect, making synthetic text-to-speech (TTS) an attractive substitute. Yet synthetic speech stays acoustically mismatched with real recordings, and work on this gap has stayed within supervised fine-tuning (SFT). We instead turn to reinforcement learning, and show that Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) extracts far more from the same synthetic speech than SFT. Synthetic-only adaptation of the model with GRPO, a critic-free method rewarding low-WER hypotheses, reduces WER by 40\% relative to SFT (36.71\%$\to$22.09\%), and an SFT-then-GRPO combination pushes this further to 45\%. We trace the gain to behavior rather than representation: GRPO reduces insertion errors by improving stopping calibration and speech-to-text alignment by better anchoring attention to audio, leaving early-layer representations intact. When synthetic speech is the main resource, reinforcement learning should be preferred over supervised fine-tuning.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that using reinforcement learning (GRPO) on synthetic speech is significantly more effective than supervised fine-tuning (SFT) for adapting LLM-based ASR models to privacy-sensitive domains like banking.
AI.motivation: In regulated domains such as banking, collecting real user speech for ASR adaptation is restricted by privacy and legal constraints, making it hard to obtain sufficient training data. Synthetic TTS data is an attractive alternative, but it has an acoustic mismatch with real speech and prior work has used only supervised fine-tuning, which underutilizes this synthetic resource. The paper aims to find a more effective way to adapt ASR models using primarily synthetic speech.
AI.method: The authors adapt an LLM-based ASR model using only synthetic TTS data and compare two training strategies: standard supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and a reinforcement learning approach based on Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). GRPO is used in a critic-free setup where the ASR model is rewarded based on low word error rate (WER) of its hypotheses. They also study an SFT-then-GRPO training pipeline, and analyze model behavior and internal representations to understand where improvements arise, focusing on insertion errors, stopping calibration, attention alignment with audio, and early-layer representations.
AI.result: Using synthetic-only training, GRPO reduces WER from 36.71% to 22.09%, a 40% relative improvement over SFT. Combining SFT followed by GRPO yields an even larger relative WER reduction of 45%. Analysis shows that GRPO mainly reduces insertion errors via better end-of-decoding decisions and improved audio-text alignment, while leaving the lower-layer representations mostly unchanged.
AI.conclusion: Reinforcement learning with GRPO is more effective than standard supervised fine-tuning when adapting LLM-based ASR models using only synthetic speech, especially in privacy-constrained domains. The gains stem from behavioral improvements—such as better stopping and alignment—rather than changes in early representations. Therefore, when synthetic TTS is the primary resource for ASR adaptation, RL methods like GRPO should be preferred over SFT.

title: Blind-Spots-Bench: Evaluating Blind Spots in Multimodal Models
summary: Modern AI models achieve strong performance on many established benchmarks, yet they still fail on tasks that humans find almost trivial, such as manipulating a string or drawing a dog with five legs. These examples suggest that existing benchmarks may under-measure persistent blind spots in current systems. We introduce $\texttt{blind-spots-bench}$, a benchmark designed to expose such blind spots through tasks that appear simple for humans but remain challenging for modern AI. We collect raw questions from students in an AI course, clean and annotate them with structured reference solutions, and propose a task taxonomy tailored to the resulting dataset of 235 samples. We further develop an automated grading pipeline to evaluate a wide range of models, including open-weight and closed-source language, vision-language, and image-generation models. Our analysis on $\texttt{blind-spots-bench}$ reveals that closed-source frontier models can substantially outperform open-weight models with even $\approx10\%$ gap, even when they attain comparable performance on existing benchmarks. A more fine-grained analysis shows that no single model dominates across all task types, and that some tasks remain challenging for all evaluated models. These results highlight the value of $\texttt{blind-spots-bench}$ as a diagnostic stress test for identifying concrete weaknesses in current modern models.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces blind-spots-bench, a benchmark of seemingly simple, human-easy tasks that reveal persistent blind spots in modern AI models.
AI.motivation: Despite strong performance on standard benchmarks, modern AI systems still fail on tasks that humans find trivial, suggesting that existing benchmarks overlook important weaknesses. The authors aim to systematically expose and measure these blind spots.
AI.method: They collect real questions from students in an AI course, clean and annotate them with structured reference solutions, and design a task taxonomy tailored to this dataset of 235 samples. They then build an automated grading pipeline and use it to evaluate a broad set of models, including open-weight and closed-source language, vision-language, and image-generation models.
AI.result: Experiments show that closed-source frontier models outperform open-weight models by about 10% on blind-spots-bench, even when both achieve similar scores on existing benchmarks. No single model is best across all task categories, and some tasks remain difficult for all evaluated systems.
AI.conclusion: blind-spots-bench effectively surfaces concrete weaknesses that standard benchmarks miss and serves as a useful diagnostic stress test for analyzing and comparing modern AI models across task types.

title: Detecting Ladder Logic Bombs in IEC 61131-3 PLC Programs using ESBMC-PLC+: A Formal Verification Approach with Trigger Synthesis
summary: A Ladder Logic Bomb (LLB) is malicious control logic in a Programmable Logic Controller (PLC) program that lies dormant until a trigger activates a payload to manipulate actuators, forge sensor readings, or deny operator control. We observe that real malicious logic hides inside function-block bodies, which existing ladder-diagram verifiers drop from their intermediate representation (IR), making bombs invisible to provers. We present ESBMC-LLB, which uses ESBMC-PLC+ as its verification engine and adds a modeling layer that exposes function-block logic and recasts bomb detection as a formal verification problem: a scan-watchdog exposes non-termination payloads, and output wiring exposes actuator-forgery payloads as safety violations. k-induction gives an unbounded proof of bomb-absence across all scans, and the bounded model checker returns a counterexample that is the trigger - guarantees that signature, anomaly, and CFG-triage detectors lack. On the public Iacobelli 2024 dataset, ESBMC-LLB detects all 30 bombs and recovers every trigger; it also detects adaptive triggers (computed, opaque-arithmetic, multi-scan) that evade CFG-triage. We also report the first semantic model-checker evaluation on PLC-Defuser's SWaT corpus: our analog extension makes the full corpus parseable; on v1.0.0, it detects 149/150 bombs (99%) with zero false positives, recovering each trigger; on a later version with nonlinear non-termination bombs, detection drops to 49% as the SMT solver times out. We conclude that semantic model checking and CFG-triage are complementary - the former gives unbounded proofs, adaptive-trigger robustness, and handles Boolean/integer and linear analog logic; the latter leads to nonlinear analog non-termination, and we delineate where each wins.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes ESBMC-LLB, a semantic model-checking framework that exposes hidden function-block logic in PLC ladder programs to reliably detect ladder logic bombs and their triggers, achieving near-perfect detection on benchmark datasets and complementing CFG-based triage approaches.
AI.motivation: Existing defenses against Ladder Logic Bombs in PLC programs miss real-world attacks because malicious logic is often hidden inside function-block bodies that current ladder-diagram verifiers omit from their intermediate representation. Signature, anomaly, and CFG-triage detectors cannot provide unbounded guarantees or reliably detect adaptive triggers. There is a need for a formal, semantic method that can reason about all scans, uncover hidden bombs in full function-block logic, and robustly recover triggers while handling realistic Boolean, integer, and analog behaviors.
AI.method: The authors build ESBMC-LLB on top of the ESBMC-PLC+ verification engine and add a modeling layer that makes the internal logic of function blocks explicit in the verification model. They recast bomb detection as a formal verification problem: (1) a scan-watchdog is modeled to expose non-termination payloads, and (2) output wiring is modeled so actuator-forgery payloads become safety-property violations. Using k-induction, the system proves unbounded absence of bombs across all PLC scans, while bounded model checking is used to find counterexamples that correspond to the bomb triggers. The approach is compared to CFG-triage and other detection methods, and extended with analog modeling to handle SWaT’s continuous signals.
AI.result: On the Iacobelli 2024 dataset, ESBMC-LLB detects all 30 ladder logic bombs and successfully recovers every trigger, including adaptive triggers such as computed, opaque-arithmetic, and multi-scan triggers that evade CFG-triage. On the PLC-Defuser SWaT v1.0.0 corpus, with an analog extension enabling full parsing, the tool detects 149 out of 150 bombs (99%) with zero false positives, again recovering each trigger. On a newer SWaT version that includes nonlinear non-termination bombs, detection falls to 49% due to SMT solver timeouts on nonlinear analog logic.
AI.conclusion: Semantic model checking that fully exposes function-block logic can provide strong, unbounded guarantees about the presence or absence of Ladder Logic Bombs in PLC code, reliably detect and explain triggers, and robustly handle Boolean, integer, and linear analog logic. However, it struggles with nonlinear analog non-termination bombs due to SMT complexity. The authors argue that semantic model checking and CFG-triage are complementary: semantic methods excel at unbounded proofs and adaptive-trigger robustness, while CFG-triage better handles certain nonlinear analog behaviors, and the paper clarifies which threat classes each approach is best suited for.

title: MobiDiff: Semantic-Aware Multi-Channel Discrete Diffusion for Human Mobility Data Generation
summary: Human mobility data are essential for transportation optimization, urban planning, and resource allocation, yet real-world mobility data are costly to collect and difficult to share due to privacy concerns. Recent diffusion-based methods have shown promise in synthesizing realistic mobility patterns, but they typically rely on continuous or latent spatio-temporal traces, limiting their ability to natively model discrete semantic events with explicit region, activity, time, and interval structures. To address this issue, we introduce MobiDiff, an end-to-end discrete diffusion framework that efficiently generates mobility data by directly denoising multi-channel semantic skeletons, avoiding the costly interpolation, latent trace construction, and coarse-to-fine realization pipelines widely used in existing diffusion-based methods. Specifically, MobiDiff decomposes each human check-in event into spatial, activity, and temporal channels, and employs structured event-, group-, and channel-level masking to jointly capture trajectory-level mobility patterns and within-event dependencies. We evaluate generation fidelity, privacy-preserving, and efficiency on three large-scale real-world datasets from Atlanta, Boston, and Seattle. Results show that MobiDiff effectively preserves trajectory length and temporal interval distributions while remaining competitive across broader mobility statistics; it is also much faster than state-of-the-art methods, e.g., 5.3$\times$ faster than GeoGen on average during inference. These findings suggest that discrete diffusion offers an interpretable and efficient framework for synthetic mobility data generation.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes MobiDiff, a discrete diffusion framework that directly generates synthetic human mobility data from multi-channel semantic event skeletons, achieving realistic, privacy-preserving, and efficient mobility synthesis.
AI.motivation: Real human mobility data are vital for transportation planning, urban design, and resource allocation, but they are expensive to collect and hard to share due to privacy concerns. Existing diffusion-based generators work mainly on continuous or latent trajectories, which poorly capture discrete semantic events (region, activity, time, interval) that are important for interpretability and many downstream applications. The paper aims to provide a framework that natively models such discrete, structured mobility events while maintaining realism, privacy, and computational efficiency.
AI.method: The authors introduce MobiDiff, an end-to-end discrete diffusion model that operates directly on multi-channel semantic skeletons of mobility events. Each human check-in event is decomposed into spatial, activity, and temporal channels. The model applies structured masking at event, group, and channel levels during diffusion and denoising to jointly learn trajectory-level patterns (how events are sequenced) and within-event dependencies (how space, activity, and time interact). By doing so, it eliminates the need for interpolation, latent trajectory construction, or multi-stage coarse-to-fine pipelines used in prior work. The framework is evaluated on multiple city-scale datasets for fidelity, privacy, and efficiency.
AI.result: On three real-world datasets (Atlanta, Boston, Seattle), MobiDiff preserves key mobility statistics such as trajectory length distributions and temporal interval distributions more faithfully than or on par with state-of-the-art baselines. It remains competitive on broader mobility metrics while significantly reducing computational cost, being on average 5.3× faster than GeoGen at inference. Privacy-preserving properties are evaluated, indicating that the synthetic data do not trivially leak individual trajectories while still capturing aggregate patterns.
AI.conclusion: Discrete diffusion over structured event skeletons is an effective and efficient approach for synthetic human mobility generation. MobiDiff can generate realistic, privacy-conscious mobility data that preserve important spatial-temporal and behavioral distributions, while substantially improving inference speed over existing diffusion-based methods. This suggests that discrete, semantically structured diffusion models are a promising direction for practical mobility data sharing and analysis.

title: Two Axes of LLM Abstention: Answer Correctness and Question Answerability
summary: A model should refuse two different things: answers it would get wrong, and questions it should not answer at all, such as unanswerable ones or ones resting on a false premise. The usual recipe thresholds a single confidence score, which cannot tell these apart. Across five instruction-tuned models from three families (2B to 14B), we find they are separate axes. Ordinary answer-confidence tracks whether an answer is right but is nearly blind to whether the question is answerable; a linear probe on hidden states does the reverse. The blind spot does not shrink with scale. It is worst on naturally occurring false-premise questions (CREPE). There, answer-confidence, P(IK), P(True), and even asking the model outright whether a premise is false all stay near chance, while a hidden-state probe reaches 0.69 to 0.77 AUROC: the model represents a problem it will not report. This turns out to be fixable. Instructing a model to check premises backfires, because it then disputes sound and false premises alike (57% false challenges), unable to tell them apart; routing the same instruction with the probe roughly triples challenge precision. We turn the two axes into a calibrated policy that answers only when an answerability score and a correctness score each clear a separately certifies behave differently: the unanswerable-answer rate is controllable at every scale, while the wrong-answer rate is capped by model accuracy, so the guarantee tightens as threshold policy certifies both budgets at 0.75 coverage of correct answers, against 0.31 for a single threshold; at 14B it is the only policy that certifies at all.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that models need two separate confidence measures—one for correctness and one for answerability—and that these can be harvested from different internal signals to build safer, better-calibrated refusal policies.
AI.motivation: Current refusal policies typically use a single confidence score to decide whether to answer or abstain. This single axis cannot distinguish between cases where the model is unsure of its answer and cases where the question itself is unanswerable or built on a false premise. This leads to models answering unanswerable or misleading questions and failing to provide strong guarantees about safety and accuracy.
AI.method: The authors empirically study five instruction-tuned models from three families (2B–14B). They measure ordinary answer-confidence (e.g., P(answer)) and train a linear probe on hidden states to predict question answerability / premise falsity. They benchmark on synthetic and naturally occurring false-premise questions, including the CREPE dataset. They test different refusal policies: single-threshold on answer-confidence, explicit instruction to check premises, and a dual-axis policy that thresholds both correctness and answerability scores separately. They evaluate AUROC, challenge precision, and certified coverage under risk budgets for wrong answers and unanswerable answers.
AI.result: They find that standard answer-confidence is well correlated with answer correctness but nearly insensitive to whether the question is answerable or has a false premise. Conversely, a simple linear probe on hidden states predicts answerability / premise falsity well (0.69–0.77 AUROC on CREPE), showing the model internally represents this information without surfacing it. The gap does not shrink with model scale and is largest on naturally-occurring false-premise questions. Explicitly instructing the model to check premises causes it to dispute both sound and false premises at high rates (57% false challenges), indicating poor discrimination; however, routing this same instruction using the probe greatly increases the precision of when the model actually challenges the premise. The dual-threshold policy can satisfy joint constraints on wrong-answer and unanswerable-answer rates while retaining substantially higher coverage than any single-threshold policy.
AI.conclusion: Answerability and correctness are distinct dimensions that require separate signals and thresholds. Standard confidence scores are inadequate for detecting false or unanswerable premises, even as models scale, but internal representations already encode this information and can be accessed via simple probes. Using two calibrated axes—a correctness score and an answerability score—enables policies that reliably avoid both wrong answers and inappropriate answers to unanswerable questions, while preserving useful coverage. Dual-axis, calibrated refusal policies therefore offer a principled path to safer and more predictable model behavior than conventional single-threshold confidence-based abstention.

title: FedOPAL: One-Shot Federated Learning via Analytic Visual Prompt Tuning
summary: With the widespread deployment of basic models in edge intelligence, communication bandwidth has become a core bottleneck restricting the scalability of federated learning. Although one-shot federated learning alleviates this problem by minimizing communication rounds, existing iterative fine-tuning or knowledge distillation methods still face challenges such as high server-side computational costs and hyperparameter sensitivity. Analytical federated learning achieves efficient gradientfree aggregation using least-squares closed-form solutions, but in environments with non-independent and identically distributed data, its static feature assumptions fail, leading to feature manifold misalignment and severely impairing model performance. To address this contradiction, this paper proposes the FedOPAL framework. This framework adapts the visual prompts as feature rectifiers, actively correcting the feature distribution of heterogeneous data to a linearly separable space by applying local proximal constraints, thereby satisfying the theoretical assumptions of analytical federated learning. Experimental results show that FedOPAL not only significantly outperforms the original analytical methods on several benchmarks, but also achieves accuracy comparable to state-of-the-art iterative methods while maintaining zero server-side training costs, providing a new engineering paradigm for efficient collaboration of large models on the edge.

AI.tldr: Proposes FedOPAL, a federated learning framework that uses visual prompts as feature rectifiers so analytical (closed-form, gradient-free) federated learning works well even under non-IID data, matching SOTA accuracy with minimal communication and zero server-side training cost.
AI.motivation: Federated learning at the edge is increasingly limited by communication bandwidth. One-shot and analytical FL reduce communication but either require expensive server-side computation or rely on unrealistic static feature assumptions that break down under non-IID data, causing feature manifold misalignment and poor performance. A method is needed that preserves the efficiency of analytical FL while remaining robust under heterogeneous data distributions.
AI.method: Introduce FedOPAL, which integrates analytical federated learning with learnable visual prompts on clients. These prompts act as feature rectifiers: through local proximal constraints, they adapt client-side representations so that heterogeneous feature distributions are mapped into a linearly separable space that satisfies analytical FL’s theoretical assumptions. The server then performs gradient-free, least-squares closed-form aggregation with zero training on its side.
AI.result: On multiple benchmark datasets, FedOPAL substantially outperforms baseline analytical FL methods under non-IID conditions and achieves accuracy comparable to state-of-the-art iterative FL/knowledge-distillation approaches, while preserving the advantages of one-shot communication and closed-form aggregation, including zero server-side training cost.
AI.conclusion: By combining feature-rectifying visual prompts with analytical, closed-form aggregation, FedOPAL resolves the incompatibility between analytical FL and non-IID edge data. It provides an efficient, communication-frugal, and computation-light paradigm for large-model collaboration at the network edge, approaching the performance of strong iterative methods without their overhead or hyperparameter sensitivity.

title: Cross-seed explainability using Procrustes-conditioned Joint End-to-end Top-K Sparse Autoencoders
summary: We present a Procrustes-conditioned Joint End-to-end Top-K Sparse Autoencoder (SAE) for extracting cross-seed universal features from independently trained BERT models. Cross-seed feature universality is a fundamental challenge in mechanistic interpretability: because dictionary learning is non-convex, independently trained networks learn misaligned feature spaces, so apparently identical features may differ by random initialization. We address this by computing an orthogonal Procrustes rotation between seeds' activation spaces before joint SAE training, combining Top-K sparsity, end-to-end downstream optimization, and an auxiliary dead-feature revival loss based on previous SAE literature. Evaluating on five independent seed pairs (ten BERT models) across three benchmark datasets (SST-2, Stanford Politeness, TweetEval Emotion), our full pipeline produces more universal features (Pearson r $\geq$ 0.70 across seeds) than post-hoc alignment baselines on all three datasets. A minimal qualitative analysis confirms that high-universality features encode interpretable sociolinguistic patterns.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a Procrustes-conditioned, jointly trained Top-K Sparse Autoencoder to extract universal, interpretable features that align across independently trained BERT models.
AI.motivation: Independently trained BERT models (different random seeds) learn activation spaces where features are misaligned because dictionary learning is non-convex. This misalignment makes it hard to identify universal, corresponding features across seeds, which is a core problem for mechanistic interpretability. The authors want a method that recovers cross-seed universal features that are stable and interpretable.
AI.method: They first compute an orthogonal Procrustes rotation to align the activation spaces of different BERT seeds. On this aligned space, they perform joint training of a Top-K Sparse Autoencoder, enforcing that a shared sparse dictionary explains activations from multiple seeds. The training is end-to-end with respect to the downstream task, and they incorporate an auxiliary "dead-feature revival" loss from prior SAE work to keep features active and useful. They then compare this pipeline to post-hoc feature alignment baselines.
AI.result: On five independent BERT seed pairs (ten models) evaluated over three datasets (SST-2, Stanford Politeness, TweetEval Emotion), the proposed pipeline yields more universal features than post-hoc alignment baselines, according to Pearson correlation of feature activations across seeds. Many features achieve Pearson r ≥ 0.70 between seeds, outperforming alternatives on all datasets.
AI.conclusion: Conditioning joint SAE training with a Procrustes rotation effectively aligns feature spaces across independently trained BERT models and produces stable, universal sparse features that are more consistent across seeds than prior methods. Some of these highly universal features correspond to interpretable sociolinguistic patterns, supporting their usefulness for mechanistic interpretability.

title: Towards Mechanistically Understanding Why Memorized Knowledge Fails to Generalize in Large Language Model Finetuning
summary: Fine-tuning LLMs to inject new knowledge faces a critical challenge: LLMs can quickly memorize new facts, yet fail to use them for downstream reasoning tasks. We formalize this failure as the \textit{\textbf{Knowing--Using Gap}}, characterized by an accuracy gap and a temporal lag between memorization and generalization. To understand this phenomenon, we fine-tune LLMs with unseen knowledge and monitor the spatial permeation dynamics of the knowledge internally using a novel intervention technique called self-patching. Self-patching identifies activation locations where relocating representations substantially improves failed generalization cases. These results are consistent with a knowledge-circuit misalignment hypothesis: memorized representations can exist internally but may not be routed to computation-effective layers. To demonstrate the practicality of this diagnostic finding, we design a simple heuristic strategy which recovers 58--75\% of the oracle headroom in generalization failure. Experiments are done cross-domain for the robustness of this finding.

AI.tldr: The paper studies why fine-tuned LLMs can memorize new facts but still fail to use them for reasoning, and introduces a diagnostic/patching method to alleviate this gap.
AI.motivation: When LLMs are fine-tuned with new knowledge, they often show good performance on direct recall of that knowledge but poor performance on tasks requiring reasoning with it. This gap between knowing and using limits the practical value of knowledge injection into LLMs, so understanding and mitigating it is important for building more reliable and useful models.
AI.method: The authors formalize the phenomenon as the Knowing–Using Gap, defined via both an accuracy difference and a time delay between memorization and generalization. They then fine-tune LLMs with previously unseen knowledge and track how this knowledge permeates the network’s internal activations over time. They propose a new intervention technique called self-patching, which searches for internal activation locations where moving or copying representations from successful cases to failed generalization cases substantially improves performance, thereby revealing where knowledge is stored versus where it is effectively used.
AI.result: Using self-patching, they observe that knowledge representations can be present internally yet not properly routed to the layers that enable effective computation and generalization, supporting a knowledge-circuit misalignment hypothesis. Based on these insights, they design a simple heuristic routing/patching strategy that recovers 58–75% of the possible improvement (“oracle headroom”) in generalization performance. They verify that these results hold across different domains, suggesting robustness of the phenomenon and the method.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that the main obstacle in injecting new knowledge into LLMs is not just whether the model memorizes the facts, but whether those representations are correctly integrated into the model’s computational circuits. The Knowing–Using Gap captures this issue, and the self-patching technique serves both as a diagnostic tool and as a basis for practical heuristics that substantially reduce the gap across domains.

title: When the Judge Changes, So Does the Measurement: Auditing LLM-as-Judge Reliability
summary: An LLM-as-judge score can move even when the candidate responses stay fixed, simply because the evaluator has changed. We treat this evaluator-replacement ambiguity as a measurement-validity problem. Across four judgment datasets, we compare two upgrade paths available in practice: scaling Qwen3 dense judges from 1.7B to 32B parameters and moving across MiniMax M2-M2.7 released APIs. The main pattern is that judge upgrades are not interchangeable: only Qwen3 1.7B to 4B gives a robust adjacent gain, while MiniMax adjacent releases do not. Stronger judges reduce but do not remove position and verbosity bias. Repeated-sample juries add little when errors are correlated. Structured debate can move decisions substantially, but without parser and fallback logs those shifts cannot be attributed to deliberation. We argue that LLM-as-judge reports should include dataset slices, bias probes, error-dependence estimates, and protocol audit trails.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how reliable LLM-as-judge scores are when the judging model is upgraded or changed, showing that scores can shift even with identical candidate answers and proposing better reporting and evaluation practices.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly used as automatic judges of other model outputs, but their scores can change just because the judging model is swapped or upgraded. This threatens measurement validity, comparability over time, and fairness in benchmarks and competitions. The authors aim to characterize how different judge-upgrade paths behave and what biases and dependencies remain, to make LLM-as-judge evaluations more trustworthy.
AI.method: They treat judge changes as a measurement-validity issue and empirically compare two practical upgrade paths across four judgment datasets: scaling Qwen3 dense judges from 1.7B to 32B parameters, and moving across versions of MiniMax M2-M2.7 API judges. They analyze how scores shift under these upgrades, measure position and verbosity bias, test repeated-sample juries when judge errors are correlated, and examine the impact of structured debate on decisions, while also checking how much can be attributed to genuine deliberation given logging limitations.
AI.result: Judge upgrades do not behave uniformly: only Qwen3 1.7B→4B provides a robust adjacent accuracy gain, while larger Qwen3 jumps and adjacent MiniMax M2-series releases do not yield consistently reliable improvements. Stronger judges attenuate but do not eliminate position and verbosity biases. Juries of repeated samples add little benefit when errors are correlated. Structured debate can substantially change decisions, yet without detailed parser and fallback logs those changes cannot be confidently traced to deliberation mechanisms.
AI.conclusion: LLM-as-judge systems exhibit significant evaluator-replacement ambiguity: evaluation scores can change with the judge even when candidate outputs are fixed, and judge upgrades are not plug-and-play improvements. To improve validity and interpretability, the authors recommend that LLM-as-judge reports systematically include dataset slices, explicit bias probes, estimates of error dependence, and full protocol audit trails (e.g., logs for parsers and fallbacks), so that score shifts and decision changes can be better understood and trusted.

title: Game Theory Driven Multi-Agent Framework Mitigates Language Model Hallucination
summary: The application of lightweight Large Language Models in rule-based scientific domains remains severely limited by their tendency to mimic linguistic patterns rather than reproduce axiomatic reasoning, causing frequent hallucinations. Here, we show that G-Frame, an adaptive multi-agent framework integrating Bayesian and team game principles, establishes an automated closed-loop for high-quality data synthesis and model training. By forcing the internalization of domain constraints through structured reasoning, we synthesized a specialized corpus of 363,045 chains-of-thought and 199,589 question-answer pairs. The resulting 7B model OmniChem achieves performance parity with GPT 4o mini on custom benchmarks and ChemBench while exhibiting a 79.46% reduction in hallucinations relative to its base architecture. We further demonstrate the advanced capabilities of OmniChem in molecular design and synthesis planning. This work establishes a scalable paradigm utilizing adaptive multi-agents to overcome inherent reasoning deficiencies, offering a feasible pathway for accelerating knowledge discovery in specialized scientific fields.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces G-Frame, an adaptive multi-agent framework that trains a specialized 7B chemistry LLM, OmniChem, which matches GPT-4o mini on chemistry benchmarks and greatly reduces hallucinations by enforcing axiomatic, constraint-based reasoning.
AI.motivation: Lightweight LLMs struggle in rule-based scientific domains because they imitate language patterns instead of performing axiomatic, constraint-respecting reasoning, leading to high hallucination rates. There is a need for scalable methods that instill domain rules and reduce hallucinations so that smaller models can reliably support scientific tasks such as molecular design and synthesis planning.
AI.method: The authors design G-Frame, an adaptive multi-agent framework grounded in Bayesian and team game theory principles. G-Frame forms an automated closed-loop pipeline that synthesizes high-quality training data and iteratively trains models. It enforces internalization of domain constraints through structured reasoning. Using this framework, they generate a specialized corpus consisting of 363,045 chain-of-thought examples and 199,589 question–answer pairs in the chemistry domain, and then fine-tune a 7B-parameter model (OmniChem) on this corpus.
AI.result: OmniChem, the resulting 7B LLM, achieves performance on par with GPT-4o mini on custom benchmarks and ChemBench, while its hallucination rate is reduced by 79.46% compared with its base architecture. The model demonstrates strong performance in applied chemistry tasks, including molecular design and synthesis planning, thereby validating the effectiveness of the G-Frame training framework and dataset.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive multi-agent systems, when combined with Bayesian and team game perspectives, can create a scalable closed-loop for high-quality data synthesis and training that forces LLMs to internalize domain constraints. This approach alleviates inherent reasoning deficiencies of lightweight models, yielding a specialized chemistry LLM with GPT-4o-mini-level performance and significantly fewer hallucinations. The paradigm offers a generalizable pathway for building reliable, domain-specific LLMs to accelerate knowledge discovery in structured scientific fields.

title: It Takes a MAESTRO To Prune Bad Experts
summary: Sparsely-activated Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) language models achieve remarkable inference efficiency by activating only a small fraction of parameters per token, yet their full expert banks reside in memory at all times, creating a prohibitive deployment bottleneck. Existing structured pruning methods, largely designed for dense transformers, assess expert importance using locally derived heuristics that are blind to the interdependent nature of MoE routing. We introduce MAESTRO (Markov-chain Approximated Expert Sparsification via Transition-based ROuting), a structured pruning framework designed for MoE architectures that models autoregressive expert activation trajectories as Ergodic Markov chains whose stationary distributions encode cross-layer dependencies, yielding a globally aware importance heuristic. Evaluated across five diverse domains including Safety, Bias, and Ethics, MAESTRO outperforms state-of-the-art baselines by up to 10.61% in average performance retention under a strict 50% compression regime, while exhibiting substantially lower cross-task variance, indicating that global, routing-congruent pruning produces models that generalize more consistently across heterogeneous tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes MAESTRO, a structured pruning framework tailored for Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) language models, which uses a Markov-chain view of expert routing to globally assess and prune experts, achieving better performance retention under heavy compression than prior methods.
AI.motivation: Although MoE language models are compute-efficient at inference by activating only a few experts per token, all experts must still be kept in memory, creating a major deployment and memory bottleneck. Existing structured pruning methods are mostly designed for dense transformers and rely on local heuristics that ignore cross-layer routing dependencies specific to MoE, leading to suboptimal pruning decisions and inconsistent generalization across tasks.
AI.method: MAESTRO models autoregressive expert activation patterns as ergodic Markov chains, where transitions capture how routing moves between experts across layers and tokens. It computes stationary distributions over experts that implicitly encode these cross-layer, sequence-level dependencies. These stationary distributions are then used as a global importance heuristic to sparsify (prune) the expert bank in a routing-congruent way, under a structured pruning framework designed for MoE architectures.
AI.result: Across five domains (including Safety, Bias, and Ethics), MAESTRO achieves up to 10.61% higher average performance retention than state-of-the-art structured pruning baselines under a strict 50% compression setting, while also showing reduced variance in performance across different tasks.
AI.conclusion: Globally-aware, routing-consistent pruning of MoE models via Markov-chain-based expert importance leads to significantly better and more stable performance under heavy compression than existing local-heuristic methods, making large MoE models more deployable without severely sacrificing task generalization.

title: OmniFood-Bench: Evaluating VLMs for Nutrient Reasoning and Personalized Health Advice
summary:
The rapid integration of Large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) into critical infrastructure promises to revolutionize
  personalized healthcare and dietary management. However, in the domain of food systems, autonomous agents face a
  unique and persistent challenge: the "Systemic Information Asymmetry" between visual appearance and intrinsic
  nutritional composition. Existing benchmarks primarily focus on coarse-grained classification tasks, such as food
  category recognition, which fail to evaluate the intricate reasoning chain required for real-world dietary management
  -- specifically, the ability to traverse from identifying hidden ingredients to estimating physical mass, and finally
  synthesizing safety-critical medical advice. In this paper, we introduce OmniFood-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark
  constructed from the MM-Food-100K dataset. Unlike previous works, OmniFood-Bench evaluates VLMs across three
  progressive capabilities: Basic Perception (Ingredients & Cooking Methods), Quantitative Reasoning (Portion Size &
  Nutritional Profiling), and Safety-Critical Advisory (Disease-Specific Recommendations). We evaluate six
  state-of-the-art VLMs, including gpt-5.1, gemini-3-flash, and qwen3-vl-8B. Our extensive experiments reveal a
  startling "Semantic-Physical Gap": while models achieve near-human accuracy in naming dishes, they exhibit
  catastrophic failure in mass estimation and frequently hallucinate benign advice for high-risk diabetic profiles. This
  work establishes a rigorous standard for trustworthiness in autonomous agents deployed for public health. The code
  and datasets are available in: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/OmniFood-Bench-7D0B

AI.tldr: OmniFood-Bench is a benchmark to test how well vision-language models handle end-to-end food understanding for safe, personalized dietary advice.
AI.motivation: Existing VLM benchmarks for food focus on simple tasks like recognizing dish categories, which do not capture the complex reasoning needed for real-world dietary and medical decision support. There is a critical need to understand whether these models can bridge the gap between what food looks like and its hidden nutritional properties when used in safety-critical healthcare settings.
AI.method: The authors build OmniFood-Bench based on the MM-Food-100K dataset and design a multi-stage evaluation protocol. It tests three progressive capabilities of VLMs: (1) Basic Perception, including ingredient recognition and cooking methods; (2) Quantitative Reasoning, such as portion size estimation and nutritional profiling; and (3) Safety-Critical Advisory, where models must generate disease-specific dietary recommendations. They benchmark six state-of-the-art VLMs, such as gpt-5.1, gemini-3-flash, and qwen3-vl-8B, on these tasks.
AI.result: Despite strong performance on dish naming and coarse perception tasks, the evaluated VLMs perform poorly on estimating physical mass and nutritional quantities and often produce incorrect or overly reassuring dietary advice for high-risk diabetic cases. This exposes a significant semantic-physical gap between visual recognition and understanding of real-world, health-relevant properties.
AI.conclusion: OmniFood-Bench provides a rigorous and structured benchmark to measure trustworthiness of VLM-based autonomous agents in food and health applications. The findings show that current VLMs are unreliable for safety-critical dietary decision making, highlighting the need for methods that better connect visual semantics with underlying physical and nutritional reality. The benchmark resources are publicly available for further research.

title: DominoTree: Conditional Tree-Structured Drafting with Domino for Speculative Decoding
summary:
Speculative decoding accelerates LLM inference by drafting several tokens and verifying them in parallel. Block-diffusion drafters such as DFlash produce
  a draft block in one pass but model only per-position marginals; best-first tree methods such as DDTree expand candidate trees from those marginals. The
  released Domino drafter adds a GRU-based causal correction that makes each draft token's distribution path-dependent, a structure DDTree's factorized
  formulation cannot represent. We introduce DominoTree, a training-free best-first draft tree scored by Domino's conditional, non-factorized correction
  along each root-to-node path, made practical by restricting the per-node correction to a candidate top-M. On Qwen3-4B across eight benchmarks, DominoTree
  reaches up to 6.6x speedup over autoregressive decoding and the highest mean accept length of any evaluated method, up to 10.7 tokens per round, at every
  temperature we test. DominoTree constructs its tree with a GPU-native, CUDA-graph builder that is bit-identical to a reference Python implementation, so
  acceptance is unchanged, while keeping per-round tree construction cheap. With this builder as default, DominoTree wins throughput over the released
  Domino decoder at every temperature, 9-10% overall on Qwen3-4B and up to +22% on Alpaca, and over DDTree/CaDDTree at every temperature we test. On Qwen3-
  8B, DominoTree keeps the highest accepted length at every temperature and adds a decisive throughput win at T=0, +24% over DDTree; at higher temperature
  that edge over DDTree/CaDDTree narrows to a tie and a small loss, while its Overall aggregate wins over DFlash and Domino persist.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces DominoTree, a training-free speculative decoding method that builds a best-first draft tree scored using Domino’s path-dependent correction, achieving higher accept lengths and throughput than prior methods like DDTree, CaDDTree, DFlash, and the released Domino decoder on Qwen3 models.
AI.motivation: Existing speculative decoding methods either model only per-position marginals (block drafters like DFlash) or assume factorized token distributions (DDTree, CaDDTree), which cannot fully exploit Domino’s path-dependent GRU-based correction. There is a need for a drafter that leverages Domino’s conditional, non-factorized token distributions while remaining efficient on GPU, to maximize accept length and throughput in LLM inference.
AI.method: The authors propose DominoTree, a best-first draft tree method that scores each root-to-node path using Domino’s GRU-based, path-dependent correction. To keep it practical, per-node correction is restricted to a top-M candidate set. They implement a GPU-native CUDA-graph tree builder that is bit-identical to a Python reference, ensuring identical acceptance behavior while minimizing per-round construction overhead. Performance is evaluated on Qwen3-4B and Qwen3-8B across multiple benchmarks and temperatures, and compared against autoregressive decoding, DFlash, DDTree, CaDDTree, and the released Domino decoder.
AI.result: On Qwen3-4B over eight benchmarks, DominoTree achieves up to 6.6x speedup over standard autoregressive decoding and the highest mean accept length among evaluated methods (up to 10.7 tokens per round) at all tested temperatures. With the CUDA builder as default, DominoTree improves throughput over the released Domino decoder at every temperature (9–10% overall and up to +22% on Alpaca) and consistently outperforms DDTree/CaDDTree at all tested temperatures. On Qwen3-8B, DominoTree maintains the highest accepted length at every temperature and obtains a +24% throughput gain over DDTree at T=0; at higher temperatures, throughput differences versus DDTree/CaDDTree narrow to parity or a slight loss, while DominoTree’s overall advantage over DFlash and Domino remains.
AI.conclusion: DominoTree effectively combines Domino’s path-dependent correction with a best-first speculative draft tree, overcoming the factorization limitations of prior tree-based methods while remaining training-free and GPU-efficient. It consistently increases accept length and throughput across models, benchmarks, and temperatures, outperforming or matching state-of-the-art speculative decoding methods and demonstrating that leveraging non-factorized conditional corrections in tree-structured drafting is a practical and beneficial direction for accelerating LLM inference.

title: Applying JEPA-Style Predictive Learning to JA4-Derived Network Fingerprints
summary:
I-JEPA and V-JEPA learn by matching latent predictions to target encoder outputs rather than regenerating the original input, and this has worked well for images and video. We explore whether the same objective works for compact network fingerprints. We built JA4-JEPA, a Transformer-based model trained on JA4, JA4H, JA4S, and JA4X subfields drawn from JA4DB and CIC-IDS- 2017. The training data combines roughly 397K samples from both sources, though no single sample contains all four view families. We evaluated the learned representations with a frozen kNN probe on protocol-family classification across TLS, DNS, and SSH. On 39,416 heldout samples the model achieved a cosine similarity of 0.9899 and a kNN accuracy of 0.9220. These results indicate that JEPA-style predictive learning can produce useful embeddings from JA4-derived fingerprints, even with incomplete view overlap across sources.
  Keywords: JA4, network fingerprinting, JEPA, predictive representation learning, self-supervised learning

AI.tldr: The paper applies JEPA-style predictive representation learning to compact network fingerprints (JA4) and shows it can learn useful embeddings for protocol-family classification across TLS, DNS, and SSH.
AI.motivation: Existing JEPA models (I-JEPA, V-JEPA) have been successful on images and video by predicting in latent space instead of reconstructing raw inputs. The authors want to investigate whether this predictive objective can be effectively transferred to network fingerprint data (JA4) to learn robust, compact representations useful for classification tasks.
AI.method: They design JA4-JEPA, a Transformer-based JEPA model trained on multiple JA4-derived subfields (JA4, JA4H, JA4S, JA4X) collected from JA4DB and CIC-IDS-2017 datasets. The training set contains about 397K samples, but no single sample includes all four subfield families, imposing incomplete view overlap. The model is trained in a self-supervised way by matching latent predictions to a target encoder’s outputs rather than reconstructing inputs. The quality of learned representations is evaluated using a frozen k-nearest-neighbor (kNN) classifier on protocol-family labels for TLS, DNS, and SSH.
AI.result: On a held-out test set of 39,416 samples, JA4-JEPA achieves a cosine similarity of 0.9899 between predictions and target encoder outputs, and a kNN classification accuracy of 0.9220 on protocol-family prediction, using only the frozen learned embeddings.
AI.conclusion: JEPA-style predictive self-supervised learning successfully produces high-quality embeddings from JA4-based network fingerprints. Even when training data lacks complete overlap among all fingerprint views, the resulting representations are strong enough to support accurate protocol-family classification, suggesting JEPA objectives are promising for network fingerprint representation learning.

title: UltraX: Refining Pre-Training Data at Scale with Adaptive Programmatic Editing
summary: As available training data approaches its physical limit, gains from Scaling Laws have begun to diminish. Consequently, improving Large Language Models (LLMs) now depends less on data expansion and more on higher-quality data utilization. However, in the context of large-scale corpora, existing refinement methodologies face significant limitations in quality, efficiency, and reliability: Rule-based approaches are constrained by fixed heuristics and struggle with instance-level variations; LLM-based approaches improve quality but fail to meet the efficiency and reliability requirements of large-scale data processing. To address these challenges, we propose UltraX, a function-calling refinement framework for large-scale pre-training data that completes the editing function space by introducing insertion in addition to deletion and modification, enabling fine-grained instance-level editing. Specifically, UltraX builds a reliable program-supervision generation pipeline. In this pipeline, dataset-adaptive prompt optimization first guides an expert LLM to produce high-quality end-to-end refined texts, and Line Alignment Mapping and Dynamic Context Replacement then convert original-refined text pairs into structured program supervision. Meanwhile, UltraX improves supervision quality and stabilizes the training distribution with low-confidence example filtering and ratio-controlled sampling by operation combination. During inference and execution, it normalizes and validates model outputs through sliding-window prediction, global operation aggregation, and systematic post-processing, improving the stability and reliability of large-scale execution. Experiments show that UltraX achieves the highest average performance across all corpora and also matches or surpasses baselines with fewer training tokens, demonstrating stronger data efficiency and refinement reliability.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces UltraX, a function-calling framework to refine large-scale pre-training data for LLMs with higher quality, efficiency, and reliability than existing rule-based or LLM-based methods.
AI.motivation: Scaling Laws gains are saturating because available training data is reaching physical limits, so simply adding more data is no longer effective. Current data refinement methods for large corpora are either low-quality and rigid (rule-based) or high-quality but inefficient and unreliable at scale (LLM-based). There is a need for a scalable, fine-grained, and robust framework to better utilize existing data for LLM pre-training.
AI.method: UltraX defines data refinement as a function-calling problem with three edit operations: deletion, modification, and a newly introduced insertion, allowing fine-grained instance-level editing. It builds a program-supervision generation pipeline where: (1) dataset-adaptive prompt optimization steers an expert LLM to generate high-quality refined texts; (2) Line Alignment Mapping and Dynamic Context Replacement convert original–refined pairs into structured program supervision; (3) low-confidence example filtering and ratio-controlled sampling over operation combinations improve supervision quality and stabilize training. At inference time, UltraX uses sliding-window prediction, global operation aggregation, and systematic post-processing to normalize, validate, and reliably execute model outputs over large corpora.
AI.result: Across multiple corpora, UltraX attains the best average performance compared with all baselines and can reach equal or better performance using fewer training tokens. This indicates that it improves both data efficiency and the reliability of large-scale refinement compared to prior rule-based and LLM-only approaches.
AI.conclusion: UltraX is an effective and scalable framework for refining large-scale pre-training data, enabling more efficient and reliable utilization of existing corpora when further scaling of raw data is limited. By structuring refinement as function calling with a complete edit operation space and carefully designed supervision, training, and inference mechanisms, it delivers higher-quality data for LLM training with better token efficiency.

title: Drift-Aware Temporal Graph Rewiring (DATGR) for Adaptive Semantic Modeling in Biomedical Text
comment: 6 pages, 4 figures. Published in the Proceedings of the 2026 IEEE Conference on Artificial Intelligence (CAI 2026)
summary: Biomedical language evolves rapidly as new discoveries emerge, causing traditional text models to lose semantic fidelity over time. Static embeddings and co-occurrence graphs cannot capture such evolution, leading to performance degradation in retrieval and knowledge discovery tasks.
