This paper introduces a Drift-Aware Temporal Graph Rewiring (DATGR) framework that models concept evolution by dynamically updating co-occurrence edges based on estimated semantic drift. Instead of retraining embeddings for each time slice, DATGR performs lightweight, feedback-driven rewiring using a logistic update rule applied to edge weights. Evaluated on the Biomedical Multi-Relation Corpus (BIOMRC), the method achieved a mean Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic (AUROC) improvement of approximately 0.066 absolute difference (0.699 vs. 0.633) over a static baseline. Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC) remained comparable (0.738 vs. 0.744), showing that drift-aware adaptation enhances link-prediction recall without a loss in precision. These results demonstrate that edge-level adaptation effectively captures temporal semantic change in evolving biomedical text while remaining computationally efficient and interpretable.

AI.tldr: Proposes a drift-aware temporal graph rewiring framework to track evolving biomedical language without retraining embeddings, improving AUROC for link prediction while keeping AUPRC comparable.
AI.motivation: Biomedical terminology and concept relationships change over time as new research emerges, but most text representation methods (static embeddings, fixed co-occurrence graphs) assume stationarity. This mismatch causes older models to lose semantic accuracy and harms tasks like retrieval and knowledge discovery. There is a need for a lightweight, interpretable way to adapt text-based knowledge graphs to temporal semantic drift without repeatedly retraining large language models or embeddings.
AI.method: Introduce Drift-Aware Temporal Graph Rewiring (DATGR), which represents biomedical concepts as nodes in a co-occurrence graph whose edges encode relationships. Instead of learning new embeddings at each time step, the method estimates semantic drift from observed data and then updates edge weights over time using a logistic update rule. The rewiring is feedback-driven and applied at the edge level, dynamically strengthening, weakening, adding, or pruning connections to reflect evolving co-occurrences. This yields a temporally adaptive graph that can be used for link prediction and related knowledge discovery tasks, while remaining computationally lightweight and interpretable.
AI.result: On the Biomedical Multi-Relation Corpus (BIOMRC), DATGR improved mean AUROC for link prediction by about 0.066 absolute (from 0.633 to 0.699) compared with a static co-occurrence baseline. AUPRC was maintained at a similar level (0.738 for DATGR vs. 0.744 for the baseline), indicating higher recall without sacrificing precision. These metrics show better discrimination of true vs. false links in the evolving biomedical graph.
AI.conclusion: Edge-level temporal adaptation of co-occurrence graphs using a drift-aware logistic update rule can effectively capture semantic changes in biomedical language over time. DATGR offers a computationally efficient and interpretable alternative to repeatedly retraining embeddings, improving recall in link prediction tasks while preserving precision, and is thus a promising approach for dynamic biomedical knowledge discovery.

title: WebSwarm: Recursive Multi-Agent Orchestration for Deep-and-Wide Web Search
summary: Large language model (LLM)-based web search agents are transforming information seeking from simple factoid question answering into complex, deep-and-wide search and research-oriented tasks. A single ReAct-style agent is constrained by one long trajectory and limited context, making it difficult to handle depth and coverage simultaneously. Existing multi-agent systems improve search coverage through parallel execution and aggregation, but still exhibit clear limitations in recursive depth, collaboration adaptability, and evidence-grounded expansion. We propose WebSwarm, a progressive recursive delegation framework that jointly constructs task decomposition, recursive expansion, and agent collaboration during inference. WebSwarm dynamically instantiates agentic search nodes, each coupling a local objective with a search mode that specifies how the node should organize search and collaboration. Each node can either solve its objective itself or further delegate child nodes; after solving, it returns evidence and results upward, enabling parent nodes to further expand, revise, or aggregate the search process. To guide this process, WebSwarm first probes how task-relevant information is organized on the web to ground subsequent node expansion, and reuses process-level experience across homogeneous sibling nodes. Experiments on BrowseComp-Plus, WideSearch, DeepWideSearch, and GISA show that WebSwarm consistently outperforms single-agent and multi-agent baselines on deep, wide, and interleaved deep-and-wide tasks. Further analyses of ablation, task difficulty, web tool efficiency, and model generalization explain WebSwarm's effectiveness and provide insights for multi-agent search systems.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces WebSwarm, a multi-agent, recursive delegation framework that improves LLM-based web search on deep, wide, and mixed search tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based web search agents either use a single ReAct-style trajectory, which struggles to simultaneously achieve depth and coverage, or multi-agent systems that improve coverage but still lack sufficient recursive depth, flexible collaboration, and evidence-grounded expansion. There is a need for a framework that can dynamically decompose complex search/research tasks, coordinate multiple agents, and adaptively explore the web in a structured, evidence-driven way.
AI.method: The authors design WebSwarm, a progressive recursive delegation framework. During inference, it builds a tree of agentic search nodes, each with a local objective and a defined search mode that governs how it searches and collaborates. Nodes can either solve their subtask directly or delegate to child nodes, then pass back evidence and results upward, allowing parent nodes to expand, revise, or aggregate. WebSwarm first probes how relevant information is structured on the web to ground this expansion, and it reuses process-level experience among similar sibling nodes to improve efficiency and coordination.
AI.result: On benchmarks BrowseComp-Plus, WideSearch, DeepWideSearch, and GISA, WebSwarm consistently outperforms strong single-agent and multi-agent baselines across deep (recursive), wide (broad coverage), and interleaved deep-and-wide search tasks. Additional analyses (ablation studies, task difficulty breakdown, evaluation of web tool usage efficiency, and model generalization tests) validate that each component contributes to the performance gains and clarify where WebSwarm is most beneficial.
AI.conclusion: WebSwarm effectively addresses limitations of existing LLM-based web search systems by enabling structured, recursive, and adaptive multi-agent collaboration grounded in web evidence. Its progressive delegation and node-level search modes yield superior performance on complex search and research tasks, offering design insights for future multi-agent web search architectures.

title: AI-guided stimuli discovery and generation to optimize facial emotion perception studies in autism
summary: Understanding perceptual differences between autistic and neurotypical adults requires behavioral assays that are sensitive, reliable, and mechanistically informative. Facial emotion perception is a useful test case because group differences have been reported, but findings vary across studies. Here we show that this variability may reflect image-level sparsity: autistic-neurotypical differences in emotion judgments were concentrated in a small subset of diagnostic facial expressions rather than spread uniformly across stimuli. We trained population-specific artificial neural network models to predict image-level judgments for autistic and neurotypical participants, then used these models to select novel faces predicted to maximize group separation. In an independent cohort, model-selected images produced larger behavioral differences than matched random images. We then used the same models with a generative adversarial network to transform diagnostic images toward greater predicted group agreement. In phenotype-matched validation, synthesized images reduced behavioral separation relative to their matched originals. These results establish a model-guided framework for discovering and transforming stimuli that reveal population-specific perceptual differences. More broadly, they show how behavioral phenotyping can move beyond averaging across fixed stimulus sets toward optimized assays that identify the conditions under which neurodivergent perception diverges or converges.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a model-guided framework to detect and manipulate subtle, image-specific differences in facial emotion perception between autistic and neurotypical adults, showing that these differences are sparse and can be amplified or reduced via optimized stimuli.
AI.motivation: Reported differences in facial emotion perception between autistic and neurotypical adults are inconsistent across studies, suggesting that existing behavioral assays may be insensitive, overly averaged, or not well targeted to the most diagnostic stimuli. The authors aim to understand whether group differences are uniformly distributed across facial expressions or concentrated in a small subset of images, and to build a principled way to discover and test such diagnostic stimuli.
AI.method: The authors collected image-level facial emotion judgments from autistic and neurotypical adults and trained separate, population-specific artificial neural network models to predict these judgments. They used these models to: (1) identify novel facial images predicted to maximally separate the two groups’ responses, and then behaviorally validated these predictions in an independent cohort; and (2) combine the predictive models with a generative adversarial network (GAN) to iteratively transform diagnostic images toward greater predicted agreement between groups, then tested whether these synthesized images indeed reduced behavioral differences.
AI.result: Model-selected facial images produced significantly larger behavioral differences between autistic and neurotypical participants than random, but otherwise matched, images, confirming that perceptual differences are concentrated in a sparse set of diagnostic stimuli. Conversely, GAN-transformed images that were optimized for predicted agreement led to reduced behavioral separation relative to their original diagnostic counterparts, demonstrating that the same framework can also attenuate group differences.
AI.conclusion: Perceptual differences between autistic and neurotypical adults in facial emotion recognition are not uniform across stimuli but are sparsely concentrated in specific diagnostic expressions. A model-guided approach using population-specific neural networks and generative models can both discover and manipulate these diagnostic stimuli, enabling more sensitive and mechanistically informative behavioral assays. This framework illustrates how behavioral phenotyping can progress beyond averaging over fixed stimulus sets to optimized, image-level tests that clarify when and how neurodivergent perception diverges from or converges with neurotypical perception.

title: Do You Need a Frontier Model as a Citation Verifier? Benchmarking Rubric LLMs for Deep-Research Source Attribution
summary: Reinforcement learning increasingly relies on an LLM judge to score each rubric criterion, and that judge acts as the reward model during training. Before such a signal can be trusted, we need to know how capable the judge must be and how biased it is. We study this calibration question for citation quality in deep-research systems, where a search-grounded LLM must support each claim it writes with a cited source. Citation quality is a structured rubric task in which each attribution-citation pair is judged along two dimensions that require an LLM, source relevance and factual support. On an adversarial long-form benchmark, we score 8 off-the-shelf LLM judges from 3 model families against gold labels over 1,248 rubric decisions, all of which were human-reviewed and 378 of which were hard cases adjudicated from judge disagreements. Cheaper judges remain competitive across both dimensions, with GPT-5-mini attaining the strongest source-relevance pass-class F1 at 0.908 ($κ$=0.636), while on factual support the judges are statistically indistinguishable (overlapping confidence intervals), so no single model dominates. At comparable F1, the judges still differ substantially in pass-rate drift, false positive rate, and false negative rate. Scalar F1 obscures this directional bias, yet it is exactly what a downstream reinforcement learning loop would reinforce. Calibrating the judge is therefore a prerequisite for using citation rubrics as reward signals, and our results show that this calibration does not require the most expensive available model.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how well different large language models can serve as judging "reward models" for citation quality in research-oriented systems, showing that cheaper models can perform competitively but with important, directional biases that must be calibrated before use in reinforcement learning.
AI.motivation: Many reinforcement learning setups now use an LLM as a judge to score outputs against a rubric, treating those scores as reward signals. For research and citation-intensive tasks, this means an LLM must accurately judge whether each claim is correctly supported by an appropriate source. However, it is unclear how capable such a judge needs to be, how its biases manifest, and whether expensive frontier models are necessary. Without understanding and calibrating these judges, training may reinforce systematic errors or biases in citation behavior.
AI.method: The authors focus on citation quality as a structured rubric task, decomposed into two dimensions for each attribution-citation pair: (1) source relevance and (2) factual support. They construct an adversarial long-form benchmark where a search-grounded LLM produces claims with citations. Eight off-the-shelf LLMs from three model families are used as judges to score 1,248 rubric decisions, each of which has gold labels that were human-reviewed; 378 especially difficult cases were adjudicated after disagreements between judges. They then compare the models’ performance using F1 for pass/fail classification on each dimension and analyze additional calibration aspects such as pass-rate drift, false positive rate, and false negative rate, along with inter-annotator agreement metrics like Cohen’s kappa.
AI.result: The study finds that relatively cheap LLM judges can match or nearly match the performance of larger models on the defined rubric. For source relevance, a smaller model, GPT-5-mini, achieves the highest pass-class F1 score of 0.908 with a kappa of 0.636, indicating strong agreement with gold labels. For factual support, the performance of the different judges is statistically indistinguishable due to overlapping confidence intervals, so no model is clearly superior. Nonetheless, when looking beyond scalar F1 scores, the judges show substantial differences in pass-rate drift and in their false positive and false negative rates, revealing systematic directional biases that F1 alone hides.
AI.conclusion: LLM judges can be effective reward models for citation quality without requiring the most expensive available models, at least in terms of overall F1 performance. However, scalar metrics like F1 can mask important directional biases in how different judges pass or fail examples. These biases are crucial because a reinforcement learning system will amplify whichever tendencies the judge encodes. Therefore, careful calibration of LLM judges—examining pass-rate drift and error asymmetries—is a necessary step before using structured citation rubrics as reward signals in training research-oriented LLMs.

title: CommuniWave:A Machine Learning Model for Quantifying the Degree of Temporary Informal Behavior in Urban Communities
summary: For urban managers and designers, improving the functional attributes of urban communities to enhance territorial resilience in the face of complexity and uncertainty is crucial. Currently, community planning often follows a top-down approach and lacks effective metrics to quantify informal behaviors of residents, leading to frequent conflicts with original plans. This study introduces CommuniWave, a machine learning model designed to efficiently detect and quantify the Degree of Informal Behavior (DIB) in urban communities. The model integrates a Behavior Capture Net (BCN) based on mmaction2, a self-developed YOLOv10 model (YLX), and a Behavior Eval Model (BEM) using random forest. Ultimately, by generating DIB fluctuation charts from street videos, the model facilitates dynamic monitoring, supporting urban managers in making refined decisions to enhance the overall resilience of communities.

AI.tldr: The paper presents CommuniWave, a machine learning model to detect and quantify informal resident behaviors in urban communities to aid resilient urban management.
AI.motivation: Urban managers need better tools to enhance community resilience under complexity and uncertainty, but current top-down planning lacks reliable metrics to measure informal resident behaviors, causing conflicts between plans and actual use.
AI.method: The study proposes CommuniWave, which processes street videos using three components: a Behavior Capture Net (BCN) based on mmaction2 for behavior recognition, a custom YOLOv10 (YLX) for object and person detection, and a Behavior Eval Model (BEM) built with random forests to compute a Degree of Informal Behavior (DIB) and generate its temporal fluctuations.
AI.result: CommuniWave successfully produces DIB fluctuation charts from street-level video, enabling quantification and temporal tracking of informal behaviors in communities.
AI.conclusion: By dynamically monitoring informal behaviors through DIB metrics, CommuniWave provides urban managers with actionable data to fine-tune planning and management decisions, thereby improving the territorial resilience of urban communities.

title: Validity of LLMs as data annotators: AMALIA on authority
summary: A national language model offers a linguistic community its own instrument for measuring what its citizens say and value. Portugal's AMALIA, a publicly funded 9B-parameter model for European Portuguese, appears competitive on agreement alone: asked to code the moral foundation of authority, it agrees with trained human coders to within six F1 points of open models eight to thirteen times its size. Yet agreement is reliability, not validity. For theoretical constructs that must be inferred rather than read from surface features, the question is whether the model follows the construct's theory or reaches the right code by correlated shortcuts. We test this with the recovery gap: the loss in performance when a holistic prompt is decomposed into the codebook's atomic clauses and recombined by the theory's explicit rule. If calibration closes that gap, some portability should survive across models and languages; where it does not, the construct-model instrument is the likely locus of failure. We ask whether a calibrated English instrument transfers to AMALIA-9B and to European Portuguese. For one construct and one corpus, it does not. Decomposition recovers only about half of AMALIA's holistic performance, and error analysis suggests reliance on surface correlates, especially moral outrage near authority figures. An open multilingual LLM closes the gap on the same Portuguese corpus under the same instructions, pointing away from the corpus as the main explanation. AMALIA can still screen and pre-code at scale, but it cannot yet measure this construct well enough to stand alone. The study is a single counterexample, not a verdict on national models; it argues that sovereign-LLM benchmark batteries should test not only agreement with human coders, but the evidential route by which that agreement is warranted.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates a national Portuguese language model (AMALIA-9B) as a scientific measurement tool for moral foundations and shows that high agreement with humans does not guarantee valid construct measurement.
AI.motivation: National or sovereign LLMs are being built to serve specific linguistic communities, but their scientific and policy value depends not just on matching human labels, but on whether they actually implement theoretical constructs rather than exploit superficial correlations. The authors want a way to test whether an LLM’s apparent success in coding abstract constructs like moral foundations reflects true construct understanding versus shortcut learning, and whether validated measurement instruments can transfer across models and languages.
AI.method: They focus on one theoretical construct: the moral foundation of authority. They compare AMALIA-9B—a 9B parameter, publicly funded European Portuguese model—against larger open models on a corpus where moral foundation codes exist. First, they assess standard agreement (F1) with trained human coders using holistic prompts (asking for a direct moral-foundation code). Then they propose the “recovery gap” test: decompose the coding task into atomic clauses from the theory’s codebook, prompt the model for each atomic element, and recombine these partial judgments according to the explicit theoretical rules. They compare performance between holistic prompting and this decomposed, theory-following procedure. They apply a calibrated English measurement instrument to AMALIA-9B and to European Portuguese data and compare results with an open multilingual LLM used under the same instructions.
AI.result: AMALIA-9B shows strong agreement with human coders in holistic coding, within about six F1 points of much larger open models. However, when the task is decomposed into atomic codebook clauses and recombined by theory, AMALIA’s performance drops sharply: the decomposed (theory-based) process recovers only about half of the holistic performance. Error analysis indicates that AMALIA often relies on surface cues, especially expressions of moral outrage around authority figures, rather than following the full theoretical decision rules for the authority foundation. In contrast, an open multilingual LLM, run on the same Portuguese corpus with the same decomposed instructions, largely closes the recovery gap, suggesting that the corpus itself is not to blame.
AI.conclusion: The study provides a concrete counterexample where a national LLM with high human agreement fails as a valid measurement instrument for an abstract moral construct, because its apparent accuracy likely comes from shortcut use of surface correlates rather than faithful implementation of theory. This implies that benchmark suites for sovereign LLMs should not rely solely on agreement metrics but must evaluate the evidential route—e.g., via recovery-gap style tests—by which the model arrives at its codes. While AMALIA-9B may still be useful for large-scale screening and pre-coding, it is currently insufficient on its own for rigorous construct measurement of the authority moral foundation in European Portuguese. The authors frame this not as a blanket indictment of national models but as a methodological warning about how to validate them as scientific instruments.

title: SHAP-Weighted Cross-Modal Expert Fusion for Emotion and Sentiment Recognition: Evidence and Limits
summary: Multimodal emotion and sentiment recognition is commonly addressed by early fusion, which concatenates modalities before classification, or late fusion, which combines independently trained unimodal predictors. Early fusion can be accurate but monolithic, while late fusion is modular but may lose cross-modal interactions. This paper revisits XAI-guided adaptive fusion (\xgaf), a tree-based mixture of unimodal and cross-modal experts whose sample-level weights are derived from TreeSHAP attribution magnitudes. We focus on the effect of SHAP attribution reduction when experts have unequal feature dimensionalities. In this setting, mean-abs and median-abs reductions can suppress high-dimensional cross-modal experts, whereas sum-abs reduction preserves total attribution mass. On MELD 7-class emotion recognition, sum-abs \xgaf{} nearly matches early fusion across three face-sequence aggregators; the Transformer variant reaches 0.5983 \wf{}, compared with 0.6018 for early fusion and 0.4598 for probability-average late fusion. McNemar testing shows no significant difference between sum-abs \xgaf{} and early fusion on MELD ($p=1.000$), while \xgaf{} remains significantly better than late fusion ($p<0.0001$). On CMU-MOSEI 3-class sentiment recognition, sum-abs \xgaf{} reaches 0.6519 \wf{}, slightly exceeding early fusion (0.6485) and late fusion (0.5696). Ablation studies show that the main gain comes from adding cross-modal experts, especially the trimodal expert, rather than from complex per-sample routing. Diagnostics further show that mean-abs and median-abs weights are nearly uniform, while sum-abs weights concentrate on the trimodal expert. Thus, the main contribution is a transparent empirical analysis of how SHAP reduction, expert dimensionality, and cross-modal expert design affect modular multimodal fusion.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how different ways of aggregating SHAP attributions affect an explainable, expert-based fusion method for multimodal emotion/sentiment recognition, and finds that summing absolute attributions lets cross-modal (especially trimodal) experts dominate and reach early-fusion-level accuracy while retaining modularity.
AI.motivation: Multimodal emotion/sentiment models often use early fusion, which is accurate but non-modular, or late fusion, which is modular but loses cross-modal interactions. A recent XAI-guided adaptive fusion method (\xgaf) promises a middle ground by using SHAP-based weights to mix unimodal and cross-modal experts, but the impact of SHAP attribution reduction when experts have different feature dimensionalities has not been systematically examined. Understanding this is important to design modular fusion systems that still capture cross-modal synergies.
AI.method: The authors revisit XAI-guided adaptive fusion, modeling it as a tree-based mixture of experts including unimodal and cross-modal (trimodal) components. For each input sample, TreeSHAP attributions are computed for each expert; these attributions are reduced to a single expert weight using different reduction schemes: mean absolute value, median absolute value, and sum of absolute values. They analyze how unequal feature dimensionalities (e.g., high-dimensional trimodal experts vs lower-dimensional unimodal ones) interact with these reductions. Experiments are conducted on MELD (7-class emotion) and CMU-MOSEI (3-class sentiment) with several face-sequence aggregators, comparing early fusion, late fusion, and \\xgaf with different reductions. They run McNemar tests, perform ablations on expert sets and routing complexity, and inspect the resulting expert weight distributions.
AI.result: On MELD, sum-abs \\xgaf almost matches early fusion across three visual aggregators; the Transformer-based variant achieves 0.5983 weighted F1 vs 0.6018 for early fusion and 0.4598 for probability-average late fusion. McNemar tests indicate no significant difference between sum-abs \\xgaf and early fusion (p=1.000), while \\xgaf significantly outperforms late fusion (p<0.0001). On CMU-MOSEI, sum-abs \\xgaf obtains 0.6519 weighted F1, slightly surpassing early fusion (0.6485) and late fusion (0.5696). Ablations show that the primary benefit comes from including cross-modal, especially trimodal, experts rather than from sophisticated per-sample routing. Diagnostic analyses reveal that mean-abs and median-abs reductions yield nearly uniform expert weights, whereas sum-abs produces sharply concentrated weights on the trimodal expert.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that in SHAP-based adaptive fusion with experts of differing dimensionalities, the choice of attribution reduction is crucial: mean and median absolute reductions can unintentionally downweight high-dimensional cross-modal experts and yield nearly uniform routing, while sum of absolute attributions preserves total importance and naturally favors powerful trimodal experts. With appropriately designed cross-modal experts and sum-abs reduction, XAI-guided adaptive fusion can achieve early-fusion-level or better performance while maintaining modularity and interpretability. The core contribution is a clear empirical characterization of how SHAP reduction, expert dimensionality, and cross-modal expert structure interact in modular multimodal fusion systems.

title: UniClawBench: A Universal Benchmark for Proactive Agents on Real-World Tasks
comment: Project Page: https://uniclawbench.github.io | GitHub Repo: https://github.com/HKU-MMLab/UniClawBench
summary: The rapid development of large language models and multimodal large language models has accelerated the emergence of proactive agents capable of operating everyday tools and assisting users in real-world environments. However, existing benchmarks struggle to evaluate such agents effectively, as they often rely on sandboxed environments and single-turn evaluation paradigms. Moreover, their scenario-based task taxonomies mix multiple model capabilities within the same task category, making it difficult to identify the root causes of agent failures. To address these limitations, we introduce UniClawBench, the first capability-driven benchmark designed to evaluate proactive agents in dynamic, real-world settings. UniClawBench is built around five foundational model capabilities: Skill Usage, Exploration, Long-Context Reasoning, Multimodal Understanding, and Cross-Platform Coordination. Based on these capabilities, we design 400 bilingual real-world tasks. Unlike previous benchmarks that rely on static, pre-recorded answers, our benchmark evaluates agents in live Docker containers using fine-grained, step-by-step completion checkpoints. Furthermore, we design a closed-loop evaluation strategy comprising an executor agent, a hidden supervisor agent, and a user agent to simulate realistic multi-turn human feedback without leaking grading criteria. To disentangle base model capabilities from framework-level design choices, we evaluate state-of-the-art models under multiple agent frameworks. Through comprehensive comparisons across both models and frameworks, we show how base model capabilities and agent framework designs jointly shape performance in real-world environments. To facilitate future research, we make our benchmark and code publicly available at https://github.com/HKU-MMLab/UniClawBench.

AI.tldr: UniClawBench is a new capability-driven benchmark for evaluating proactive LLM-based agents in realistic, dynamic tool-use scenarios using live Docker environments and multi-turn, stepwise evaluation.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks for proactive agents rely mostly on sandboxed, static, or single-turn setups and scenario taxonomies that entangle multiple capabilities, making it hard to diagnose why agents fail in real-world tool-use situations. There is a need for a benchmark that both reflects real-world, dynamic environments and separately assesses core capabilities of agentic LLM systems.
AI.method: The authors design UniClawBench around five atomic capabilities—Skill Usage, Exploration, Long-Context Reasoning, Multimodal Understanding, and Cross-Platform Coordination—and create 400 bilingual, real-world tasks aligned with these capabilities. They run evaluations in live Docker containers and use fine-grained step-by-step checkpoints rather than static answers. They further develop a closed-loop evaluation setup with three agents (executor, hidden supervisor, and user) that simulates realistic multi-turn feedback without revealing grading criteria. Finally, they test multiple state-of-the-art models under different agent frameworks to disentangle base model ability from framework design choices.
AI.result: Experiments show differentiated performance profiles across both base models and agent frameworks, demonstrating that both underlying model capabilities and the surrounding framework architecture significantly influence success in real-world agent tasks. UniClawBench exposes where and how models or frameworks break down across the five core capability dimensions.
AI.conclusion: UniClawBench provides a more diagnostic, capability-centric, and realistic benchmark for proactive LLM agents than prior work. By using dynamic Docker-based environments, multi-turn closed-loop evaluation, and a decomposed capability taxonomy, it enables clearer analysis of failure modes and the interaction between model capabilities and agent-framework design, and is released publicly to support further research.

title: Towards Precision Therapy in Hepatocellular Carcinoma: A Clinical-Reasoning LLM for Risk Stratification and Treatment Guidance
summary: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) is a common malignancy and a leading cause of cancer-related mortality. Current guidelines and staging systems provide coarse categories, but often miss within-stage heterogeneity and the clinical context in electronic medical records (EMRs). We present HCC-STAR (Hepatocellular Carcinoma Staging, Treatment And pRognosis), a clinically aligned large language model that reads routine EMR narratives and jointly outputs risk score-based staging, ranked guideline-consistent treatments with evidence-based rationales, and individualized survival estimates. We curated about 30,000 HCC cases from SEER and expanded them into EMR-style narrative training data using a clinician-validated, prompt-based augmentation workflow. On this corpus, we developed a knowledge-aligned reasoning framework optimized with a step-verifiable composite reward, moving beyond text-level memorization of clinical guidelines. In a multi-center cohort of 6,668 patients from 12 hospitals in China, HCC-STAR achieved state-of-the-art performance in treatment recommendation and risk stratification compared with clinical guidelines and competitive models, including GPT-5 and Gemini-2.5 Pro. Hypothetical overall-survival analysis showed a median survival of 51 months under adherence to HCC-STAR recommendations, compared with 29 and 32 months under BCLC and CNLC. In clinician-centric evaluations, blinded hepatobiliary specialists rated HCC-STAR's reasoning and evidence-based justifications as trustworthy. The model surpassed resident and attending physicians in treatment accuracy and helped physicians make more accurate decisions faster when used as an assistant. These findings support HCC-STAR as a reliable and verifiable decision-support system for risk stratification and precision therapy in HCC.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces HCC-STAR, a large language model that reads hepatocellular carcinoma EMRs to provide fine-grained staging, guideline-consistent treatment recommendations with explanations, and individualized survival predictions, outperforming existing guidelines and frontier LLMs while being trusted by clinicians.
AI.motivation: Existing HCC staging systems and guidelines offer only coarse categories and do not capture within-stage heterogeneity or the rich clinical context buried in EMR narratives, leading to suboptimal treatment decisions and outcomes. There is a need for an automated, clinically aligned system that can interpret routine text notes, integrate guideline knowledge, and provide precise, explainable treatment and prognosis support.
AI.method: The authors curated about 30,000 HCC cases from SEER and transformed them into EMR-style narratives using a clinician-validated, prompt-based data augmentation pipeline. They then trained a large language model under a knowledge-aligned reasoning framework, optimized with a step-verifiable composite reward so the model learns structured reasoning and guideline-consistent decisions rather than text memorization. The resulting model, HCC-STAR, jointly outputs risk score-based staging, ranked treatment options with evidence-based rationales, and individualized survival estimates from free-text EMRs. They evaluated it on a multi-center real-world cohort from 12 hospitals and via blinded clinician assessments and decision-support experiments.
AI.result: On 6,668 real-world HCC patients from 12 Chinese hospitals, HCC-STAR achieved state-of-the-art performance in both treatment recommendation and risk stratification, outperforming standard clinical guidelines (BCLC and CNLC) and strong baseline models, including GPT-5 and Gemini-2.5 Pro. Simulated overall-survival analyses suggested markedly improved median survival (51 vs. 29 and 32 months for BCLC and CNLC) if its recommended treatments were followed. In blinded evaluations, hepatobiliary specialists rated its reasoning and evidence citations as trustworthy, and the model exceeded resident and attending physicians in treatment decision accuracy while accelerating clinicians’ decision-making when used as an assistant.
AI.conclusion: HCC-STAR functions as an accurate, trustworthy, and explainable decision-support tool for hepatocellular carcinoma, capable of reading routine EMR narratives to provide granular staging, guideline-concordant treatment recommendations, and personalized survival predictions. Its superior performance relative to existing guidelines, frontier LLMs, and practicing physicians, along with positive clinician feedback, suggests it can enable more precise therapy and better risk stratification in HCC care.

title: The complexities of patient-centred conversational artificial intelligence
summary: Consumer-facing health chatbots powered by large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used for symptom assessment. However, chatbot development and evaluation often rely on cooperative, articulate, simulated patients. We analysed 2,053 real patient-chatbot conversations and found that communication patterns and expression of emotions vary widely across users. We developed a patient simulator that separately models clinical content, emotional state, conversational strategy, and communication style. In a Turing-inspired evaluation of realism with 15 human graders, simulated conversations were nearly indistinguishable from real ones, with human graders achieving an accuracy of 55%. We used five distinct patient personae, across 1,164 clinician-graded cases, to evaluate the performance of four LLMs in urgency assessment. We found that communication style can significantly alter triage outcomes. Patient-centred conversational artificial intelligence must accommodate communication diversity: systems designed for idealised, rather than realistic, interactions risk underperforming and amplifying health disparities when deployed in the real world.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes real patient-chatbot conversations, builds a realistic patient simulator capturing diverse communication behaviors, validates its realism, and shows that patients’ communication styles can significantly influence LLM-based triage decisions, implying risks for equity and performance if systems are only tuned on idealized users.
AI.motivation: Most health LLM chatbots are trained and tested on cooperative, articulate, simulated patients, which does not reflect the wide variability of how real patients describe symptoms or express emotions. This mismatch may lead to unrealistic performance estimates and potential inequities in real-world deployment. The authors aim to understand real patient-chatbot communication patterns and build a more realistic simulator to rigorously evaluate LLM-based symptom assessment and triage systems.
AI.method: The authors analyzed 2,053 real conversations between patients and a health chatbot to characterize variation in communication patterns, emotional expression, and interaction styles. They then built a patient simulator with modular components that separately model: (1) clinical content (symptoms and health information), (2) emotional state, (3) conversational strategy (e.g., cooperative vs withholding, focused vs tangential), and (4) communication style (e.g., verbosity, formality). They evaluated the realism of the simulator using a Turing-style test where 15 human graders tried to distinguish real from simulated conversations. Finally, they defined five distinct patient personae and used these to generate 1,164 simulated cases, which clinicians graded for urgency while four different LLMs performed urgency assessment on the same persona-based conversations, enabling analysis of how communication style affects triage performance.
AI.result: Human graders in the Turing-style evaluation could distinguish real from simulated conversations only slightly better than chance, with an accuracy of 55%, indicating the simulator produces highly realistic dialogues. Applying five distinct patient personae across 1,164 clinician-graded cases to four LLMs, the study found that different communication styles—despite representing the same underlying clinical content—can significantly change the models’ urgency assessment decisions. This shows that LLM-based triage is sensitive not just to what patients say but how they say it.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates that realistic modeling of patient communication diversity is critical for developing and evaluating health chatbots. LLM-based triage systems that are optimized on idealized, cooperative, or overly clear patient interactions may perform poorly when confronted with the heterogeneous, emotionally nuanced, and sometimes indirect communication seen in real-world users. Such mismatches risk underperforming in practice and may exacerbate health disparities, especially for users whose communication styles deviate from the assumed norm. The authors argue that patient-centered conversational AI must explicitly accommodate and be stress-tested against diverse communication styles, emotional states, and interaction strategies to ensure robust and equitable real-world performance.

title: Formal Mechanisms for Market Stability in Self-Interested Agent Societies: A Marketplace Simulation Study
summary: Self-interested agents, left unconstrained, tend toward defection in repeated social dilemmas, causing cooperative gains from trade to collapse. This paper investigates what formal mechanisms, layered on top of unrestricted communication, are sufficient for a society of such agents to maintain market stability, and how resilient those mechanisms are to adversarial attack. We instantiate the research question as a multi-agent marketplace simulation where 18 LLM agents (DeepSeek-V3) with complementary production specialties must trade within a constrained social network to obtain utility. We conduct two experimental phases: (1) a mechanism comparison across eight conditions under progressive troll injection over 200 rounds, identifying Mediation as the top-performing mechanism; and (2) adversarial red-teaming of Mediation using iteratively prompt-optimised LLM-driven trolls, finding that the best attack (v6) reduces honest-agent utility by 13.3% but cannot collapse the market. Mediation enables recovery even under sustained adversarial pressure. We define adversarial robustness as a mechanism's ability to sustain positive honest-agent utility under optimised attack, and find that Mediation is robust: it can be bent but not broken.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how to keep markets of self-interested LLM agents stable and cooperative under adversarial pressure, finding that a Mediation mechanism remains robust even against optimized trolling attacks.
AI.motivation: In repeated social dilemmas, self-interested agents tend to defect, which causes cooperative trade benefits to collapse. With LLM agents increasingly used in autonomous and multi-agent systems, understanding what formal mechanisms can preserve cooperation and market stability under both normal and adversarial conditions is important. The authors want to know which institutional layer on top of free communication can prevent collapse and how resistant such mechanisms are to sophisticated attacks.
AI.method: The authors build a multi-agent marketplace simulation with 18 DeepSeek-V3 LLM agents, each with different production specialties, who must trade along a constrained social network to gain utility. They compare eight different institutional or coordination mechanisms, all on top of unrestricted communication, under conditions of progressively injected trolling behavior over 200 interaction rounds. After identifying Mediation as the best-performing mechanism, they then conduct an adversarial red-teaming phase where LLM-driven troll agents are iteratively prompt-optimized to improve their attack strength against Mediation, with multiple attack versions (v1–v6).
AI.result: In the mechanism comparison, Mediation emerges as the top-performing mechanism in terms of preserving market stability and honest-agent utility as the level of troll injection increases. In the focused red-teaming phase, the strongest discovered troll strategy (v6) is able to reduce honest-agent utility by 13.3%, but it still fails to collapse the market or drive honest agents’ payoffs negative. The Mediation mechanism supports recovery dynamics even when adversarial pressure is both strong and persistent.
AI.conclusion: Mediation, as a formal institutional mechanism layered on top of free-form communication, is adversarially robust by the authors’ definition: it maintains positive honest-agent utility even under prompt-optimized, LLM-driven trolling attacks. While adversaries can degrade performance ("bend" the system), they cannot fully destabilize the marketplace ("break" it). This suggests that appropriately designed coordination and dispute-resolution mechanisms can substantially enhance the resilience of LLM-based multi-agent markets to adversarial manipulation.

title: SolarChain-Eval: A Physics-Constrained Benchmark for Trustworthy Economic Agents in Decentralized Energy Markets
summary: As agentic AI systems are increasingly applied to cyber-physical environments, their evaluation requires assessment of both task performance and trustworthiness. In decentralized energy markets, autonomous agents may improve market utility, but may also exploit invalid physical data, create artificial liquidity, and produce unstable governance decisions. Therefore, we propose SolarChain-Eval, a physics-constrained benchmark for evaluating trustworthy economic agents. It formulates market governance as a Gymnasium-compatible Markov Decision Process, where agents make hourly decisions. SolarChain-Eval evaluates each policy across multiple dimensions, including market utility, physical safety, slippage, action smoothness, spatial fairness, and auditability. To support agentic evaluation, SolarChain-Eval incorporates an LLM-based Planner/Auditor layer. The Planner defines episode-level action bounds and audit rules, while the Auditor reviews and revises high-risk actions. All interventions are recorded through structured logs, including trigger signals, proposed actions, revised actions, and audit rationales. Experiments with static, random, myopic, RL, and RL+LLM policies reveal a clear utility-safety trade-off. RL agents improve market utility but can still produce unsafe behavior. When the physics penalty is removed, reward-maximizing agents exploit invalid generation and increase artificial liquidity. The LLM Planner/Auditor improves auditability and mitigates selected risks, but it cannot fully compensate for a misspecified reward function. These results indicate that trustworthy agentic AI evaluation requires both physical constraints and transparent intervention traces. We release data and code as open access on GitHub for replicability.

AI.tldr: SolarChain-Eval is a benchmark and environment to evaluate how trustworthy autonomous economic agents are in decentralized energy markets under physical constraints.
AI.motivation: Agentic AI systems are starting to act in cyber-physical settings like energy markets, where they can both improve efficiency and introduce new risks such as exploiting bad sensor data, creating fake liquidity, or destabilizing governance. Existing evaluations focus largely on task performance and overlook trust, safety, and physical validity, especially in market contexts tightly coupled to real-world physics. The paper aims to provide a systematic way to measure both utility and trustworthiness of agents operating in such environments.
AI.method: The authors design SolarChain-Eval, a benchmark that models decentralized energy market governance as a Gymnasium-compatible Markov Decision Process with hourly decisions. They define a multi-dimensional evaluation suite covering market utility, physical safety (physics constraints), slippage, temporal smoothness of actions, spatial fairness across participants, and auditability. On top of the environment, they add an LLM-based Planner/Auditor layer: the Planner sets episode-level bounds and rules for actions, and the Auditor inspects and can revise high-risk actions based on these rules. All interventions and decisions are logged in a structured way, capturing triggers, proposed and revised actions, and rationales. They then run experiments comparing a variety of policies (static, random, myopic, RL, RL+LLM) and manipulate the presence or absence of physics penalties in the reward.
AI.result: Experiments show that reinforcement learning agents increase market utility compared to baselines but still exhibit unsafe behaviors, especially when physics penalties in the reward are relaxed or removed. Without physics penalties, reward-maximizing agents exploit invalid physical generation levels and inflate artificial liquidity, exposing vulnerabilities in naive reward design. The LLM-based Planner/Auditor layer improves auditability and reduces some high-risk behaviors; however, it does not fully prevent unsafe strategies when the underlying reward function is misspecified. A utility-safety trade-off is clearly observed across the tested policies.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that trustworthy evaluation of agentic AI in decentralized energy markets requires explicitly enforcing physical constraints in both environment and reward, as well as maintaining transparent, inspectable intervention traces. An LLM-based Planner/Auditor can meaningfully support oversight and risk mitigation but cannot by itself fix flawed reward specifications. SolarChain-Eval, released with open-source data and code, provides a physics-constrained, multi-metric benchmark to study and improve trustworthy economic agents in cyber-physical markets.

title: Remember When It Matters: Proactive Memory Agent for Long-Horizon Agents
summary: In long-horizon tasks, decision-relevant state is often scattered across an expanding trajectory, while the action agent must surface it and act. As trajectories grow, task requirements, environment facts, prior attempts, diagnoses, and open subgoals can be buried in the context window or pushed beyond it, failing to influence decisions when needed. We call this failure mode "behavioral state decay". We study memory as an active intervention mechanism rather than passive retrieval. A separate memory agent runs alongside an unmodified action agent, updating a structured memory bank from the recent trajectory and deciding whether to inject a memory-grounded reminder or remain silent. The module is plug-and-play with frontier action agents and existing agent harnesses. Across Terminal-Bench 2.0 and $τ^2$-Bench, it improves pass@1 for both weaker and stronger action agents, with gains of +8.3 pp on Terminal-Bench and +6.8 pp on $τ^2$-Bench. Ablations show that selective intervention outperforms passive bank exposure, always-on injection, advisor-only guidance, and general retrieval. As an early step toward open-weight memory policies, we train Qwen3.5-27B on SETA using SFT and GRPO, improving validation reward and achieving partial transfer to Terminal-Bench.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an auxiliary memory agent that monitors long-horizon trajectories, maintains a structured memory bank, and selectively reminds an action agent of relevant past information to prevent “behavioral state decay,” thereby improving task performance on long-context benchmarks.
AI.motivation: In long-horizon tasks, crucial decision-relevant information is dispersed across long trajectories and can be buried or pushed out of an LLM agent’s context window. This leads to “behavioral state decay,” where the agent forgets key facts, goals, prior attempts, or diagnoses, harming decision quality. Existing approaches that rely on passive retrieval or simply enlarging context windows are insufficient. The authors are motivated to design an active memory mechanism that can intervene at the right times to bring back crucial information for decision-making without changing the underlying action agent.
AI.method: The authors introduce a separate memory agent that runs in parallel with an unmodified action agent. This memory agent maintains a structured memory bank built from the recent trajectory (including task requirements, environment facts, subgoals, and attempts). At each step, it decides whether to remain silent or to inject a concise, memory-grounded reminder into the action agent’s context. The design is plug-and-play: it can be attached to different frontier action agents and integrated into existing agent harnesses. They evaluate it on Terminal-Bench 2.0 and τ^2-Bench and run ablations comparing selective intervention against alternatives such as passive exposure to the memory bank, always-on memory injection, advisor-only setups, and generic retrieval. They also explore training an open-weight memory policy by fine-tuning Qwen3.5-27B on the SETA dataset using supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and GRPO reinforcement-style optimization.
AI.result: On Terminal-Bench 2.0 and τ^2-Bench, the memory agent improves pass@1 performance for both weaker and stronger underlying action agents, yielding gains of +8.3 percentage points on Terminal-Bench and +6.8 percentage points on τ^2-Bench. Ablation studies demonstrate that the selective, policy-driven memory intervention strategy outperforms simpler baselines such as passive access to the memory bank, unconditional (always-on) memory injection, advisor-only guidance, and unguided general retrieval. For open-weight models, training Qwen3.5-27B on SETA using SFT and GRPO improves validation reward and shows partial transfer of the learned memory policy to Terminal-Bench tasks.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that “behavioral state decay” is a key failure mode in long-horizon LLM agents and that treating memory as an active intervention mechanism—implemented by a dedicated memory agent that selectively reminds the action agent—can meaningfully improve performance without modifying the core action policy. Their plug-and-play memory module provides consistent gains across benchmarks and model strengths, and selective intervention is more effective than naïve or passive memory use. Early experiments with open-weight training (Qwen3.5-27B on SETA) indicate that learnable, general-purpose memory policies are feasible and can transfer across tasks, pointing toward future work on more robust and general open-weight memory agents.

title: The Illusion of Equivalency: Statistical Characterization of Quantization Effects in LLMs
summary: Post-training quantization is widely used to deploy large language models in resource-constrained settings, yet its evaluation relies almost exclusively on accuracy and perplexity. We show that these metrics fail to capture behavioral changes induced by quantization. We introduce correctness agreement, a decision-level metric that measures overlap in correct predictions between a base model and its quantized variants, independent of absolute accuracy. Across multiple models and quantization schemes from 8-bit to 2-bit, we find that behavioral divergence emerges under moderate quantization even when task performance appears preserved. To explain this effect, we analyze quantization as a structural operator on attention weights and quantify layer-wise distortions using statistical and distributional measures. Our results reveal non-linear breakpoints at low bit-widths and show that query and key projections are consistently more sensitive than value and output projections. These findings expose an illusion of equivalence between base and quantized models and motivate behavioral evaluation beyond conventional performance metrics.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that standard metrics like accuracy and perplexity are insufficient to evaluate post-training quantization of LLMs, and introduces a new behavior-focused metric plus an analysis of how quantization structurally distorts attention layers.
AI.motivation: Post-training quantization is widely adopted to make large language models deployable on resource-limited hardware, but current evaluation practices mostly look at accuracy or perplexity, implicitly assuming that if those metrics are preserved then the quantized model behaves like the original. The authors suspect this assumption is false—that models might show different decision behavior even with similar overall performance—creating an “illusion of equivalence” that could be problematic for downstream applications and safety.
AI.method: They propose a new metric called correctness agreement, which measures the overlap in correct predictions between the base model and its quantized versions, decoupled from absolute accuracy. They evaluate multiple models under various post-training quantization schemes ranging from 8-bit to 2-bit, and compare behavior using this metric. To understand why behavior diverges, they interpret quantization as a structural operator acting on attention weights and perform a layer-wise analysis of distortions, using statistical and distributional metrics to quantify how query, key, value, and output projections are affected.
AI.result: They observe that even when quantized models maintain similar overall accuracy or perplexity, they can differ substantially from the base model in which specific instances they get correct—that is, correctness agreement drops under moderate quantization. Their structural analysis reveals non-linear breakpoints at low bit-widths, where distortions sharply increase, and shows that the query and key projections in attention layers are systematically more sensitive to quantization noise than value and output projections.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates that standard performance metrics can mask significant behavioral changes introduced by quantization, challenging the assumption that similar accuracy implies equivalent behavior. By highlighting the higher sensitivity of certain attention components and the existence of non-linear breakpoints at low precision, the paper argues for incorporating behavioral metrics like correctness agreement into quantization evaluation pipelines and for more careful design of quantization strategies that account for differential sensitivity across model components.

title: Workflow as Knowledge: Semantic Persistence for LLM-Mediated Workflows
summary:
Large language model (LLM) applications increasingly use explicit workflows for tool use, retrieval, branching, checkpointing, and human approval. Existing workflow systems already address many execution concerns. This paper proposes a Lisp-inspired but language-independent conceptual model: symbolic forms, object identity, and live-image thinking are used as explanatory lenses, not implementation commitments. In this model, workflow definitions, workflow instances, inference records, context snapshots, and dependency relations are represented as persistent knowledge objects in a shared knowledge substrate. Its central semantic distinction is between derive and infer: derive is deterministic computation over available state; infer is mediated LLM judgment under declared context and executor-controlled capability policy.
  The result is a preliminary conceptual account of semantic persistence: workflows do not merely produce knowledge and leave traces, but can themselves be represented as inspectable, resumable, and reviewable knowledge objects, while formal transition semantics remain future work.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a conceptual model for representing LLM-driven workflows and their artifacts as persistent knowledge objects, distinguishing deterministic computation (derive) from LLM-mediated judgment (infer).
AI.motivation: LLM applications increasingly rely on complex workflows involving tools, retrieval, branching, and human oversight, but existing workflow systems lack a clear semantic account of how workflows, their executions, and their knowledge products persist, interrelate, and remain inspectable over time.
AI.method: The authors introduce a language-independent, Lisp-inspired conceptual framework based on symbolic forms, object identity, and live-image thinking. Within this framework, they model workflow definitions, workflow instances, inference records, context snapshots, and dependencies as persistent knowledge objects in a shared knowledge substrate, and define a core semantic distinction between deterministic derivations (derive) and LLM-mediated inferences (infer) governed by explicit context and capability policies.
AI.result: They obtain a preliminary semantic model in which workflows, their runs, and intermediate states are themselves first-class, inspectable, resumable knowledge objects, enabling semantic persistence rather than transient execution traces, though without yet formalizing full transition semantics.
AI.conclusion: Representing workflows and all related artifacts as persistent knowledge objects, and distinguishing derive from infer, provides a conceptual foundation for more transparent, resumable, and reviewable LLM application workflows, while leaving rigorous formal semantics and implementations to future work.

title: AUTOPILOT VQA: Benchmarking Vision-Language Models for Incident-Centric Dashcam Understanding
summary: Recent advances in Vision-Language Models, Large Language Models, and Multimodal Large Language Models have improved autonomous driving tasks such as scene understanding, decision making, trajectory prediction, and visual question answering. However, evaluating whether these models can reliably reason about safety-critical incidents remains challenging. To address this gap, we present AUTOPILOT-VQA, an incident-centric visual question answering benchmark for dashcam video understanding. The dataset evaluates different systems through structured questions designed around real-world driving incidents and near-incidents. The benchmark covers diverse safety-relevant categories, including weather and lighting conditions, traffic environment, road layout, road surface state, signage, involved entities, accident occurrence, impact location, and avoidability-related reasoning. By requiring models to answer grounded questions about both contextual scene properties and event-level incident details, AUTOPILOT-VQA moves beyond object recognition toward temporally grounded, safety-aware reasoning. The dataset is released as part of the AUTOPILOT CVPR 2026 competition and provides a standardized benchmark for assessing the reliability of autonomous driving systems in different scenarios. Our benchmark support developments for more interpretable, robust, and safety-conscious vision-language systems for real-world autonomous driving.

AI.tldr: AUTOPILOT-VQA introduces an incident-centric dashcam-based visual question answering benchmark to evaluate vision-language and multimodal language models on safety-critical reasoning for autonomous driving.
AI.motivation: While recent vision-language and multimodal language models perform well on generic tasks, it is unclear whether they can reliably reason about safety-critical incidents in autonomous driving scenarios. Existing benchmarks largely emphasize perception and object recognition rather than structured, temporally grounded safety reasoning about real-world incidents and near-incidents.
AI.method: The authors construct AUTOPILOT-VQA, a visual question answering dataset built from real dashcam videos of driving incidents and near-incidents. They design structured question sets around each event, targeting diverse safety-relevant aspects such as environmental conditions, traffic context, road layout and surface, signage, actors involved, whether and how an accident occurs, where impact happens, and whether it was avoidable. The benchmark is released within the AUTOPILOT CVPR 2026 competition to enable standardized evaluation of different vision-language and autonomous driving systems.
AI.result: AUTOPILOT-VQA provides a curated, incident-centric VQA benchmark that systematically probes models’ understanding of both scene context and fine-grained incident dynamics. It differentiates models that can merely recognize objects from those that can perform temporally grounded, safety-aware reasoning in diverse driving scenarios.
AI.conclusion: By focusing on incident-centric, safety-relevant questions anchored in real dashcam footage, AUTOPILOT-VQA offers a standardized way to assess and compare the reliability, interpretability, and robustness of vision-language systems for autonomous driving. This benchmark is intended to drive progress toward more safety-conscious and trustworthy autonomous driving models, and underpins the AUTOPILOT CVPR 2026 competition.

title: Using AI-based Learning Assistants in Higher Education: A Large-Scale Descriptive Analysis
summary: In this study, we present a large-scale descriptive analysis of the use of an AI-based learning assistant (Syntea) in higher education. Based on objective log data from 77,543 students enrolled in distance studies, we examine usage patterns across gender, age group, study cluster, degree, and study mode. To date, existing research on educational chatbots has largely relied on comparatively small samples and self-reported survey data, while large-scale evidence on actual usage behavior remains limited. Our findings show that Syntea is already embedded in the study routines of many learners, but that usage differs across demographic and structural contexts. By identifying these patterns, our study provides an empirical basis for the further development of AI-based learning support and contributes a large-scale analysis of educational chatbot usage in higher education.

AI.tldr: Large-scale log-data study of how 77k+ distance students use an AI learning assistant (Syntea), describing usage patterns across demographic and study characteristics.
AI.motivation: Most prior educational chatbot research uses small samples and self-reported surveys, so we lack large-scale, objective evidence of how students actually use AI learning assistants in real higher-education settings. This limits our ability to design and implement effective AI-based learning support.
AI.method: Use objective log data from 77,543 higher-education distance students interacting with the Syntea AI learning assistant. Conduct descriptive, large-scale analysis of usage patterns, disaggregated by gender, age group, study cluster, degree type, and study mode, to identify differences in adoption and integration into study routines.
AI.result: Syntea is already integrated into the study routines of many learners, indicating substantial adoption of the AI assistant. However, usage intensity and patterns vary systematically across demographic groups and structural study characteristics such as study cluster, degree, and mode of study.
AI.conclusion: AI learning assistants like Syntea are becoming an established part of higher-education study practices, but their use is not uniform across student groups. Mapping these differences offers an empirical foundation for tailoring and improving AI-based learning support and provides one of the first large-scale, log-data-based analyses of educational chatbot usage in higher education.

title: Ideas Have Genomes: Benchmarking Scientific Lineage Reasoning and Lineage-Grounded Idea Generation
summary: Scientific ideas rarely start from a blank page. They inherit mechanisms, repair known limitations, and recombine pieces of earlier work, much like biological genomes. Current benchmarks still say little about whether AI systems can follow this inheritance structure. We present IdeaGene-Bench (IG-Bench), a benchmark for scientific lineage reasoning and lineage-grounded idea generation. IG-Bench is organized around the IdeaGene framework: each paper or proposal is represented as a set of minimal, typed, evidence-grounded Idea Genome objects, and a GenomeDiff aligns these objects to record inheritance, mutation, loss, external import, and novel insertion under six operational evolutionary dynamics. The benchmark contains 1,961 golden lineage traces, 1,085 curated Idea Genome objects, and 920 pairwise GenomeDiff records across 10 scientific domains. It supports two evaluations. IG-Exam (42 task types, 1,029 instances) tests closed-form lineage reasoning across Idea Genome abstraction, inheritance tracing, evolutionary reasoning, and lineage verification. IG-Arena evaluates generation with a lineage-conditioned Population-Evolution Score(PES), asking whether a proposal can be inserted as a coherent descendant of a given lineage population: it should inherit the right Idea Genome objects, vary meaningfully from nearby work, and offer selection value for future research. Experiments on 14 LLM-based scientists expose a compositional bottleneck. The strongest system reaches only 27.3% exact accuracy on lineage reasoning, and structured lineage context reshuffles system rankings rather than helping every participant uniformly.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces IG-Bench, a benchmark to evaluate how well AI systems understand and generate scientific ideas within explicit lineages of prior work.
AI.motivation: Existing AI benchmarks do not capture whether models can track how scientific ideas inherit, modify, and recombine prior work, despite this being central to real scientific progress. The authors want a way to test if AI can reason about and generate research ideas that are faithful to such lineages.
AI.method: They build IdeaGene-Bench based on the IdeaGene framework, representing each paper/proposal as a set of minimal, typed, evidence-grounded “Idea Genome” objects. They define GenomeDiff to align these objects across works and categorize inheritance events into six evolutionary dynamics (inheritance, mutation, loss, external import, novel insertion, etc.). The benchmark spans 10 domains with 1,961 annotated lineage traces, 1,085 Idea Genomes, and 920 GenomeDiff records. Two evaluations are defined: IG-Exam, a suite of 42 closed-form task types testing various aspects of lineage reasoning; and IG-Arena, a generative setting where models must propose new ideas that fit coherently into a given lineage, scored by a lineage-conditioned Population-Evolution Score (PES). They evaluate 14 LLM-based “scientist” systems on these tasks.
AI.result: On IG-Exam, even the best-performing LLM-based scientist reaches only 27.3% exact accuracy on lineage reasoning tasks. Providing structured lineage context does not uniformly improve all systems and instead reshuffles their relative rankings. These findings suggest a strong compositional reasoning bottleneck in current models when handling scientific lineages.
AI.conclusion: IG-Bench exposes that current LLMs struggle to reason about and generate scientific ideas in a way that respects explicit lineages of prior work. The benchmark, with its Idea Genome abstraction, GenomeDiff dynamics, and dual reasoning/generation evaluations, provides a structured way to measure and drive progress on lineage-aware scientific AI systems.
